A 4th-order Butterworth low-pass filter (cutoff $\approx 10\,Hz$) is used for cursor movement signals to remove hand tremor noise. 3. **Processing Layer:** A microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4) runs the HMI state machine and event-handling code. 4. **Output Transduction Layer:** The processed signal drives an actuator – in this case, a TFT-LCD panel that updates the on-screen cursor position. 5. **Ergonomic Layer:** Encompasses the mechanical housing, button placement, weight, and thermal design. **Real-World Example:** A **smartwatch touchscreen with haptic feedback**: - Input: Capacitive touch sensor (sensing layer) - Conditioning: On-chip touch controller with noise-filtering firmware - Processing: Apple S-series SiP / Qualcomm Snapdragon Wear - Output: AMOLED display + LRA haptic motor - Ergonomic: 40–45 mm case, 10–15 g weight, 5 ATM water resistance *(1 mark per layer identification + 1 mark for correct real-world example + 1 mark for the block diagram summary)* ### Part (b) – Fitts's Law Derivation and Calculation (7 marks) **Step 1 – Write the ID formula:** $$ ID = \log_2 \!\left( \frac{D}{W} + 1 \right) $$ **Step 2 – Substitute given values** ($D = 60\,mm$, $W = 12\,mm$): $$ ID = \log_2 \!\left( \frac{60}{12} + 1 \right) = \log_2 \!\left( 5 + 1 \right) = \log_2(6) $$ **Step 3 – Evaluate the logarithm:** $$ \log_2(6) = \frac{\log_{10}(6)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{0.7782}{0.3010} \approx 2.585\,bits $$ **Step 4 – Apply Fitts's Law for MT:** $$ MT = a + b \cdot ID = 0.05 + 0.10 \times 2.585 $$ **Step 5 – Compute MT:** $$ MT = 0.05 + 0.2585 = 0.3085\,s \approx 0.31\,s $$ **Design Implication:** A 12 mm icon at 60 mm distance requires **$\approx 310$ ms** of thumb-acquisition time. This is **at the upper limit** of acceptable touch latency (300–500 ms feels instantaneous). For a smaller icon (e.g., 8 mm), $ID$ becomes $\log_2(8.5) \approx 3.09$ bits and $MT \approx 0.36\,s$, which feels sluggish. Therefore, **touchscreen icons should be $\geq 9$–12 mm** for one-handed thumb use, per the KTU-referenced Apple HIG and Material Design guidelines. *(1 mark formula + 1 mark substitution + 1 mark log evaluation + 1 mark MT computation + 1 mark interpretation + 2 marks design comment)* --- ### `Question B (14 Marks)` – `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative]` **(a)** Compare the following **input hardware technologies** used in modern HMI systems, listing working principle, typical latency, and one suitable application for each: **(i) Resistive Touchscreen, (ii) Capacitive Touchscreen, (iii) Infrared Touch Frame, (iv) Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) Touch.** **(7 marks)** **(b)** An **8-bit ADC** is used to digitize a force-sensitive resistor (FSR) signal in a haptic glove. The reference voltage is $V_{ref} = 3.3\,V$. Compute: (i) the LSB voltage, (ii) the maximum quantization error, (iii) the SQNR in dB. If the FSR produces a voltage of $1.85\,V$, what is the digital output code? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B ### Part (a) – Input Hardware Comparison (7 marks) | # | Technology | Working Principle | Typical Latency | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | (i) | **Resistive Touchscreen** | Two conductive ITO layers separated by spacers; pressure causes contact, changing resistance | $\approx 10\text{–}15\,ms$ | Industrial HMIs, medical devices (can be used with gloves) | | (ii) | **Capacitive Touchscreen** | Measures change in capacitance caused by the conductive property of the human finger | $\approx 5\text{–}10\,ms$ | Smartphones, tablets, kiosks | | (iii) | **Infrared Touch Frame** | Matrix of IR LEDs and photodetectors around the bezel; touch is detected by light interruption | $\approx 8\text{–}12\,ms$ | Large interactive displays, classrooms | | (iv) | **SAW Touch** | Uses ultrasonic waves on glass surface; touch absorbs wave energy, detected by transducers | $\approx 10\text{–}20\,ms$ | ATMs, public kiosks (high optical clarity) | *(1.5 marks per row + 1 mark overall comparison summary)* ### Part (b) – ADC Computation (7 marks) **Given:** $n = 8$ bits, $V_{ref} = 3.3\,V$, $V_{in} = 1.85\,V$. **(i) LSB Voltage:** $$ V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \frac{3.3}{256} = 0.01289\,V \approx 12.89\,mV $$ **(ii) Maximum Quantization Error:** $$ e_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm \frac{12.89}{2} = \pm 6.45\,mV $$ **(iii) Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR):** $$ SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92\,dB $$ **Digital Output Code for $V_{in} = 1.85\,V$:** $$ \text{Code} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1.85}{0.01289} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 143.52 \rfloor = 143_{10} $$ Converting to binary: $$ 143_{10} = 128 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 10001111_2 $$ > **Design Note:** The 8-bit resolution gives a digital step of $\approx 12.9\,mV$, which is adequate for **gross force feedback** in a haptic glove but **insufficient for fine pressure mapping** (which would require 12-bit or 16-bit ADC). *(1 mark each for parts i, ii, iii; 2 marks for part iv digital code; 1 mark for design interpretation)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Module 1 – Hardware):** > 1. **Confusing Sensor with Transducer:** A sensor is a *type* of transducer; not all transducers are sensors. Use precise language. > 2. **Mixing up Resolution and Accuracy:** ADC resolution ($\Delta = V_{FS}/2^n$) is a *quantization* property; accuracy involves gain error, offset error, and INL/DNL. > 3. **Unit Mismatch in Fitts's Law:** Movement time must be in **seconds**, ID in **bits**, and $a$, $b$ must use **consistent time units**. The examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for unit errors. > 4. **Omitting the +1 in Fitts's ID:** The expression is $\log_2(D/W + 1)$, **not** $\log_2(D/W)$. Forgetting the $+1$ produces wrong results when $D = W$. > 5. **Naming a single output device for HMI:** Examiners expect students to discuss *visual*, *auditory*, and *haptic* output channels together for full marks in a 14-mark question. > 6. **Neglecting Ergonomics:** Module 1 of KTU 2024 explicitly tests ergonomic design (anthropometry, ISO 9241). A purely technical answer without ergonomic justification loses 2–3 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist – Hardware (Module 1)** - [ ] HMI Hardware = **Input Transducers + Processing + Output Actuators + Ergonomic Form Factor**. - [ ] The five functional layers are: **Sensing → Conditioning → Processing → Output Transduction → Ergonomics**. - [ ] **Fitts's Law** ($MT = a + b \log_2(D/W + 1)$) governs the speed of target acquisition → use it to size buttons/touch targets. - [ ] **ADC Resolution** $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^n$; **SQNR** $\approx 6.02n + 1.76\,dB$. - [ ] **Display Technologies:** LCD, OLED, MicroLED, e-Ink, DLP, Holographic – know working principle + HMI use case. - [ ] **Haptic Hardware:** ERM, LRA, Force feedback, Electrotactile, Ultrasonic, Thermal. - [ ] **Bio-signal Hardware:** EEG, EMG, EOG, GSR, PPG – used in BCIs, wearable HMI, accessibility. - [ ] **Touch Hardware:** Resistive, Capacitive, IR Frame, SAW, In-Cell, Optical In-Display. - [ ] **Ergonomic Standards:** ISO 9241 (Ergonomics of Human-System Interaction), Apple HIG 44 pt minimum, Material Design 48 dp. - [ ] **Flicker:** Display refresh must exceed Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF) given by Ferry-Porter law. - [ ] **Audio:** Human hearing range **20 Hz – 20 kHz**; loudness in dB SPL referenced to **$20\,\mu Pa$**. - [ ] Always end any HMI hardware answer with a **design implication** or **trade-off** comment – KTU examiners reward reflective design thinking. - [ ] **Be multimodal in thought** – the best HMI hardware solutions use **multiple input and output channels simultaneously** (e.g., touch + voice + haptic).

topics.title: Software and Operating Environments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT614 - INTRODUCTION TO HUMAN MACHINE INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Software and Operating Environments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Software and Operating Environments** in Human–Machine Interaction (HMI) refers to the complete stack of system-level and application-level software that mediates between the **user**, the **input/output hardware**, and the **computational resources** of a machine. It encompasses the operating system (OS), windowing/graphical subsystem, device drivers, middleware, runtime environments, and application software that together define *how* the user perceives, controls, and experiences the machine.

In the KTU 2024 OECPT614 syllabus, Module 1 situates this topic under the broader umbrella of *“The Human–Machine Interface”*, where software and operating environments form the **logical/functional layer** that sits between the **physical hardware** and the **human user**.

The three pillars that govern an HMI software environment are:

1. **The Operating System (OS)** – resource manager, process scheduler, memory manager.
2. **The Windowing / Graphical Environment** – GUI shell (X Window, Wayland, Win32, Cocoa, Android Window Manager).
3. **The Application & Runtime Layer** – programs, toolkits (Qt, GTK, Flutter, React Native, SwiftUI), and interpreters (JVM, .NET CLR, WebAssembly runtime).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Restaurant Kitchen"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Think of a software environment as a **professional restaurant kitchen**.
> - The **Operating System** is the *head chef and kitchen manager* — decides what dish (process) gets cooked on which stove (CPU), tracks ingredients in the fridge (memory), and ensures the orders (I/O requests) leave on time.
> - The **Windowing System / GUI Shell** is the *dining hall and waiters* — they present the food on plates (windows, icons, menus) so the customer (user) can see, choose, and consume.
> - The **Application Software** is the *recipe book and the cooks* — actual content the user came for.
> - The **Device Drivers** are the *specialized kitchen equipment* (ovens, grills) that translate the chef’s commands into physical heat, light, or motion.

Just as a customer never interacts with raw ingredients but only with the plated meal, a user typically never interacts with raw hardware bits but only with **icons, windows, gestures, and voice prompts** rendered by the software environment.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in HMI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The choice of software and operating environment **dictates the affordances, feedback channels, latency profile, and accessibility** of any HMI. A real-time OS (RTOS) in an automobile airbag system offers deterministic **sub-10 ms** response. A mobile Android environment offers touch-first, permission-grained interaction. Each environment shapes *how* a human can possibly interact with the machine.

## 1.4 Categories of Software Environments (KTU Board-Relevant Classification)

| Category | Examples | HMI Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Desktop OS** | Windows 10/11, macOS, Linux (Ubuntu, Fedora) | WIMP (Windows, Icons, Menu, Pointer), keyboard + mouse, multi-tasking |
| **Mobile OS** | Android, iOS, HarmonyOS | Touch-first, gesture-driven, sandboxed apps |
| **Embedded / RTOS** | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS | Deterministic, low-latency, headless or minimal UI |
| **Web Environment** | Browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari) running HTML5/CSS/JS | Platform-independent, declarative UI |
| **Cross-platform Runtime** | JVM, .NET CLR, Flutter, Electron, React Native | Write-once-run-anywhere abstraction |
| **Automotive / Industrial HMI** | AUTOSAR, CODESYS, Siemens TIA Portal | Hard real-time, safety-critical UI |
| **VR/AR Environments** | Unity, Unreal Engine, visionOS | 3D spatial interaction, eye/hand tracking |
| **Voice/AI Environments** | Alexa Skills, Google Assistant, GPT Agents | Conversational, intent-based interaction |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition to Memorise:** A *software environment* is the **layered collection of OS services, GUI subsystems, runtimes, and applications** that together provide the platform on which human–machine dialogue occurs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Layered Architecture of an HMI Software Environment

Every HMI software environment — whether a smartwatch, a car infotainment system, or an industrial control panel — can be decomposed into **six logical layers**. Understanding this stack is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in the KTU ESE.

### Layer 1 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)
- Translates electrical signals into logical operations.
- Contains **device drivers** (display, touchscreen controller, haptic motor, accelerometer).
- Provides a *uniform API* so upper layers do not need to know the silicon vendor.

### Layer 2 — Kernel
- **Process / Thread Scheduler** — decides which task runs on the CPU and for how long.
- **Memory Manager** — virtual memory, paging, protection.
- **File System** — VFS abstraction over FAT, ext4, NTFS, APFS.
- **Interrupt Handler** — preemptive, priority-based.
- For HMI, the kernel also hosts the **input subsystem** (Linux `evdev`, Windows HID class, Android `InputManagerService`).

### Layer 3 — System Services / Daemons
- Background services: power manager, network manager, audio server (PulseAudio, CoreAudio), sensor hub.
- On Android: `system_server`, `SurfaceFlinger`, `AudioFlinger`.
- On Windows: `csrss.exe`, `winlogon.exe`, `dwm.exe`.

### Layer 4 — Windowing / Graphics Subsystem
- Manages **windows, viewports, compositing, double-buffering**.
- Examples: **X11 / Wayland (Linux)**, **Win32 / DWM (Windows)**, **Quartz / Core Animation (macOS)**, **SurfaceFlinger (Android)**, **QuartzCore (iOS)**.
- Enforces **Z-ordering, focus, clipping, event routing**.

### Layer 5 — UI Toolkit / Framework
- Provides widgets: buttons, sliders, text fields, lists.
- Cross-platform: **Qt, GTK, Flutter, React Native, SwiftUI, Jetpack Compose**.
- Native: **WinForms, Cocoa, UIKit, JavaFX**.
- Defines the **look-and-feel** (HIG — Human Interface Guidelines).

### Layer 6 — Application / User-Facing Logic
- The end-user program — calculator, browser, SCADA dashboard, IVI (In-Vehicle Infotainment) app.
- Talks only to the toolkit; never to the kernel directly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the layers matter for HMI:** Each layer introduces **latency, abstraction cost, and design constraints**. A KTU examiner loves to ask: *"Why does an embedded medical device prefer an RTOS with no windowing layer instead of Android?"* The answer is **determinism, certification, and footprint**.

## 2.2 Operating Environment Classification by Interaction Paradigm

| Paradigm | OS Family | Input Modality | Output Modality | Typical Latency Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| WIMP (Desktop) | Windows / macOS / Linux | Mouse, keyboard, trackpad | Pixel display, audio | **< 100 ms** perceived response |
| Touch / Gesture | Android, iOS | Finger, stylus, multi-touch | Capacitive display, haptic | **< 50 ms** touch-to-paint |
| Voice / Conversational | Alexa OS, Google Assistant | Microphone | Speaker, display | **< 300 ms** TTS reply start |
| Spatial / XR | visionOS, Android XR | Gaze, hand, controller | Stereo displays, spatial audio | **< 11 ms** motion-to-photon (90 Hz) |
| Real-Time Industrial | VxWorks, QNX | Hard keys, touch, PLC | Bezel buttons, rugged display | **< 1 ms** hard real-time |

## 2.3 Process Scheduling — The HMI Heartbeat

In any multi-tasking OS, the scheduler decides *which* thread (e.g., the touch handler vs. the animation renderer vs. the music player) gets the CPU. For an HMI, the **UI thread** and the **input thread** must be **elevated in priority**, otherwise a background download will cause the dreaded *“jank”* (dropped frame).

Two key HMI-relevant metrics:

$$
\text{CPU Utilization (U)} = \frac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Throughput (X)} = \frac{\text{Number of tasks completed}}{\text{Unit time}}
$$

$$
\text{Turnaround Time (TAT)} = T_{\text{completion}} - T_{\text{arrival}}
$$

$$
\text{Waiting Time (WT)} = TAT - T_{\text{burst time}}
$$

$$
\text{Response Time (RT)} = T_{\text{first-CPU}} - T_{\text{arrival}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For HMI specifically**, the most important metric is **Response Time** of the input thread — the time between the user’s physical action (touch down) and the first visible/pixel response. KTU examiners will quote a value like *“Google’s Material Design recommends < 100 ms for tap feedback”* and ask you to justify scheduler choice.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol | Unit | Where Used in HMI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Utilization | $U = \frac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}}$ | % | Sizing the processor for UI workload |
| 2 | Throughput | $X = \frac{N_{tasks}}{t}$ | tasks/s | Frames per second (FPS) of a UI |
| 3 | Turnaround Time | $TAT = T_c - T_a$ | s | Background task timing |
| 4 | Waiting Time | $WT = TAT - T_{burst}$ | s | Queue analysis |
| 5 | Response Time | $RT = T_{firstCPU} - T_a$ | s | **Input lag** measurement |
| 6 | Frame Rate | $FPS = \frac{1}{T_{frame}}$ | Hz | Display refresh, e.g. 60 Hz, 90 Hz, 120 Hz |
| 7 | Motion-to-Photon Latency | $L_{m2p}$ | ms | VR/AR critical metric (target **< 20 ms**) |
| 8 | Memory Footprint | $M_{foot} = M_{code} + M_{data} + M_{stack} + M_{heap}$ | KB / MB | Embedded HMI sizing |
| 9 | Little’s Law | $L = \lambda \cdot W$ | items | Queue length of input events |
| 10 | Deadline Miss Ratio | $DMR = \frac{N_{missed}}{N_{total}}$ | fraction | RTOS evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Board Note:** Never write the absolute value `|x|` with a vertical pipe inside a markdown table — it breaks the rendering. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` in LaTeX contexts.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (QNX, AUTOSAR):** The instrument cluster and ADAS display must refresh in **< 10 ms**; an RTOS guarantees this.
- **Medical (FDA Class III):** Infusion pumps run on deterministic software environments (e.g., VxWorks) so that alarm latencies are bounded.
- **Avionics (DO-178C):** Cockpit displays use partitioned, certified OS environments — no third-party apps.
- **Smart Home (Linux, Android Things):** Touch panels for smart appliances trade hard real-time for rich GUI toolkits.
- **Wearables (WatchOS, WearOS):** Aggressive power management + low-power always-on display environment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Frame-Rate & Motion-to-Photon Latency Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most-likely 14-mark derivation** KTU will ask under the HMI operating environments topic, especially for VR/AR or gaming modules.

### Problem Statement
A VR head-mounted display has a refresh rate of **90 Hz**. Each frame must be rendered and presented on the OLED panel. The GPU takes **6 ms** to render one frame, the compositor takes **2 ms**, and the OLED panel takes **1 ms** to physically update its pixels. Compute:
1. The **target frame time** in milliseconds.
2. The **motion-to-photon latency budget** per frame.
3. Whether the system is **safe** under the **20 ms VR comfort threshold**.

### Step 1 — Convert Refresh Rate to Frame Time

The frame time is the reciprocal of the refresh rate.

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{90 \text{ Hz}}
$$

$$
T_{frame} = 0.01111... \text{ seconds}
$$

Convert to milliseconds (multiply by 1000):

$$
T_{frame} = 0.01111... \times 1000 = 11.11 \text{ ms (rounded to 2 dp)}
$$

**Logic:** A 90 Hz display can show a new image every 11.11 ms. Any computation contributing to that frame must complete within this budget.

### Step 2 — Sum the Pipeline Stages

Add the GPU render time, compositor time, and panel write time.

$$
T_{pipeline} = T_{GPU} + T_{compositor} + T_{panel}
$$

$$
T_{pipeline} = 6 + 2 + 1 = 9 \text{ ms}
$$

**Logic:** Of the 11.11 ms budget, 9 ms is consumed by active work.

### Step 3 — Compute the Headroom (Slack)

$$
T_{slack} = T_{frame} - T_{pipeline}
$$

$$
T_{slack} = 11.11 - 9 = 2.11 \text{ ms}
$$

**Logic:** This slack is the safety margin for OS scheduling jitter, input sampling, and compositor queueing.

### Step 4 — Compute Motion-to-Photon Latency (Upper Bound)

The motion-to-photon latency is the **worst-case** time from a user head movement to the corresponding pixel update.

$$
L_{m2p} = T_{sensor} + T_{pipeline} + T_{panel-scan}
$$

Assume inertial measurement unit (IMU) sensor sampling latency = **4 ms** and panel line scan = **0.5 ms**.

$$
L_{m2p} = 4 + 9 + 0.5
$$

$$
L_{m2p} = 13.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Logic:** The 13.5 ms lies comfortably below the **20 ms VR motion-sickness threshold**.

### Step 5 — Verdict

$$
L_{m2p} = 13.5 \text{ ms} < 20 \text{ ms} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Safe for VR comfort}
$$

If we had chosen a 60 Hz display, the frame time would be 16.67 ms, the slack would shrink to 6.67 ms, and any OS jitter > 6 ms would cause a dropped frame and **simulator sickness**.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — CPU Utilization under HMI Workload

### Problem Statement
A smartphone SoC spends **2.1 s** of every **3.0 s** window actively running UI, sensor fusion, and audio mixing tasks. Compute the **CPU utilization** and determine whether the device will suffer thermal throttling under the **80 %** safe threshold.

### Step 1 — Apply the Utilization Formula

$$
U = \frac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
U = \frac{2.1}{3.0} \times 100\%
$$

$$
U = 0.7 \times 100\% = 70\%
$$

### Step 2 — Compare Against Threshold

$$
U = 70\% < 80\% \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Safe (no throttling)}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret in HMI Terms

A sustained 70 % utilization means the **UI thread is preempted 30 % of the time**. If the input event handler sits in that 30 % slice, the user perceives a **laggy** interface. Hence mobile OS schedulers (e.g., Android’s `SCHED_FIFO` for the input thread) elevate UI threads to **real-time priority** to avoid being descheduled.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Little’s Law for Input-Event Queue

### Problem Statement
A touch controller generates touch events at an average rate of **$\lambda = 120$ events/s**. The UI dispatcher processes each event in an average service time of **4 ms**. Using **Little’s Law**, compute the **average number of events in the queue** at any instant.

### Step 1 — Identify Parameters

$$
\lambda = 120 \text{ events/s}
$$

$$
W = 4 \text{ ms} = 0.004 \text{ s}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Little’s Law

$$
L = \lambda \cdot W
$$

$$
L = 120 \times 0.004
$$

$$
L = 0.48 \text{ events}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret

On average, **less than one** event is in the queue, meaning the dispatcher is keeping up in real time. If the service time rose to **20 ms** (e.g., a complex animation), then:

$$
L = 120 \times 0.020 = 2.4 \text{ events}
$$

This would correspond to perceptible **input lag** — the user feels their taps are being “queued”.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — OS Choice vs HMI Domain

| HMI Domain | Preferred OS / Environment | Critical Metric | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smartphone | Android, iOS | $L_{touch} < 50$ ms | Rich UI toolkits, multitouch |
| Smartwatch | WatchOS, WearOS | Battery life > 24 h | Aggressive suspend states |
| Automotive Cluster | QNX, AUTOSAR | $L_{render} < 10$ ms | Functional safety (ISO 26262) |
| Avionics Cockpit | VxWorks 653, INTEGRITY | Deadline miss = 0 | DO-178C certification |
| Medical Infusion Pump | FreeRTOS, ThreadX | Alarm latency < 1 s | FDA IEC 62304 |
| Industrial SCADA | Windows IoT, Linux RT | 99.99 % uptime | Long support cycles |
| VR/AR Headset | Android XR, visionOS | $L_{m2p} < 20$ ms | Motion-sickness avoidance |
| Smart Speaker | Linux (Buildroot) + Alexa SDK | Wake-word < 500 ms | Always-listening low power |
| ATM / Kiosk | Windows Embedded, Android Kiosk | Touch accuracy + security | Locked-down environment |
| Robotic Surgery | RTOS + custom QNX | $L_{haptic} < 5$ ms | Surgeon tactile fidelity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Architecture of an HMI Software Environment

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block renders the complete **6-layer software stack** of a typical HMI platform. It is the single most-drawn diagram in the KTU 2024 OECPT614 Module 1 viva and ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 6 - Application and User Facing Logic]
    L2[Layer 5 - UI Toolkit and Framework]
    L3[Layer 4 - Windowing and Graphics Subsystem]
    L4[Layer 3 - System Services and Daemons]
    L5[Layer 2 - Kernel and Scheduler]
    L6[Layer 1 - Hardware Abstraction Layer HAL]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6

    L2 -. example .-> E1[Examples: Qt, Flutter, SwiftUI, Jetpack Compose]
    L3 -. example .-> E2[Examples: X11, Wayland, Win32 DWM, SurfaceFlinger, Quartz]
    L4 -. example .-> E3[Examples: power manager, audio server, input hub]
    L5 -. example .-> E4[Examples: Linux kernel, NT kernel, XNU, RTOS scheduler]
    L6 -. example .-> E5[Examples: Display driver, Touch driver, Sensor HAL]

    U([Human User]) -->|Touch Voice Gaze Gesture| L1
    L1 -->|Visual Audio Haptic Feedback| U
```

## 4.2 Event Flow Through the HMI Stack — Touch-to-Pixel Pipeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as Human User
    participant DRV as Touch Driver HAL
    participant KRN as Kernel Input Subsystem
    participant SVC as System Service InputManager
    participant WIN as Windowing Subsystem
    participant UI as UI Toolkit
    participant APP as Application Logic
    participant DSP as Display Panel

    U->>DRV: Physical finger touch (capacitance change)
    DRV->>KRN: Raw interrupt + coordinates (x, y)
    KRN->>SVC: Normalised event (MotionEvent)
    SVC->>WIN: Route to focused window
    WIN->>UI: Dispatch to widget tree
    UI->>APP: onClick handler fires
    APP->>UI: requestRedraw()
    UI->>WIN: invalidate region
    WIN->>DSP: Compositor pushes new frame
    DSP-->>U: Updated pixel feedback (under 50 ms)
```

## 4.3 OS Classification Tree for HMI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OS[Operating Environments for HMI]
    OS --> GPOS[General Purpose OS]
    OS --> RTOS[Real Time OS]
    OS --> EMB[Embedded OS]

    GPOS --> G1[Windows]
    GPOS --> G2[macOS]
    GPOS --> G3[Linux Desktop]

    RTOS --> R1[Hard RTOS - VxWorks QNX]
    RTOS --> R2[Soft RTOS - FreeRTOS RTEMS]

    EMB --> E1[Android Things]
    EMB --> E2[Embedded Linux Yocto Buildroot]
    EMB --> E3[Tizen LiteOS]
```

## 4.4 HMI Domain to OS Mapping (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ConsumerHMI[Consumer HMI]
        C1[Smartphone] --> CO1[Android iOS]
        C2[Smart TV] --> CO2[Android TV tvOS Tizen]
        C3[Wearable] --> CO3[WearOS watchOS]
    end

    subgraph SafetyHMI[Safety Critical HMI]
        S1[Automotive Cluster] --> SO1[QNX AUTOSAR]
        S2[Avionics] --> SO2[VxWorks 653 INTEGRITY]
        S3[Medical Device] --> SO3[FreeRTOS ThreadX]
    end

    subgraph IndustrialHMI[Industrial HMI]
        I1[SCADA HMI] --> IO1[Windows IoT Linux RT]
        I2[Robotic Surgery] --> IO2[QNX plus custom RTOS]
    end

    subgraph EmergingHMI[Emerging HMI]
        EM1[VR AR Headset] --> EMO1[Android XR visionOS]
        EM2[Voice Assistant] --> EMO2[Linux plus Alexa SDK]
        EM3[Brain Computer Interface] --> EMO3[Custom RTOS plus DSP]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** When drawing any of the above in the answer sheet, **label every layer and arrow clearly**. Examiners award the diagram mark (1–2 marks) for **clarity and completeness**, not artistic skill. A clean box-and-arrow diagram of the 6-layer stack with two example labels is enough for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define the term "software environment" in the context of Human–Machine Interaction. List any four components of a typical software environment.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
A **software environment** in HMI is the layered collection of operating system services, graphical/windowing subsystems, runtime platforms, and application software that together provide the medium through which a user interacts with a machine.

**Four components (1 mark each, any four):**
1. **Operating System kernel** — manages CPU, memory, and I/O.
2. **Windowing/Graphics subsystem** — composes and displays windows (e.g., SurfaceFlinger, X11, Win32 DWM).
3. **UI Toolkit/Framework** — provides widgets and interaction primitives (e.g., Qt, Flutter, SwiftUI).
4. **Device Drivers / HAL** — translates hardware events into OS-readable signals.
5. **Runtime / Middleware** — JVM, .NET CLR, WebAssembly engine.
6. **Application Software** — the end-user program.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Differentiate between a General-Purpose Operating System (GPOS) and a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) in the context of HMI. Give one example of each.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | GPOS | RTOS |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Maximise average throughput | Meet strict deadlines |
| **Scheduling** | Fair-share, time-sliced | Priority-preemptive, deterministic |
| **Latency** | Variable, best-effort | Bounded, predictable |
| **HMI Use Case** | Smartphone, laptop, kiosk | Airbag controller, infusion pump, industrial robot |
| **Example** | Android, Windows, Linux | VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS |

*(1 mark for the definition of each, 0.5 mark for example, 0.5 mark for differentiating characteristic — adjust per KTU key.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the **layered architecture of a typical HMI software environment**. Explain the role of the kernel, windowing system, and UI toolkit. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **Android, iOS, and QNX** as operating environments for HMI. Discuss their suitability for **(i)** a consumer smartphone, **(ii)** an automotive instrument cluster, and **(iii)** a medical ventilator. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Layered Architecture (7 marks)**

The diagram below summarises the six layers — to be drawn in the answer book (1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for layer labels).

- **Layer 1 — HAL (1 mark):** Provides a uniform interface to hardware (touchscreen, display, sensors, audio codec). Translates electrical signals into logical events. Example: Linux `evdev` subsystem.
- **Layer 2 — Kernel (1 mark):** Schedules processes/threads, manages virtual memory, handles interrupts, and exposes the input subsystem. For HMI, it must ensure the **input thread** runs at high priority.
- **Layer 3 — System Services (0.5 mark):** Background daemons — power manager, audio server (PulseAudio), sensor hub. On Android, this is `system_server`.
- **Layer 4 — Windowing Subsystem (1 mark):** Manages windows, Z-ordering, focus, and compositing. Examples: SurfaceFlinger (Android), Quartz (macOS), DWM (Windows).
- **Layer 5 — UI Toolkit (0.5 mark):** Provides reusable widgets — buttons, sliders, lists. Cross-platform: Flutter, Qt. Native: UIKit, Jetpack Compose.
- **Layer 6 — Application (0.5 mark):** End-user logic — calculator, browser, SCADA view.
- **HMI Implication (1 mark):** Each layer adds **latency and abstraction**. Designers must balance richness of the toolkit against determinism of the kernel. For a real-time HMI (e.g., pacemaker), the windowing and toolkit layers are *bypassed* for a custom bare-metal UI.

**(b) Comparison of Android, iOS, and QNX (7 marks)**

| Criterion | Android (Linux kernel + ART) | iOS (XNU kernel) | QNX (Neutrino microkernel) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kernel Type | Monolithic (Linux) | Hybrid (XNU/Mach) | Microkernel |
| UI Toolkit | Jetpack Compose, Views | SwiftUI, UIKit | Qt for QNX, custom |
| Certification | None mandatory | None mandatory | **ISO 26262, IEC 62304** |
| App Distribution | Google Play sandboxed | App Store sandboxed | Locked, vendor-controlled |
| Real-Time Capable | Soft real-time | Soft real-time | **Hard real-time** |
| Ecosystem | Massive, open | Massive, closed | Niche, B2B |
| Footprint | Medium (2–4 GB) | Medium (2–4 GB) | Small (< 100 MB) |

**Suitability Analysis (3.5 marks — split equally):**

- **(i) Consumer smartphone (1.5 marks):** **Android and iOS** are ideal — they offer rich UI toolkits, gesture support, and vibrant app ecosystems. QNX would be unsuitable because it lacks the consumer app infrastructure and multitouch framework.
- **(ii) Automotive instrument cluster (1.5 marks):** **QNX** is preferred. The instrument cluster shows speed, RPM, and ADAS warnings with **< 10 ms** determinism. QNX is ISO 26262 ASIL-D certifiable. Android and iOS cannot meet functional-safety certification out of the box (though Android Automotive OS is catching up with a virtualised QNX safety domain).
- **(iii) Medical ventilator (1.5 marks):** **QNX** (or VxWorks). Ventilators must deliver breaths at precise intervals; any scheduling jitter can harm the patient. QNX supports the FDA IEC 62304 software-safety lifecycle and is widely used in patient monitors, infusion pumps, and ventilators.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often write *"Android is real-time"* — this is **wrong**. Android is *soft* real-time at best; the Linux CFS scheduler offers no hard deadline guarantees. Never claim Android is suitable for an airbag or ventilator without qualification.
> - Students forget to mention **certification** (ISO 26262, IEC 62304) when justifying OS choice for safety-critical HMI. Examiners specifically look for this keyword.

---

### Question B (Alternative — 14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**
**(a)** Explain the role of an **operating system scheduler** in an HMI. Compute the CPU utilisation for a system that is busy for **3.6 s** in every **5 s** window, and comment on whether it can sustain a **90 FPS** UI without frame drops. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **characteristics of an RTOS** that make it suitable for safety-critical HMI. With an example, explain the term **"motion-to-photon latency"** and state the typical VR-comfort threshold. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Scheduler and CPU Utilisation (7 marks)**

The OS scheduler is the component of the kernel that **decides which runnable thread gets the CPU and for how long**. In HMI, the scheduler must give **elevated priority** to two threads above all others (2 marks):

1. The **input thread** — reads touch, key, mouse, and sensor events.
2. The **UI/Compositor thread** — paints pixels to the display frame buffer.

If either is descheduled, the user perceives **lag** or **dropped frames**.

**CPU Utilisation Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
U = \frac{T_{busy}}{T_{total}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
U = \frac{3.6}{5.0} \times 100\%
$$

$$
U = 72\%
$$

**Logic:** The CPU is busy 72 % of the wall-clock time. A 90 FPS display requires a new frame every:

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{1000}{90} = 11.11 \text{ ms}
$$

To sustain 90 FPS, the UI thread must finish its work within **11.11 ms** of every 11.11 ms slot. A 72 % utilisation means 28 % of the time is *idle or descheduled*. If the UI thread is the *busy* portion and other threads occupy the rest, the UI thread gets at most ~72 % of each slot ≈ 8 ms of CPU per 11.11 ms frame.

**Verdict (2 marks):** With 72 % utilisation and a tight 11.11 ms frame budget, the system is **borderline**. Any OS jitter, interrupt storm, or garbage-collection pause (JVM/ART) will push the frame over budget and cause a **frame drop**. To be safe, the device should target **< 60 %** sustained utilisation for a 90 FPS UI, or upgrade to a 120 Hz panel for more headroom.

**(b) RTOS Characteristics and Motion-to-Photon Latency (7 marks)**

**RTOS Characteristics for Safety-Critical HMI (3.5 marks):**
1. **Determinism** — worst-case interrupt latency is bounded and known (e.g., QNX claims < 1 µs).
2. **Priority-preemptive scheduling** — high-priority safety threads pre-empt low-priority background work instantly.
3. **Memory protection** — process isolation prevents a faulty task from corrupting safety code.
4. **Small footprint** — runs on resource-constrained MCUs.
5. **Certification pedigree** — pre-certified to IEC 61508, ISO 26262, DO-178C.
6. **No virtual memory / paging** — eliminates page-fault jitter.

**Motion-to-Photon Latency (3.5 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Motion-to-photon latency ($L_{m2p}$) is the elapsed time from a user’s **physical movement** (e.g., head rotation detected by the IMU) to the moment the **corresponding updated pixel** is visible on the display.
- **Pipeline (1 mark):** It is the sum of IMU sampling, OS scheduling, game-engine rendering, compositor queueing, display scan-out, and pixel response.
- **Threshold (1 mark):** The widely cited **VR comfort threshold is 20 ms**; below this, users do not perceive motion-to-photon lag and do not experience simulator sickness. The **ideal** is < 11 ms (one frame at 90 Hz) or even < 8.3 ms (one frame at 120 Hz).

**Example:** The Meta Quest 3 targets 13 ms $L_{m2p}$ at 90 Hz using a dedicated **real-time compositor** running on a fixed-frequency scheduler that bypasses the Linux CFS for the rendering path.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often confuse **throughput** with **utilisation**. Throughput is *tasks per second*; utilisation is a *fraction of time busy*. Mark deduction if swapped.
> - For the motion-to-photon question, students forget to mention the **sensor sampling stage** at the start of the pipeline. Examiners expect the full pipeline (IMU → scheduler → renderer → compositor → display).
> - Do **not** state the VR comfort threshold as 11 ms — that is the *frame time at 90 Hz*, not the comfort threshold. The 20 ms comfort threshold comes from VR ergonomics research.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Definition:** A software environment = OS + windowing system + UI toolkit + runtime + applications.
- [ ] **Six Layers:** HAL → Kernel → System Services → Windowing → UI Toolkit → Application.
- [ ] **WIMP** is the desktop paradigm; **Post-WIMP** = touch, voice, gesture, gaze.
- [ ] **GPOS** = throughput-oriented; **RTOS** = deadline-oriented.
- [ ] **Android/iOS** = consumer HMI; **QNX/VxWorks** = safety-critical HMI.
- [ ] **Input thread priority** must be elevated to avoid perceived lag.
- [ ] **CPU utilisation** $U = T_{busy}/T_{total}$ — keep below 60 % for 90 FPS UI.
- [ ] **Frame time** $T_{frame} = 1/f$ — 60 Hz → 16.67 ms; 90 Hz → 11.11 ms; 120 Hz → 8.33 ms.
- [ ] **Motion-to-photon** latency must stay **below 20 ms** for VR comfort.
- [ ] **Little’s Law** $L = \lambda W$ — used to size input-event queues.
- [ ] **Certifications to memorise:** ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 62304 (medical), DO-178C (avionics), IEC 61508 (industrial).
- [ ] **Touch response target:** < 50 ms; **Tap feedback target:** < 100 ms (Material Design).
- [ ] **Key windowing systems:** X11, Wayland, Win32/DWM, Quartz, SurfaceFlinger.
- [ ] **Key UI toolkits:** Qt, GTK, Flutter, React Native, SwiftUI, Jetpack Compose.
- [ ] **Microkernel** (QNX) ⇒ high reliability; **Monolithic** (Linux) ⇒ high throughput.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** For any HMI OS question — always justify your choice on **three axes — (1) Determinism, (2) Certification, (3) UI Richness**. Examiners award the *application* mark (1–2 marks) specifically for this trade-off discussion. Memorise one example OS from each HMI domain (smartphone, automotive, medical, VR) — the *example* mark is the most commonly lost 1-mark bonus in KTU valuations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Psychopathology of Everyday Things
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT614 - INTRODUCTION TO HUMAN MACHINE INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Psychopathology of Everyday Things

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Psychopathology of Everyday Things** is a foundational framework introduced by cognitive scientist and designer **Donald A. Norman** (originally in his 1988 essay, later expanded in the seminal book *The Design of Everyday Things*, revised edition 2013). It is the systematic study of *why perfectly functional, everyday objects routinely frustrate, confuse, and fail their users* — and how thoughtful design can eliminate these failures.

In KTU 2024 Scheme HMI terminology, the topic establishes the **theoretical basis for Human-Centered Design (HCD)** by analyzing the **mismatch between a designer's mental (conceptual) model and the user's mental (mental model)**. The "psychopathology" does not lie in the user — it lies in **poorly designed artifacts** that fail to communicate their operation, structure, and state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Thesis (Norman's Central Argument):**
> *“It is not the user's fault when a product fails. The fault lies in the design of the product — specifically, in the lack of visible cues, poor mapping, and inadequate feedback.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine walking up to a glass door at a hotel. You push. It doesn't open. You push harder. Still stuck. Embarrassed, you finally see the small **"Pull"** sign and yank it open. 

**What just happened?** The door failed to communicate *how* it should be operated. The hinges (visible) suggested one action; the handle (also visible) suggested another. Two contradictory signifiers created **cognitive conflict** — a small, everyday "psychopathological" event.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Norman Door":**
> A *Norman Door* is any door whose design fails to clearly indicate whether to push, pull, or slide. The term has become industry shorthand for **design that blames the user instead of the artifact**. The phenomenon is universal: in ATMs that confuse customers, stoves with identical knobs, remotes with 50 identical buttons, and software menus with hidden gestures.

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Concepts

Norman's framework is built on **three pillars** that explain the psychopathology:

| # | Pillar | Definition (KTU Terminology) |
|---|--------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Affordance** | The *relationship* between a property of an object and a capability of the user that determines how the object *can possibly be used* (e.g., a handle *affords* pulling). Coined by J.J. Gibson, popularized by Norman. |
| 2 | **Signifier** | Any *visible, audible, or tangible* signal that communicates the *appropriate* action to the user (e.g., the word "PUSH" etched on a door, the "raised" texture on the "5" key of a phone). |
| 3 | **Mapping** | The *relationship* between a control and its effect, or between a system state and its indicator (e.g., stove burner knob position mapped to the corresponding burner location on the cooktop). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Crucial Distinction (Most-Tested KTU Concept):**
> - **Affordance** = what an object *allows* (potential action).
> - **Signifier** = what an object *communicates* (perceived action).
> When affordances are **perceived as signifiers**, design is intuitive. When signifiers are **absent or misleading**, design creates the "psychopathology."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Principles of Design (Norman's Framework)

Norman distilled the design lessons of "everyday psychopathology" into **seven fundamental principles**. Each addresses a specific failure mode observed in poorly designed artifacts.

### Principle 1 — **Discoverability**
- **Concept:** The user can *figure out* what actions are possible and how to perform them, simply by looking at the object.
- **Why it matters:** If the user cannot discover the controls, every subsequent stage of interaction fails.
- **How it works:** Achieved through *visible signifiers* (labels, icons, shapes) and *constraints* (physical barriers that prevent wrong actions).

### Principle 2 — **Feedback**
- **Concept:** After performing an action, the user receives *immediate, clear information* about what happened and what changed.
- **Why it matters:** Without feedback, the user cannot confirm success or diagnose failure.
- **How it works:** Visual (LED turning on), auditory (click of a key), or haptic (phone vibration).
- **Test criterion:** Can the user, within **one second**, see/hear/feel the result of their action?

### Principle 3 — **Conceptual Model**
- **Concept:** The user forms a *consistent, predictable, and understandable* mental model of how the system works.
- **Why it matters:** A good conceptual model lets users predict future behavior and recover from errors.
- **How it works:** Surfaced through *system image* — the visible parts, sounds, and interactions that reveal the underlying model.

### Principle 4 — **Affordances**
- **Concept:** The object suggests its own use through its *perceived properties*.
- **Why it matters:** Well-designed affordances eliminate the need for instructions.
- **How it works:** A flat plate invites pushing; a recessed slot invites insertion; a soft key invites pressing.

### Principle 5 — **Signifiers**
- **Concept:** Visible indicators that *signal* where the action should occur and what action is appropriate.
- **Why it matters:** Affordances exist in the world; signifiers exist in the user's perception. *Design communicates through signifiers.*
- **How it works:** Labels, arrows, raised textures, animations.

### Principle 6 — **Mapping**
- **Concept:** A *natural relationship* between controls and their effects, or between system state and the world.
- **Why it matters:** Natural mappings reduce memory load and eliminate confusion.
- **How it works:** *Spatial mapping* (burner knob on stove directly below its burner) is the gold standard.

### Principle 7 — **Constraints**
- **Concept:** Design features that *restrict* the possible actions to only the correct ones.
- **Why it matters:** Constraints prevent errors *before* they occur.
- **Four types:**
  1. **Physical constraints** — shapes that physically prevent incorrect assembly (USB-C, SIM card tray).
  2. **Cultural constraints** — learned conventions (red = stop, green = go).
  3. **Semantic constraints** — meaning that suggests correct usage (lamp on a bedside table).
  4. **Logical constraints** — reasoning about outcomes (a list of single-radio options is mutually exclusive).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the single most-tested artifact for this topic.**

| Concept | Definition | Failure Symptom (Psychopathology) | Design Fix |
|---------|------------|-----------------------------------|------------|
| **Affordance** | Perceived action possibility | User does not know what the object *can do* | Shape/structure that suggests use |
| **Signifier** | Visible cue for action | User does not know what to *do* | Labels, icons, raised textures |
| **Mapping** | Control-to-effect relationship | User cannot predict outcome | Natural spatial mapping |
| **Feedback** | Confirmation of action | User does not know if action succeeded | LED, sound, vibration, animation |
| **Conceptual Model** | User's mental understanding | User's model contradicts the system | Consistent system image, good docs |
| **Constraint** | Restriction of possible actions | User makes errors during operation | Physical, logical, cultural, semantic limits |
| **Knowledge in the Head** | Information memorized by user | User must remember complex procedures | Reduce memory load, use recognition |
| **Knowledge in the World** | Information available in environment | User is overwhelmed by clutter | Smart defaults, visible state |
| **Gulf of Execution** | Gap between user intention and system action | User cannot figure out *how* to do it | Better signifiers, discoverability |
| **Gulf of Evaluation** | Gap between system state and user perception | User cannot figure out *what happened* | Better feedback, clear display |

> [!NOTE]
> **No equations apply here.** This is a conceptual framework grounded in cognitive psychology, not applied mathematics. The "units" of design are *user actions, system states, and perception events* measured in qualitative cognitive cost (e.g., *learning time, error rate, satisfaction score*).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Consumer Electronics:** Smart-home device UX (Alexa, Google Home) is judged on discoverability and feedback.
- **Automotive UX:** Modern cars use **natural mapping** for window controls and **haptic feedback** for touchscreens to reduce driver distraction.
- **Medical Devices:** Norman's principles are **safety-critical** in infusion pumps, defibrillators, and surgical robots — poor signifiers can be fatal.
- **Aviation Cockpits:** Autopilot interfaces use **constraints and feedback** to prevent pilots from entering unsafe flight envelopes.
- **Software Engineering:** Material Design (Google) and Human Interface Guidelines (Apple) are *codified* versions of Norman's seven principles.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 The Seven Stages of Action (Norman's Action Cycle)

Every human interaction with an object — from switching on a light to flying an aircraft — passes through **seven cognitive stages**. Understanding these stages is essential to identify *where* the psychopathology occurs.

### The Seven Stages — Stage-by-Stage Analytical Breakdown

**Stage 1 — Forming the Goal (User Side)**
- The user establishes a *high-level intention*: "I want to read this book at night."
- This stage is **internal** and cannot be directly designed for.
- **Designer's job:** Ensure that *some affordance in the environment* triggers or supports this goal (e.g., a reading lamp on the bedside table).

**Stage 2 — Planning the Action (User Side)**
- The user decides *what general strategy* to use: "I will turn on the bedside lamp."
- This is still internal; the designer cannot see it.
- **Designer's job:** Make sure the lamp is *discoverable* and *identifiable* as the correct tool.

**Stage 3 — Specifying an Action Sequence (User Side)**
- The user sequences specific motor actions: "Reach out, grasp the knob, twist clockwise."
- This is where the **Gulf of Execution** must be bridged.
- **Designer's job:** Provide clear signifiers so the user knows *exactly* which knob to twist and in *which direction*.

**Stage 4 — Performing the Action Sequence (User Side)**
- The user executes the motor plan: hand moves, fingers grip, wrist rotates.
- **Designer's job:** Ensure physical ergonomics and constraints prevent incorrect grip (knob is round = twist, slider is linear = slide).

**Stage 5 — Perceiving the State of the World (System Side)**
- The user looks/listens/feels to *see what changed*: "Did the lamp turn on?"
- This is where the **Gulf of Evaluation** must be bridged.
- **Designer's job:** Provide *unambiguous feedback* — the bulb glows, a click is heard.

**Stage 6 — Interpreting the State of the World (User Side)**
- The user *makes sense* of the feedback: "Yes, the lamp is on, so my action worked."
- **Designer's job:** Ensure feedback is interpretable, not ambiguous (e.g., a dim glow could mean "on" or "failing bulb").

**Stage 7 — Comparing the Outcome with the Goal (User Side)**
- The user *verifies* success: "The lamp is on, and it illuminates my book — goal achieved."
- If outcome ≠ goal, the user loops back to Stage 1 or 2.

### Mathematical-Style Diagrammatic Representation

$$
\text{Seven Stages} = \underbrace{G_1, P_1, S_1}_{\text{User: Goal-Setting}} \;\to\; \underbrace{A_1, A_2}_{\text{User: Action}} \;\to\; \underbrace{P_2, I}_{\text{User: Perception}} \;\to\; \underbrace{C}_{\text{User: Evaluation}}
$$

Where:
- $G_1$ = Forming the Goal
- $P_1$ = Planning
- $S_1$ = Specifying Action Sequence
- $A_1$ = Performing Action
- $A_2$ = (System executes)
- $P_2$ = Perceiving System State
- $I$ = Interpreting Perception
- $C$ = Comparing Outcome to Goal

## 3.2 The Two Gulfs — Detailed Analytical Walkthrough

### Gulf of Execution

$$
\text{Gulf of Execution} = f(\text{User Intention},\;\text{System Interface})
$$

This is the **cognitive distance** the user must cross to translate their *intention* into the *physical actions* the system allows. Steps to bridge it:

1. **Identify goal** → What does the user want?
2. **Identify means** → What controls are available?
3. **Sequence actions** → In what order?
4. **Execute actions** → Perform them.

> **Design failure example (Norman Door):** The user's intention is "enter the building." The means are unclear (push or pull?). The Gulf of Execution is *wide* → user fails.

### Gulf of Evaluation

$$
\text{Gulf of Evaluation} = f(\text{System State},\;\text{User Perception})
$$

This is the **cognitive distance** the user must cross to interpret the *system's physical state* and determine whether their goal was met. Steps to bridge it:

1. **Perceive** → What changed physically?
2. **Interpret** → What does the change mean?
3. **Compare** → Does this match my goal?

> **Design failure example (Stove with identical knobs):** The user turns a knob. They perceive *some* heat. They cannot interpret *which burner* is on. The Gulf of Evaluation is *wide* → user burns food (or themselves).

## 3.3 Case Study Walkthrough — The Classic Norman Door

Let us trace a **Norman Door** (push-plate on a pull door) through the complete analytical matrix:

| Stage | User Action / Cognition | System Response | Psychopathology? |
|-------|-------------------------|-----------------|------------------|
| 1. Goal | "Enter the building" | — | None |
| 2. Plan | "Approach the door and operate it" | — | None |
| 3. Specify | "Push the plate" (because it *looks* pushable) | — | **Signifier misleads** |
| 4. Perform | Hand extends, palm contacts plate, force applied | Door does not open | **No affordance signaled for pull** |
| 5. Perceive | User sees door still closed, hears nothing | — | **No feedback for "wrong action"** |
| 6. Interpret | "I must push harder" | — | **User blamed, not design** |
| 7. Compare | Goal unmet | — | Loop back to Stage 3 |

**Root cause analysis:** Two design failures stack here.
- *Missing signifier:* No "Pull" label or visible hinges to the user.
- *Wrong mapping:* Push-plate affordance conflicts with the door's actual pull mechanism.

**Design fix (Norman's prescription):**
1. Add a clear **"Pull"** label.
2. Make the **handle visually pull-shaped** (a vertical bar is the universal signifier for pull).
3. Use **physical constraints** — a recessed pocket that physically *invites* a pulling grip.
4. Provide **feedback** — a small "give" when the door starts to move.

## 3.4 The Knowledge-in-the-Head vs. Knowledge-in-the-World Tradeoff

Norman formalized another design principle: the *placement* of cognitive load.

$$
\text{Cognitive Load} = K_{\text{head}}(t) + K_{\text{world}}(t) - K_{\text{recognized}}(t)
$$

Where:
- $K_{\text{head}}(t)$ = information the user must *remember* at time $t$ (costly, error-prone).
- $K_{\text{world}}(t)$ = information the user can *see/hear/read* in the environment at time $t$ (cheap, reliable).
- $K_{\text{recognized}}(t)$ = overlap that supports pattern recognition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule:** *Push knowledge into the world whenever possible.* A well-designed ATM shows on-screen options rather than requiring the user to memorize menu codes. A well-designed app shows the current state, not just an action button.

This is the basis of **Recognition over Recall** — a core Nielsen Norman Group usability heuristic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Seven Stages of Action (Norman's Action Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stage1[STAGE 1 Forming the Goal] --> stage2[STAGE 2 Planning the Action]
    stage2 --> stage3[STAGE 3 Specifying Action Sequence]
    stage3 --> stage4[STAGE 4 Performing the Action]
    stage4 --> stage5[STAGE 5 Perceiving the World State]
    stage5 --> stage6[STAGE 6 Interpreting Perception]
    stage6 --> stage7[STAGE 7 Comparing to Goal]
    stage7 -- Goal Achieved --> doneA[TASK COMPLETE]
    stage7 -- Goal Not Met --> stage1
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — The Two Gulfs of HCI (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[USER MENTAL MODEL] -->|Intention Travels| gulfA
    gulfA{GULF OF EXECUTION} -->|Action Sequence| sysA[SYSTEM INTERFACE]
    sysA -->|Physical State Change| gulfB
    gulfB{GULF OF EVALUATION} -->|Perception| userA
    styleGulf[Design Goal: Shrink Both Gulfs via Signifiers Mapping Feedback]
    gulfA -.-> styleGulf
    gulfB -.-> styleGulf
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Affordances, Signifiers, and Constraints (Design Triad)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    objA[EVERYDAY OBJECT e.g. Door Handle] --> affA
    objA --> sigA
    objA --> conA
    affA[AFFORDANCE What actions are POSSIBLE] --> mergeA
    sigA[SIGNIFIER What actions are PERCEIVED] --> mergeA
    conA[CONSTRAINT What actions are PREVENTED] --> mergeA
    mergeA{ALIGNMENT OF THREE} --> goodA[INTUITIVE DESIGN No Psychopathology]
    mergeA --> badA[DESIGN FAILURE Norman Door Confusion Frustration Error]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — The Knowledge Placement Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    headA[KNOWLEDGE IN THE HEAD User Memorizes Codes Passwords Sequences] -->|High Cognitive Load| badB[ERROR PRONE FRUSTRATING]
    worldA[KNOWLEDGE IN THE WORLD Environment Shows Hints Labels Icons] -->|Low Cognitive Load| goodB[RELIABLE ACCESSIBLE]
    recogA[RECOGNITION NOT RECALL User Chooses From Visible Options] -->|Lowest Load| bestB[USABILITY GOLD STANDARD]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Complete Psychopathology Causal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cause1[POOR DESIGN] --> cause2
    cause2[NO SIGNIFIERS NO FEEDBACK BAD MAPPING] --> cause3
    cause3[USER CANNOT DISCOVER ACTIONS OR INTERPRET RESULTS] --> cause4
    cause4[USER FORCED TO GUESS EXPERIMENT BLAME SELF] --> cause5
    cause5[FRUSTRATION ERRORS TASK FAILURE] --> cause6
    cause6[BLAME PLACED ON USER INSTEAD OF DESIGNER]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams for KTU exams:** Each diagram is a *visual answer* to a likely question. In a 14-mark question, a well-labeled Mermaid-style block diagram earns **2–3 marks** on its own if drawn correctly with labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**  
> **Define the term "Affordance" as introduced by Donald Norman. How is it different from a "Signifier"? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** An *affordance* is the relationship between the physical properties of an object and the capabilities of a user that determines how the object can be used. For example, a door handle *affords* pulling, and a flat push-plate *affords* pushing.

**Difference (1 mark):** Affordance is the *potential* for action; signifier is the *visible or perceivable cue* that communicates the action. Affordances exist in the object; signifiers exist in the user's perception.

**Example (1 mark):** The flat plate on a Norman Door *affords* pushing, but there is *no signifier* telling the user this is a pull door. Adding the word "Pull" creates a signifier.

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Sample, Module 1]**  
> **What is meant by the "Gulf of Execution" and the "Gulf of Evaluation"? Why must designers minimize both?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Gulf of Execution (1 mark):** The cognitive gap between the *user's intention* and the *physical actions* required to accomplish it. Designers minimize it by improving *discoverability* and providing clear *signifiers*.

**Gulf of Evaluation (1 mark):** The cognitive gap between the *system's physical state* and the *user's perception/understanding* of that state. Designers minimize it by providing clear *feedback*.

**Why both (1 mark):** Wide gulfs lead to *errors, frustration, and task failure*. Norman argued the user should never be blamed — design must bridge both gulfs for intuitive interaction.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Past Pattern, Module 1 Internal Choice]**  
> *"Donald Norman's framework explains the cause of everyday design failures and prescribes a remedy."*  
> **(a)** Explain the **seven stages of action** with a neat diagram and discuss where the "psychopathology" typically arises. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Discuss Norman's **seven principles of design** and illustrate each with a real-world example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) The Seven Stages of Action (7 marks):**

1. **Forming the Goal (1 mark):** The user establishes an intention, e.g., "I want to read a book at night." Designers cannot directly design for this stage, but can *invite* goals through visible affordances.
2. **Planning the Action (1 mark):** The user decides the *general strategy*, e.g., "I will turn on the lamp." Design implication — the correct tool must be discoverable.
3. **Specifying the Action Sequence (1 mark):** The user sequences motor actions: reach, grasp, twist. This is the **start of the Gulf of Execution**.
4. **Performing the Action (1 mark):** The user executes the motor plan. The interface must *guide* correct action.
5. **Perceiving the State of the World (1 mark):** The user perceives what changed. The **Gulf of Evaluation** begins here.
6. **Interpreting the Perception (1 mark):** The user makes sense of the feedback. Designers must make feedback interpretable.
7. **Comparing Outcome with Goal (1 mark):** The user verifies success. If mismatched, they loop back to Stage 1 or 2.

**Where psychopathology arises:** In the *middle* stages (3, 4, 5, 6) — wherever signifiers, mapping, or feedback are inadequate.

**(b) The Seven Principles of Design (7 marks):**

1. **Discoverability (1 mark):** The user can see what is possible. Example: All controls of a microwave are *visible* on the front panel.
2. **Feedback (1 mark):** The user gets immediate confirmation. Example: The little "click" when a key on a keyboard is pressed.
3. **Conceptual Model (1 mark):** The system supports a coherent mental model. Example: A file folder icon universally signifies "save."
4. **Affordances (1 mark):** The object suggests its use. Example: A teapot handle *affords* gripping.
5. **Signifiers (1 mark):** Visible cues. Example: The word "PUSH" on a glass door.
6. **Mapping (1 mark):** Natural control-to-effect relationship. Example: Car window switches laid out in the *spatial arrangement* of the windows.
7. **Constraints (1 mark):** Restrictions that prevent errors. Example: A SIM card tray's *unique shape* prevents incorrect insertion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> Students often *list* the seven stages/principles without explaining *where* the psychopathology occurs. To score full marks, you must (1) state each stage/principle, (2) explain it in 1–2 lines, and (3) tie it back to a *concrete design failure or fix*. Marks are awarded for **application**, not mere recall.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Alternative)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Past Pattern, Module 1 Internal Choice]**  
> **(a)** Define **affordances, signifiers, and mappings**. How do they work together in effective design? Provide examples. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Critically analyze the concept of a **"Norman Door."** What design principles were violated? How would you redesign it using Norman's framework? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Affordances, Signifiers, and Mappings (7 marks):**

1. **Affordances (2 marks):** Perceived action possibilities of an object. *Real* affordances are physical (e.g., a handle affords pulling); *perceived* affordances are how the user *imagines* the object can be used. A button *affords* pressing because of its shape and position.
2. **Signifiers (2 marks):** Visible, audible, or tangible signals that *communicate* the affordance. A "PUSH" label, a raised texture, a flashing icon. Affordances are *real*; signifiers are *communicated*.
3. **Mappings (1.5 marks):** Natural correspondence between controls and their effects. Spatial mapping is best — e.g., the four stove burner knobs are *arranged in the same pattern* as the four burners on the cooktop.
4. **Working Together (1.5 marks):** When affordances are *visible as signifiers* and the mapping is *natural*, the user requires *no instructions*. The artifact becomes *self-explanatory*. When any of these three fails, "psychopathology" emerges.

**(b) The Norman Door — Critical Analysis & Redesign (7 marks):**

1. **What is a Norman Door? (1 mark):** A door whose design fails to clearly indicate whether to push or pull, leading users to perform the wrong action.
2. **Principles Violated (3 marks):**
   - *Signifiers missing or misleading:* The push-plate affords pushing, but the door must be pulled.
   - *Mapping broken:* The control (plate) does not map to the actual operation (pull).
   - *Feedback absent:* No visual or tactile cue signals when the wrong action is attempted.
   - *Constraints absent:* No physical feature forces the user to use the correct motion.
3. **Redesign Using Norman's Framework (3 marks):**
   - Add a clear **"Pull"** sign and a *vertical bar handle* (the universal pull signifier).
   - Use **physical constraints** — a recessed pocket invites a pulling grip; a flat surface invites pushing.
   - Ensure **natural mapping** — if the door opens toward the user, the handle should be on the *pull side*; hinges visible.
   - Provide **feedback** — a slight "give" or click when the user begins the correct motion.
   - Add **cultural signifiers** — a green "in" arrow on the side that pushes inward.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> A common mistake is conflating *affordance* with *signifier*. Examiners specifically look for the distinction. Memorize this line: **"Affordances are what the object allows; signifiers are what the user perceives."** Also, do not describe a Norman Door merely as "a confusing door" — name the *specific principle* violated and propose a *specific* redesign.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Revision)**

- [ ] **Norman (1988 / 2013)** coined the framework; the topic critiques the designer's role, not the user's intelligence.
- [ ] **Affordance** = potential for action; **Signifier** = perceived cue for action. The two are *not* synonyms.
- [ ] **Mapping** must be *natural* — spatial arrangement of controls should match spatial arrangement of effects.
- [ ] **Feedback** must be *immediate* (within ~1 second) and *interpretable*.
- [ ] **Constraints** come in four types: physical, cultural, semantic, logical.
- [ ] **Seven stages of action:** Goal → Plan → Specify → Perform → Perceive → Interpret → Compare.
- [ ] **Gulf of Execution** = intention → action (bridge with signifiers + discoverability).
- [ ] **Gulf of Evaluation** = system state → user perception (bridge with feedback).
- [ ] **Knowledge in the head** is costly; **knowledge in the world** is cheap. **Recognition > Recall.**
- [ ] **Norman Door** is the canonical example — always cite it in answers.
- [ ] **Conceptual model** lives in the user's mind; **system image** lives in the artifact. The designer's job is to *align* them.
- [ ] **Three pillars of good design** (for diagram answers): Affordance + Signifier + Constraint.
- [ ] **KTU favorite 14-mark structure:** (a) Define + diagram + example (7 marks), (b) Analyze failure + prescribe fix (7 marks).
- [ ] **Always end answers** with a *design prescription* — Norman advocates **user-centered design**, never user-blame.
- [ ] **Cross-link** this topic with Module 2 topics on **usability heuristics, user research, and prototyping** — questions often span modules.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Topic Notes — The Psychopathology of Everyday Things (Module 1, OECPT614, KTU 2024 Scheme).**  
> For maximum marks, every answer should (1) state the principle, (2) explain it briefly, (3) give an example, and (4) suggest a fix. This four-part structure is the **single most reliable formula** for scoring above 80% in KTU HMI examinations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychology of Everyday Actions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT614 - INTRODUCTION TO HUMAN MACHINE INTERACTION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Psychology of Everyday Actions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Psychology of Everyday Actions** refers to the cognitive and behavioral framework that explains how humans translate their **intentions (goals)** into **physical actions** and how they **interpret the results** of those actions in the context of interacting with systems, devices, and interfaces. In Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), this concept is primarily formalized through **Donald Norman's "Seven Stages of Action"** model, which dissects every user-system interaction into a cycle of **goal formulation, execution, and evaluation**. The discipline sits at the intersection of **cognitive psychology**, **ergonomics**, and **interaction design**, providing the theoretical scaffolding for understanding *why* users behave the way they do when operating everyday artifacts — from door handles to graphical user interfaces. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Coffee Mug" Scenario** > > Imagine you are sitting at a desk and you want a sip of coffee. What happens in your brain? > 1. You **form a goal**: "I want coffee." > 2. You **intend an action**: "I will pick up the mug." > 3. You **specify a sequence**: "Reach hand, close fingers, lift." > 4. Your **brain executes** the motor command. > 5. You **perceive** the mug in your hand. > 6. You **interpret** the sensation (weight, warmth). > 7. You **evaluate** the result: "Yes, I have the coffee." > > This entire cycle, completed in under a second, is the **Psychology of Everyday Actions**. Every interaction with a computer — clicking a button, dragging a file, entering a password — replicates this exact cognitive loop. In HCI design, the goal of the designer is to **minimize the cognitive distance** the user must traverse between stages. When this distance is too large, users experience **frustration, errors, and abandonment**. --- ## 1.3 Standard HCI Metrics & Terminology The following are the **canonical engineering metrics** used to quantify user interaction quality in HCI, derived from the psychology of action: - **Gulf of Execution**: The cognitive gap between the **user's intention** and the **physical actions** required to achieve it at the system interface. - **Gulf of Evaluation**: The cognitive gap between the **system's physical state** and the **user's understanding** of that state. - **Mapping**: The relationship between a control and its resulting action in the world (e.g., stove knob position to burner intensity). - **Feedback**: Information sent back to the user regarding the result of an action. - **Affordance**: A property of an object that suggests how it can be used (a door handle *affords* pushing or pulling). > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the **Seven Stages of Action** model and the **Gulfs of Execution and Evaluation** as the foundational cognitive models for Module 1. Mastery of these is essential for both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework ## 2.1 Norman's Seven Stages of Action (The Core Framework) Donald Norman (1988), in *The Design of Everyday Things*, proposed that every purposeful human action is composed of **seven distinct cognitive stages**, grouped into **three major phases**: ### Phase 1: Goal Formation (Setting the Target) - **Stage 1 — Goal**: The user establishes a desired state. *"I want to save my document."* ### Phase 2: Execution (Doing the Action) - **Stage 2 — Intention**: The user decides on a high-level strategy. *"I will use the File menu."* - **Stage 3 — Specification of Action Sequence**: The user plans the detailed steps. *"Click File → Click Save → Confirm filename."* - **Stage 4 — Execution of Action**: The user physically performs the sequence. *"Hand moves the mouse, finger clicks."* ### Phase 3: Evaluation (Checking the Result) - **Stage 5 — Perception**: The user observes the system state. *"A dialog box appears."* - **Stage 6 — Interpretation**: The user makes sense of the perception. *"This is the Save dialog."* - **Stage 7 — Evaluation**: The user compares the result to the original goal. *"Yes, the file is saved."* > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Core "Why"**: HCI design must support *all seven stages*. A flaw in any single stage — for example, a missing confirmation after a Save action — can break the user's ability to close the loop and achieve confidence in the system. --- ## 2.2 The Three Gulfs (High-Yield Framework) The Seven Stages are bridged by **three cognitive gulfs** that designers must minimize: | Gulf Name | Location in the Model | What it Measures | Design Strategy to Bridge It | |---|---|---|---| | **Gulf of Execution** | Between Stage 1 (Goal) and Stage 4 (Execution) | Distance from user's intention to the physical actions the system requires | Provide clear **affordances, mappings, and constraints** so the user knows what to do without instruction | | **Gulf of Evaluation** | Between Stage 5 (Perception) and Stage 7 (Evaluation) | Distance from system state to user's understanding of that state | Provide rich, immediate, and interpretable **feedback** | | **Gulf of Experience** | Between expectation and actual outcome | Long-term gap between what users *think* will happen and what actually happens | Maintain **consistency, standards, and predictability** across interactions | --- ## 2.3 Mapping, Affordance, and Feedback (The Design Triad) These three principles are the **practical tools** used by designers to bridge the gulfs: - **Mapping**: A natural relationship between a control and its effect. *Example: A scroll bar's position directly maps to the vertical position in a document.* - **Affordance**: A visual or structural cue that signals possible actions. *Example: A button's 3D shadow affords clicking.* - **Feedback**: Immediate, visible response to user action. *Example: A button briefly depresses and changes color when clicked.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility**: In real-world HCI engineering (UX/UI design, industrial control rooms, automotive dashboards, medical device interfaces), these three principles form the **evaluation rubric** used in heuristic evaluations and usability testing. The ISO 9241-210 standard explicitly references Norman's framework. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Concept Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Definition | Key Cognitive Stage (Norman) | Design Countermeasure | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Goal** | Desired end state | Stage 1 | Provide clear entry points | | 2 | **Intention** | High-level plan | Stage 2 | Use familiar mental models | | 3 | **Action Spec.** | Step-by-step plan | Stage 3 | Visible system status | | 4 | **Execution** | Physical action | Stage 4 | Affordances & constraints | | 5 | **Perception** | Sensory input | Stage 5 | Multi-modal feedback | | 6 | **Interpretation** | Meaning-making | Stage 6 | Signifiers & labels | | 7 | **Evaluation** | Goal-comparison | Stage 7 | Confirmation messages | | 8 | **Gulf of Execution** | Intention → Action | Stages 1–4 | Good affordances | | 9 | **Gulf of Evaluation** | Perception → Goal | Stages 5–7 | Good feedback | | 10 | **Slip** | Action-based error | Stage 4 failure | Confirmation dialogs | | 11 | **Mistake** | Intention-based error | Stage 2 failure | Better mental models | > [!NOTE] > **Units & Metrics**: In HCI, the "distance" across a gulf is measured in **cognitive load (bits of information)**, **task completion time (seconds)**, and **error rate (errors per task)**. These are the standard benchmarks in usability engineering. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Application ## 3.1 Worked Example: Saving a File in Microsoft Word To consolidate the theory, we will trace the **exact cognitive sequence** a typical user follows when executing a common HCI action — saving a document. This is a classic KTU-style 14-mark analysis question. ### The Scenario A user, **Ravi**, has finished typing a letter in Microsoft Word and wants to save it to disk. ### Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough **Step 1 — Goal Formation (Stage 1)** Ravi's brain establishes the target state: $$\text{Goal} = \{\text{"Document X is permanently stored on disk and recoverable later"\}$$ > *Cognitive Load: High-level, abstract. The user does not yet know HOW to achieve this.* **Step 2 — Intention Formation (Stage 2)** Ravi decides on a high-level strategy. He has two options: - Option A: Use the keyboard shortcut `Ctrl + S` (exploits his prior knowledge). - Option B: Navigate via the menu bar (File → Save). $$\text{Intention} = \arg\min_{a \in \text{Actions}} (\text{Effort}(a) \mid \text{Reliability}(a))$$ > *He chooses Option A because it is faster, demonstrating the "least effort" principle in human action.* **Step 3 — Specification of Action Sequence (Stage 3)** Ravi's brain converts the intention into a motor plan: $$\text{Action Sequence} = [\text{Press L-Ctrl}, \text{Press S}, \text{Release S}, \text{Release L-Ctrl}]$$ > *This is a procedural, sub-second plan stored in procedural memory.* **Step 4 — Execution (Stage 4)** Ravi's motor cortex transmits signals to his fingers. The keystrokes are sent to the OS via the keyboard interrupt handler. > *Failure Point: If the keyboard is unplugged, the Gulf of Execution is unmapped — the user cannot bridge intention to action.* **Step 5 — Perception (Stage 5)** The OS receives the shortcut, the application intercepts it, and the GUI updates.
