Ravi's eyes detect a **brief flash on the Save icon** in the title bar (or a dialog appears asking for a filename if first-time save). $$\text{Perceived Signal} = \{\text{Visual change in title bar} \cup \text{Dialog box appearance}\}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation (Stage 6)** Ravi's brain decodes the visual signal: - *If the title bar changed and no dialog appeared* → "The file was already named and was saved silently." - *If a dialog appeared* → "I need to provide a filename and location." $$\text{Interpretation} = f(\text{Perceived Signal}, \text{System Affordances}, \text{Context})$$ **Step 7 — Evaluation (Stage 7)** Ravi compares the current state to his goal: $$\text{Goal Match} = \begin{cases} \text{True} & \text{if file exists on disk and is unchanged} \\ \text{False} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ If `Goal Match = True`, the action loop closes successfully. If `False`, Ravi initiates a **new goal formation** cycle (e.g., "Where did the file actually save?"). > *This 7-stage loop completes in roughly 1.5 to 3 seconds for an expert user — the *cognitive throughput* of the human action cycle.* --- ## 3.2 Slips vs. Mistakes (A Critical Distinction) This distinction is **heavily tested** in KTU examinations. The framework differentiates two types of user error based on *where* in the seven stages they occur. ### Slips (Action-Based Errors) - **Definition**: The user's *intention* is correct, but the *execution* goes wrong. The error is at **Stages 3 or 4**. - **Example**: You intend to save with `Ctrl + S`, but accidentally press `Ctrl + Shift + S` (Save As), opening an unexpected dialog. - **Cognitive Cause**: Inattentional blindness, autopilot behavior, muscle memory mismatch. - **Design Remedy**: Provide **confirmation dialogs, undo functionality, and clear signifiers**. ### Mistakes (Intention-Based Errors) - **Definition**: The user's *intention* itself is wrong, even though the execution is perfect. The error is at **Stages 1 or 2**. - **Example**: You press `Ctrl + S` on a document you *intended* to print, because you misread the menu icons. - **Cognitive Cause**: Wrong mental model, poor system feedback earlier in the session. - **Design Remedy**: Provide **better mental models, clearer conceptual models, and information-rich feedback**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pattern**: When asked to "distinguish between slips and mistakes with examples," students MUST mention (1) the stage of the model where each occurs, (2) a concrete real-world example, and (3) the design remedy. Missing any of these three elements typically results in a **2-mark deduction**. --- ## 3.3 The Action Cycle — Mathematical Representation of Closure The seven stages can be expressed as a **closed feedback loop**: $$\text{Action Cycle} = \oint_{\text{Goal}}^{\text{Evaluation}} \left( G + I + S + E + P + M + V \right) \, dt$$ Where: - $G$ = Goal state - $I$ = Intention formation - $S$ = Specification of action - $E$ = Execution - $P$ = Perception - $M$ = Interpretation (Meaning) - $V$ = Evaluation (Verification) The **cycle time** $T_c$ for an expert user on a well-designed interface is typically: $$T_c \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 3.0 \text{ seconds per action}$$ > *For a poorly designed interface (large gulfs), $T_c$ can balloon to 15–30 seconds, and error rates increase by an order of magnitude. This is why HCI optimization is a measurable engineering discipline.* # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Norman's Seven Stages of Action — Mermaid Flowchart The following Mermaid block visualizes the complete seven-stage action cycle, with the two major gulfs clearly demarcated. This diagram is the **single most important visual** for Module 1 examinations. ```mermaid flowchart TD goalA["STAGE 1: GOAL FORMATION
User establishes desired state"]:::goalStyle intentA["STAGE 2: INTENTION
User decides high-level strategy"]:::execStyle specA["STAGE 3: SPECIFICATION
User plans detailed steps"]:::execStyle execA["STAGE 4: EXECUTION
User performs physical action"]:::execStyle perceptA["STAGE 5: PERCEPTION
User observes system state"]:::evalStyle interpA["STAGE 6: INTERPRETATION
User makes sense of perception"]:::evalStyle evalA["STAGE 7: EVALUATION
User compares result to goal"]:::evalStyle gulf1["GULF OF EXECUTION
Bridge with Affordances, Mappings,
Constraints, and Standards"]:::gulfStyle gulf2["GULF OF EVALUATION
Bridge with Feedback, Signifiers,
and Visibility"]:::gulfStyle goalA --> intentA intentA --> specA specA --> execA execA -->|Physical change
in world| perceptA perceptA --> interpA interpA --> evalA evalA -->|Goal Met? Yes| goalA evalA -->|Goal Not Met| intentA gulf1 -.->|Spans Stages 1 to 4| execA gulf2 -.->|Spans Stages 5 to 7| evalA classDef goalStyle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef execStyle fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef evalStyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gulfStyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 ``` --- ## 4.2 The Three Gulfs — Conceptual Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR userNode["USER MIND
Goals, Intentions,
Mental Models"]:::userStyle sysNode["SYSTEM STATE
Buttons, Menus,
Display, Feedback"]:::sysStyle worldNode["PHYSICAL WORLD
Effects, Outcomes,
Real Changes"]:::worldStyle ge["GULF OF EXECUTION
Intention → Action
Requires: Affordances"]:::geStyle ge2["GULF OF EVALUATION
Perception → Understanding
Requires: Feedback"]:::geStyle ge3["GULF OF EXPERIENCE
Expectation vs Reality
Requires: Consistency"]:::geStyle userNode -->|Bridge via| ge ge --> sysNode sysNode -->|Bridge via| ge2 ge2 --> userNode sysNode -->|Bridge via| ge3 ge3 --> worldNode classDef userStyle fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef sysStyle fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef worldStyle fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B6914,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef geStyle fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000,stroke-dasharray: 3 3 ``` --- ## 4.3 Slips vs. Mistakes — Error Location Map ```mermaid flowchart TD errorStart["USER ERROR DETECTED
Action failed to achieve goal"]:::errorStyle checkInt["Was the user's
INTENTION correct?"]:::checkStyle slip["SLIP
Error at Stage 3 or 4
Execution failed"]:::slipStyle mistake["MISTAKE
Error at Stage 1 or 2
Wrong goal or plan"]:::mistakeStyle slipEx["Example: Pressed Ctrl+Shift+S
instead of Ctrl+S"]:::exStyle mistakeEx["Example: Clicked 'Print' icon
intending to 'Save'"]:::exStyle slipFix["Remedy: Confirmation dialogs,
Undo, Signifiers"]:::fixStyle mistakeFix["Remedy: Better mental models,
Clearer conceptual models"]:::fixStyle errorStart --> checkInt checkInt -->|YES| slip checkInt -->|NO| mistake slip --> slipEx mistake --> mistakeEx slip --> slipFix mistake --> mistakeFix classDef errorStyle fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef checkStyle fill:#FFFFE0,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef slipStyle fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mistakeStyle fill:#DA70D6,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef exStyle fill:#F5F5DC,stroke:#A9A9A9,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef fixStyle fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]` **Model Answer:** The **Gulf of Execution** is the cognitive gap between a user's *intention* (what they want to do) and the *physical actions* the system requires them to perform. A well-designed interface provides clear **affordances and mappings** to bridge this gulf. The **Gulf of Evaluation** is the cognitive gap between the *system's physical state* (what actually happened) and the *user's perception and understanding* of that state. A well-designed interface provides rich, immediate **feedback** to bridge this gulf. Both concepts originate from Donald Norman's Seven Stages of Action model and are foundational in evaluating the usability of any interactive system. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between a Slip and a Mistake. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper 2]` **Model Answer:** A **Slip** is an action-based error where the user's intention is correct, but the execution step fails. It occurs at **Stages 3 or 4** of Norman's model. *Example*: Intending to click "Save" but accidentally clicking the adjacent "Print" icon due to a slip of the mouse. A **Mistake** is an intention-based error where the user's goal or plan itself is incorrect, even though execution is perfect. It occurs at **Stages 1 or 2**. *Example*: Believing the floppy disk icon means "Save" when it actually means "Eject." Slips are remedied by better *signifiers and confirmation dialogs*; mistakes are remedied by *better mental models and conceptual models* in the interface. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A: Explain in detail Donald Norman's Seven Stages of Action model. How do the Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation arise from this model? Suggest design strategies to bridge each gulf. (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Question Pattern]` #### Part (a) — The Seven Stages of Action Model (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** Donald Norman's **Seven Stages of Action** model, proposed in *The Design of Everyday Things* (1988), decomposes every purposeful human interaction with a system into seven distinct cognitive stages, organized into three phases: **Phase 1 — Goal Formation (The "What")** 1. **Goal**: The user establishes the desired end-state. *Example*: "I want to send an email." 2. **Intention**: The user forms a high-level plan. *Example*: "I will click the Compose button." 3. **Specification of Action Sequence**: The user details the steps. *Example*: "Move mouse to Compose icon, click left button, type in To field." 4. **Execution**: The user physically performs the sequence via motor actions. **Phase 2 — Evaluation (The "What Happened")** 5. **Perception**: The user observes the system state via sensory input (sight, sound). 6. **Interpretation**: The user decodes the perception to form meaning. 7. **Evaluation**: The user compares the interpreted state to the original goal, closing the action loop. This model is **cyclic**, not linear. If evaluation reveals the goal is not met, a new goal formation cycle begins. --- #### Part (b) — The Gulfs and Their Design Remedies (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Gulf of Execution** (spans Stages 1–4): This is the cognitive distance between the user's *intention* and the *physical actions* the system allows. A large gulf means the user does not know *how* to operate the system. **Remedies**: - Use **natural mappings** (e.g., a volume slider moves up = louder). - Provide strong **affordances** (e.g., a button's 3D look invites clicking). - Apply **constraints** to prevent impossible actions (e.g., a disabled "Send" button until all fields are filled). - Adhere to **standards and conventions** (e.g., red icon = stop). **Gulf of Evaluation** (spans Stages 5–7): This is the cognitive distance between the *system's physical state* and the *user's understanding* of that state. A large gulf means the user does not know *what happened* after their action. **Remedies**: - Provide **immediate, visible feedback** (e.g., a button flash on click). - Use **multi-modal signals** (e.g., sound + visual for a successful action). - Display **system status** persistently (e.g., "Saving..." spinner). - Use **signifiers** to clarify perception (e.g., a green checkmark vs. red cross). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students frequently lose 2–3 marks by (1) listing the seven stages without grouping them into the three phases (Goal/Execution/Evaluation), and (2) stating "improve the design" without naming **specific design principles** (affordance, mapping, feedback, signifier). Always name the principle. --- ### Question B: Define the Psychology of Everyday Actions. With a suitable example, illustrate the seven stages of action. Also discuss the difference between slips and mistakes with examples. (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Alternative Pattern]` #### Part (a) — Definition and Worked Example (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Definition**: The Psychology of Everyday Actions is the study of how humans form intentions, execute physical actions to achieve goals, and evaluate the results of those actions when interacting with systems and artifacts. It is a foundational concept in HCI, formalized by **Donald Norman's Seven Stages of Action model**. **Worked Example — Setting an Alarm on a Smartphone:** | Stage | User's Internal Process | Observable Action | |---|---|---| | **1. Goal** | "I want to wake up at 6:00 AM tomorrow." | None yet | | **2. Intention** | "I will use the Clock app." | None yet | | **3. Specification** | "Open Clock → Tap Alarm → Tap + → Set time to 6:00 → Save." | None yet | | **4. Execution** | Thumb taps the sequence on screen. | Physical taps | | **5. Perception** | Eye sees "Alarm set for 6:00 AM" on screen. | Visual feedback | | **6. Interpretation** | "The system understood my input." | Cognitive decoding | | **7. Evaluation** | "Yes, my goal is met — I will wake at 6:00." | Goal match confirmed | This loop typically takes an expert user **2–4 seconds**. --- #### Part (b) — Slips vs. Mistakes (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Slips** are *execution* errors. The intention is correct, but the motor action deviates. - *Example*: Intending to tap "6:00" but tapping "6:05" on the alarm screen. - *Stage of failure*: Stage 3 or 4. - *Remedy*: Confirmation dialogs, undo, larger touch targets. **Mistakes** are *intention* errors. The user's mental model is wrong, so the plan itself is flawed. - *Example*: Believing the bell icon on a phone means "Set Alarm" when it actually means "Silent Mode," and tapping it expecting an alarm to be set. - *Stage of failure*: Stage 1 or 2. - *Remedy*: Better mental models, clearer conceptual models, onboarding tutorials. **Key Difference**: Slips occur *despite* a correct intention; mistakes occur *because of* an incorrect intention. Slips are common in routine tasks under distraction; mistakes occur in novel or ambiguous situations. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: When asked to "illustrate with an example," students often provide only 2–3 stages instead of all seven. Always trace the **full seven stages** in a tabular or numbered format. Incomplete traces lose **3–4 marks** routinely. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Psychology of Everyday Actions** - ✅ **Core Model**: Donald Norman's **Seven Stages of Action** (Goal → Intention → Specification → Execution → Perception → Interpretation → Evaluation). - ✅ **Three Phases**: Goal Formation, Execution, Evaluation. - ✅ **Gulf of Execution**: Intention → Action. Bridge with **affordances, mappings, constraints, standards**. - ✅ **Gulf of Evaluation**: Perception → Goal. Bridge with **feedback, signifiers, visibility**. - ✅ **Gulf of Experience**: Long-term expectation gap. Bridge with **consistency and predictability**. - ✅ **Slip**: Action error (Stages 3–4). Correct intention, wrong execution. - ✅ **Mistake**: Intention error (Stages 1–2). Wrong goal or plan. - ✅ **Key Principles**: **Affordance, Mapping, Feedback, Signifier, Constraint, Visibility**. - ✅ **Action Cycle Time**: Expert users complete one cycle in **~1.5–3 seconds** on a well-designed interface. - ✅ **Design Philosophy (Norman)**: Good design makes the user **think less and act more naturally**, minimizing the gulfs. - ✅ **Exam Tip**: Always **name the stage number** when discussing an error. Always **name the specific design principle** when proposing a remedy. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Board Note**: In the 14-mark questions, the **structure of your answer matters as much as the content**. Use headings, numbered lists, and (if possible) a small Mermaid-style or table-based diagram. Examiners allocate marks for *organization and clarity* in addition to factual correctness. A well-structured 7-mark answer with a clear example can often score higher than a rambling, disorganized 14-mark attempt.

topics.title: Reasoning and Problem Solving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT614 - INTRODUCTION TO HUMAN MACHINE INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reasoning and Problem Solving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reasoning** is the **higher-order cognitive process** by which a person consciously draws conclusions, makes predictions, or constructs explanations from premises, evidence, experiences, or beliefs, using logical, analytical, or intuitive mechanisms.

In the context of **Human–Computer Interaction (HCI)**, reasoning is the internal mental process users employ to understand system behaviour, predict outcomes, and make decisions when interacting with an interface. It is broadly classified into three primary logical forms studied in cognitive psychology:

- **Deductive Reasoning** – Drawing logically certain conclusions from a set of general premises. *Example:* "All admin accounts require passwords → Sumi is admin → Sumi needs a password."
- **Inductive Reasoning** – Drawing probable conclusions from specific observations or patterns. *Example:* "This app crashes twice when network is low → Network is the likely cause."
- **Abductive Reasoning** – Inferring the *best* explanation for an observed set of facts (hypothesis formation). *Example:* "The screen is black → The monitor could be unplugged, broken, or in sleep mode."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Solving** is the **goal-directed cognitive activity** in which a person moves from an initial state (the problem) to a desired goal state (the solution) by overcoming a barrier or constraint, often using reasoning, search, and learned strategies.

**Newell and Simon (1972)** formally defined problem solving as the search through a **Problem Space** — the internal representation of all possible states and the operators that transition between them. This framework is foundational to designing intuitive, learnable, and error-tolerant user interfaces.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Detective at the Crime Scene**

Imagine a detective at a crime scene:

- **Reasoning** is the detective's *mental process* of forming theories from the clues (footprints, fingerprints, motives). Each new clue triggers either **deductive logic** (rigid rules of evidence), **inductive logic** (patterns seen in past cases), or **abduction** (the best-fit story).
- **Problem Solving** is the detective's *action plan* to reach the case-closure goal: gather evidence, interrogate suspects, eliminate possibilities — navigating a **problem space** of suspects, leads, and routes.

In HCI, the user is the detective, the **interface** is the crime scene, and the **system's behaviour** is the evidence. A well-designed interface provides clear *cues* (footprints) that guide the user's reasoning, and *affordances* (tools) that reduce the search effort during problem solving.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Cognitive Principles (Highlighted)

The following cognitive metrics are central to evaluating reasoning and problem-solving performance in HCI design:

- **Mental Model Accuracy** — Degree to which the user's internal representation matches the system's actual model. Valid range: **0 (incorrect) to 1 (perfect match)**.
- **Cognitive Load** — Working-memory demand imposed by a task; measured via **NASA-TLX** (Task Load Index).
- **Problem-Space Search Time (T_s)** — Total time taken to traverse from initial to goal state.
- **Error Rate (E_r)** — Number of reasoning mistakes per 100 interactions; target for novice users is **< 5%** in well-designed systems.
- **Number of Operators Used** — Distinct actions invoked during problem resolution; efficiency benchmark: **< 1.5x the optimal path length** for expert users.
- **Gestalt Principles** — Perceptual grouping laws (proximity, similarity, closure, continuity, figure-ground) that **bias user reasoning** in interface comprehension.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Three Pillars of Reasoning — Structured Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Deductive Reasoning
- **Premise → Conclusion** structure: conclusions are *guaranteed* if premises are true.
- Operates on **validity**, not truth of content.
- Example syllogism:
  - Premise 1: All notifications can be dismissed.
  - Premise 2: This banner is a notification.
  - Conclusion: Therefore, this banner can be dismissed.
- **HCI implication:** System rules, menu hierarchies, and logic-based help-systems depend on deductive consistency. A *deductive inconsistency* (e.g., a button that should be clickable but is greyed-out) **breaks user trust instantly**.

#### 2.1.2 Inductive Reasoning
- **Observation → Generalization**: conclusions are *probable*, not certain.
- **How it works:**
  - Sample data is collected.
  - Patterns are extracted.
  - A rule or hypothesis is proposed.
- **HCI implication:** Recommendation engines, autocomplete, and predictive text rely on inductive inference from user behaviour logs.

#### 2.1.3 Abductive Reasoning
- **Evidence → Best Explanation**: highly used in **diagnostic and troubleshooting** tasks.
- **HCI implication:** Error messages, FAQs, and troubleshooting wizards support abductive reasoning by offering the *most likely cause first* (e.g., "No internet? Check Wi-Fi connection").

---

### 2.2 Problem-Solving Strategies (Bulleted Logic Steps)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Strategies (Köhler, Duncker, Newell & Simon framework):**

1. **Algorithmic Strategy** — Exhaustive step-by-step procedure guaranteed to find the solution.
   - Pros: Reliable.
   - Cons: Often too slow or impractical for large problem spaces.
2. **Heuristic Strategy** — Rule-of-thumb shortcuts (e.g., *means-ends analysis*, *hill-climbing*, *working backwards*).
   - Pros: Fast, practical.
   - Cons: May produce suboptimal or incorrect solutions.
3. **Trial and Error** — Random attempts until success; common in novice behaviour.
4. **Insight / Gestalt Reorganisation** — Sudden restructuring of the problem; *the "aha!" moment*.
5. **Analogy Transfer** — Mapping a known solution from a similar prior problem to the current one.
6. **Sub-Goal Decomposition** — Breaking a complex problem into smaller, manageable sub-problems.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises every key concept, metric, and decision rule required for KTU 2024 evaluation. Note: vertical pipes `|` inside table cells have been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown syntax.

| **Concept** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Definition / Boundary Condition** | **Units / Notes** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Mental Model Accuracy | $M_a = \dfrac{\text{Matches}}{\text{Total System Features}}$ | $0 \le M_a \le 1$; closer to 1 indicates accurate model | Dimensionless ratio |
| Cognitive Load | $C_L = W_m + E_m + S_m$ | Working memory ($\vert W_m \vert$), Effort ($\vert E_m \vert$), Stress ($\vert S_m \vert$) — NASA-TLX sub-scales | Score 0–100 |
| Problem-Space Search Time | $T_s = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta t_i$ | Sum of transition times between $n$ states | Seconds (s) |
| Error Rate | $E_r = \dfrac{\text{Reasoning Errors}}{\text{Interactions}} \times 100$ | Acceptable target $\vert E_r \vert \le 5\%$ for novices | Percent (%) |
| Path Optimality | $P_o = \dfrac{L_{\text{optimal}}}{L_{\text{actual}}}$ | $P_o = 1$ ideal; $P_o < 1$ indicates sub-optimal search | Dimensionless |
| Gestalt Closure | $G_c = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Unclosed Gaps}}{\text{Total Segments}}$ | $0 \le G_c \le 1$ | Dimensionless |
| Deductive Validity | $V_d = 1 \iff (\text{Premises True} \rightarrow \text{Conclusion True})$ | Boolean: 1 = valid, 0 = invalid | Boolean |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Application of Reasoning & Problem Solving Theory** |
| :--- | :--- |
| **UX/UI Design** | Designing error-tolerant flows, predictable navigation, intelligent defaults. |
| **AI/ML Systems** | Rule-based expert systems (deduction), Bayesian inference (induction), diagnostic engines (abduction). |
| **Aviation & Medical Devices** | Checklist-based algorithms + heuristic warnings to reduce *human error* (e.g., Reason's Swiss-Cheese Model). |
| **Educational Technology** | Adaptive learning platforms use problem-space tracking to personalise instruction. |
| **Cybersecurity** | Threat modelling and intrusion detection depend on *abductive* inference from anomalous logs. |
| **Assistive Technology** | Predictable interfaces reduce cognitive load for users with cognitive disabilities. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Newell & Simon's Problem-Space Model (1972)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the formal structure of the *Problem Space* and show how a user's *operator set* transforms the initial state into a goal state.

#### Step 1 — Define the Problem Space (P)

The Problem Space is formally the 4-tuple:

$$
P = (S, s_0, s_g, O)
$$

where:

- $S$ = the finite set of all reachable *states*.
- $s_0 \in S$ = the **initial state** (where the user starts).
- $s_g \in S$ = the **goal state** (the desired outcome).
- $O$ = the set of *operators* $o_i : S \rightarrow S$ that transition one state to another.

#### Step 2 — Define an Operator

An operator $o_i$ maps a current state to a new state:

$$
o_i(s_k) = s_{k+1} \quad \text{where} \quad s_{k+1} \in S
$$

Each operator has:
- **Preconditions** (what must be true to apply it).
- **Effects** (how it changes the state).

#### Step 3 — Define the Search Path

A user navigates the problem space via a sequence of operators:

$$
P_{\text{path}} = (s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_g)
$$

The total path length is:

$$
L = \sum_{i=0}^{g-1} \mathbb{1}\big[o_i \text{ applied at step } i\big]
$$

where $\mathbb{1}$ is the indicator function (1 if the operator is applied, 0 otherwise).

#### Step 4 — Define Path Optimality

If $L_{\text{optimal}}$ is the shortest possible path, then the path optimality metric is:

$$
P_o = \dfrac{L_{\text{optimal}}}{L_{\text{actual}}}
$$

By definition:

$$
0 < P_o \le 1
$$

A $P_o = 1$ indicates the user took the *minimum* possible number of steps to reach $s_g$.

#### Step 5 — Worked Example (Email-Attachment Scenario)

Suppose a user wants to attach a file to an email. Let:

- $s_0$ = Email draft, no attachment.
- $s_g$ = Email draft with attachment.
- Operators: $o_1$ = click "Attach", $o_2$ = select file, $o_3$ = confirm.

Then:

$$
P_{\text{path}} = (s_0, o_1(s_0), o_2(o_1(s_0)), o_3(o_2(o_1(s_0))) = s_g)
$$

$$
L_{\text{actual}} = 3 \quad \text{and} \quad L_{\text{optimal}} = 3 \implies P_o = \dfrac{3}{3} = 1.0
$$

A novice might add extra states (e.g., opening Help, browsing menus) → $L_{\text{actual}} = 5$ → $P_o = 0.6$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Error Rate Bound for Safe Interface Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Establish the maximum allowable user reasoning error rate for safety-critical systems (medical, aviation, nuclear).

A conservative design constraint requires:

$$
E_r \le 5\% \quad \text{(novice tolerance)}
$$

Given the error-rate formula:

$$
E_r = \dfrac{E_n}{I_n} \times 100
$$

where $E_n$ = number of errors, $I_n$ = number of interactions.

**Solve for the maximum allowable errors over 1000 interactions:**

$$
E_n \le \dfrac{5}{100} \times 1000 = 50 \text{ errors}
$$

Thus, the **safety bound** is:

$$
E_n^{\text{max}} = \left\lfloor 0.05 \times I_n \right\rfloor
$$

For aviation cockpits, this threshold is even stricter:

$$
E_r^{\text{aviation}} \le 0.001 \implies E_n \le 1 \text{ per 1000 interactions}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Reasoning Modes

| **Dimension** | **Deductive** | **Inductive** | **Abductive** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Logical direction | General → Specific | Specific → General | Effect → Best Cause |
| Certainty of conclusion | Certain (if premises true) | Probable | Plausible / best guess |
| Cognitive demand | Low–Medium | Medium | High |
| Error type | Formal fallacy | Overgeneralisation | Wrong hypothesis |
| HCI example | Menu hierarchy rules | Auto-suggest predictions | Troubleshooting wizard |
| Typical user mindset | "I know the rule" | "I've seen this before" | "What is the most likely cause?" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Reasoning Triad

The following block diagram illustrates how the three reasoning modes interact within a user's cognition during HCI tasks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[User receives interface cue] --> nodeB{Reasoning mode invoked?}
    nodeB -- General rules known --> nodeC[Deductive reasoning]
    nodeB -- Pattern recognised --> nodeD[Inductive reasoning]
    nodeB -- Anomaly observed --> nodeE[Abductive reasoning]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Logical conclusion]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Probable prediction]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Best-fit hypothesis]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Action selected]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Operator applied to system]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[New state in problem space]
    nodeK --> nodeB
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **central decision diamond** (`nodeB`) routes the user's cognition into one of three reasoning channels based on context.
- All three paths converge on **action selection** (`nodeI`), which then **transforms the problem space** (`nodeK`) — closing the cognitive loop.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Newell & Simon's Problem-Space Search Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0node((Initial state s0)) --> op1[Operator o1]
    op1 --> s1((State s1))
    s1 --> op2[Operator o2]
    op2 --> s2((State s2))
    s2 --> op3[Operator o3]
    op3 --> s3((State s3))
    s3 --> decide{Goal state sg reached?}
    decide -- Yes --> goalNode((Goal state sg))
    decide -- No --> op4[Backtrack / apply heuristic]
    op4 --> s2
```

**Key insight for HCI designers:** A well-designed interface **shrinks the search space** by making valid operators *visually discoverable*, and removes *dead-end states* (e.g., broken links) so the user rarely has to backtrack.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Architecture of Reasoning in HCI

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph perceptionLayer[PERCEPTION LAYER]
        p1[Visual cues]
        p2[Auditory cues]
        p3[Haptic feedback]
    end
    subgraph mentalModelLayer[MENTAL MODEL LAYER]
        m1[User knowledge]
        m2[Schema activation]
        m3[Expectation generation]
    end
    subgraph reasoningLayer[REASONING LAYER]
        r1[Deduction]
        r2[Induction]
        r3[Abduction]
    end
    subgraph problemSolvingLayer[PROBLEM SOLVING LAYER]
        ps1[Problem space search]
        ps2[Strategy selection]
        ps3[Operator execution]
    end
    p1 --> m1
    p2 --> m1
    p3 --> m1
    m1 --> m2 --> m3
    m3 --> r1
    m3 --> r2
    m3 --> r3
    r1 --> ps1
    r2 --> ps1
    r3 --> ps1
    ps1 --> ps2 --> ps3
    ps3 --> perceptionLayer
```

**Architectural insights:**

- The **perception layer** feeds raw sensory data into the user's **mental model** (their internal representation of the system).
- The **reasoning layer** transforms the mental model into inferred conclusions via the three logical modes.
- The **problem-solving layer** operationalises those conclusions into concrete actions that loop back to the perception layer — closing the **HCI interaction loop**.
- **Design implication:** A failure in *any* layer (poor cue → wrong model → faulty reasoning → wrong action) propagates to the user and degrades UX.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Differentiate between **deductive** and **inductive reasoning** with one HCI example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Deductive Reasoning** | **Inductive Reasoning** |
| :--- | :--- |
| Moves from *general rule* to *specific conclusion*; certainty guaranteed if premises are valid. | Moves from *specific observations* to *generalised rule*; conclusion is probable, not certain. |
| **HCI Example:** "All dropdowns expand on click" → therefore the "Settings" dropdown will expand on click. | **HCI Example:** The app lags on slow networks in 3 of 3 trials → user concludes "network speed is the bottleneck." |
| Validity is *formal*; content truth is separate. | Strength depends on *sample size* and *representativeness*. |

*(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark direction-of-logic, 1 mark HCI example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

Define the term **"Problem Space"** as per **Newell and Simon (1972)** and list its four components.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Problem Space** is the internal cognitive representation of all possible states a problem can occupy and all the transitions between them.

**Four components:**

1. $S$ — the set of all possible *states*.
2. $s_0$ — the *initial state* where the user begins.
3. $s_g$ — the *goal state* representing the desired solution.
4. $O$ — the set of *operators* that transform one state into another.

*(Award: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for naming all four components, 1 mark for indicating the initial → goal transition.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE Question (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **three types of reasoning** (deductive, inductive, abductive) with **two real-world HCI examples each**. Discuss how each reasoning mode is supported (or hindered) by interface design decisions.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using **Newell and Simon's Problem-Space framework**, derive the **path optimality metric** $P_o$. Apply it to a user trying to *upload a profile picture* in a mobile app where the optimal sequence requires **3 steps** but the user actually performed **5 steps**, including one backtrack. Comment on what design changes could improve $P_o$ toward 1.0.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Types of Reasoning with HCI Examples [7 marks]**

1. **Deductive Reasoning (2 marks):** Moves from *general rule → specific conclusion*. The conclusion is *logically certain* if the premise is true.
   - *Example 1:* A user reasons, *"Every 'Save' button saves the document → therefore, clicking this green disk icon will save my document."*
   - *Example 2:* A user deduces, *"I am logged in as Admin → therefore I have access to the Admin panel."*
   - *Design support:* Consistency in iconography (e.g., a single floppy-disk icon always meaning "Save") reinforces deductive certainty. *Design hinderance:* Changing icon meanings across screens destroys deductive validity.

2. **Inductive Reasoning (2 marks):** Moves from *specific instances → generalised rule*. The conclusion is *probable*.
   - *Example 1:* After two instances of the app crashing when many tabs are open, the user infers, *"Opening many tabs causes crashes."*
   - *Example 2:* The user notices that pressing 'Enter' always submits forms → predicts it will submit *this* form too.
   - *Design support:* Visible patterns (e.g., a consistent layout) help users form accurate inductive rules. *Design hinderance:* Hidden state changes (e.g., a button silently becoming disabled) break inductive expectations.

3. **Abductive Reasoning (2 marks):** Infers the *best explanation* for an observed phenomenon. High cognitive demand; central to troubleshooting.
   - *Example 1:* Login fails → user hypothesises, *"Wrong password, locked account, or server down?"* → tries resetting the password.
   - *Example 2:* Map shows wrong location → user thinks, *"GPS off, or wrong address, or app bug?"*
   - *Design support:* A diagnostic error message that lists *the top 3 most likely causes* (ranked by probability) accelerates abductive reasoning. *Design hinderance:* A generic "Something went wrong" message leaves the user stuck.

   *Concluding remark (1 mark):* A truly *user-centred* design supports *all three* modes: **deductive** (consistent rules), **inductive** (learnable patterns), and **abductive** (helpful diagnostics).

---

**(b) Path Optimality Derivation & Application [7 marks]**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from Newell & Simon's problem-space tuple $P = (S, s_0, s_g, O)$, a user's path is a state sequence:

$$
P_{\text{path}} = (s_0, s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_g)
$$

Let $L_{\text{actual}}$ be the number of state transitions the user actually performed, and $L_{\text{optimal}}$ be the *shortest* possible sequence. The path optimality metric is defined as:

$$
P_o = \dfrac{L_{\text{optimal}}}{L_{\text{actual}}}
$$

By definition:

$$
0 < P_o \le 1
$$

A value of $P_o = 1$ indicates optimal navigation; lower values indicate sub-optimal search.

**Application (2 marks):**

Given $L_{\text{optimal}} = 3$ and $L_{\text{actual}} = 5$:

$$
P_o = \dfrac{3}{5} = 0.6
$$

Interpretation: The user is **60% efficient** — they took **66.7% more steps** than necessary.

**Design Recommendations to raise $P_o$ toward 1.0 (1 mark):**

1. **Add visual affordances** to the "Upload" button (e.g., a clear icon) so the user does not wander.
2. **Reduce backtracking** by providing inline previews of the image *before* final submission.
3. **Guide with progressive disclosure** — show only the necessary steps in sequence.
4. **Implement contextual tooltips** explaining what each step does, reducing exploratory clicks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define **problem solving** in the context of HCI. Explain any **four problem-solving strategies** with one HCI example each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] What is a **mental model**? Discuss the **Norman's Gulf of Execution** and **Gulf of Evaluation** with the help of a diagram. Show how poor mental-model accuracy ($M_a = 0.4$) leads to user errors and how it can be improved through interface design.

---

**Model Solution (Sketch — Full key expected by KTU examiners):**

**(a)** *Problem solving* is a goal-directed cognitive activity moving from an initial state to a goal state using reasoning, search, and operators (Newell & Simon). Four strategies:

1. **Algorithmic** (e.g., installation wizard that automates every step).
2. **Heuristic — Means–Ends Analysis** (e.g., reducing the difference between current and target font size by trial).
3. **Working Backwards** (e.g., from desired invoice format → required fields).
4. **Analogy Transfer** (e.g., applying knowledge from Word to Pages).

**(b)** *Mental Model:* the user's internal, simplified representation of how a system works.
- **Gulf of Execution:** the gap between *user's intention* and *actions the system allows*.
- **Gulf of Evaluation:** the gap between *system's actual state* and *user's perception of it*.
- $M_a = 0.4$ means only 40% of the system's features are mapped correctly in the user's mind → high error probability.
- Improvement: **affordances, feedback, mapping, constraints, and visible system status** (Norman's design principles).

*(A full diagram and derivation should accompany this answer — students are advised to redraw the four-quadrant Norman's action cycle.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where students lose marks on Reasoning & Problem-Solving questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing induction with deduction.** Many students write "deduction" when they actually mean "generalisation from examples." This is a guaranteed **−1 to −2 mark penalty**.
> 2. **Omitting the HCI context.** A purely psychological answer without linking to interface design or user behaviour is marked *out-of-syllabus*. Always anchor the answer to HCI.
> 3. **Forgetting to define $s_0$ and $s_g$.** When asked about the Problem Space, students often mention $S$ and $O$ but skip the *initial* and *goal* states. Both are **mandatory** in the 4-tuple definition.
> 4. **Skipping the derivation of $P_o$.** A direct formula answer is worth 1 mark; the full derivation is worth up to 4 marks. **Show every step.**
> 5. **No diagram in Part B (b) questions.** KTU examiners *expect* a diagram for Gulf of Execution/Evaluation. Missing diagram = **−2 marks**.
> 6. **Mixing up Mental Model and Conceptual Model.** Mental Model = *in the user's head*; Conceptual Model = *the designer's intent*. Examiners are strict about this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for "Reasoning and Problem Solving" (KTU Module 1):**

- ✅ **Reasoning** is a higher-order cognitive process; it has **three modes**: *Deductive, Inductive, Abductive*.
- ✅ **Deduction** = *certain* (general → specific). **Induction** = *probable* (specific → general). **Abduction** = *best guess* (effect → cause).
- ✅ **Problem Solving** is a *goal-directed* search from an initial state $s_0$ to a goal state $s_g$ using **operators** $O$.
- ✅ **Problem Space** $P = (S, s_0, s_g, O)$ — Newell & Simon's foundational 4-tuple.
- ✅ **Key strategies:** *Algorithmic, Heuristic (means–ends, hill-climbing, working-backwards), Trial-and-Error, Insight, Analogy Transfer, Sub-Goal Decomposition*.
- ✅ **Path Optimality:** $P_o = \dfrac{L_{\text{optimal}}}{L_{\text{actual}}}$, where $0 < P_o \le 1$.
- ✅ **Error Rate:** $E_r = \dfrac{E_n}{I_n} \times 100$; safety threshold $E_r \le 5\%$.
- ✅ **Mental Model Accuracy:** $M_a = \dfrac{\text{Matches}}{\text{Total Features}}$; target $M_a \rightarrow 1$.
- ✅ **Gestalt Principles** (proximity, similarity, closure, continuity, figure-ground) **bias perceptual reasoning** in interface design.
- ✅ **Norman's Gulf of Execution & Gulf of Evaluation** are central to designing for *reasoning-friendly* interfaces.
- ✅ **HCI design principle:** *Shrink the problem space* by making operators discoverable and removing dead-end states.
- ✅ **Expert vs. Novice:** Experts use *forward reasoning* + *chunked knowledge*; novices use *backward reasoning* + *trial-and-error* — design for the **novice** while still rewarding the **expert** (progressive disclosure).
- ✅ **Common exam traps:** (i) confusing deduction with induction, (ii) forgetting the HCI context, (iii) omitting diagrams, (iv) skipping derivations, (v) confusing *mental model* with *conceptual model*.

> *End of Topic Notes — Reasoning and Problem Solving (Module 1, OECPT614, KTU 2024 Scheme).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Computer: Devices, Memory, Processing and Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT614 - INTRODUCTION TO HUMAN MACHINE INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Computer: Devices, Memory, Processing and Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Computer: A Formal Definition

A **Computer** is a programmable, electronic, digital device that accepts data (input), processes it under the control of a stored program, produces information (output), and stores both data and program instructions for future use. In the context of **Human–Machine Interaction (HMI)**, the computer is the *machine* half — a layered stack of **devices, memory, processing, and network interfaces** that the human operator must sense, comprehend, and control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1)**
> A computer is a binary (base-2) information-processing system composed of four tightly coupled subsystems: **Input Devices**, **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Memory (Primary & Secondary)**, and **Output Devices**, all interconnected through **buses** and **networks**. The human interacts with this stack through the **I/O boundary** — the design of which is the central concern of HMI.

The four functional units follow **John von Neumann's Stored-Program Architecture (1945)**:

1. **Input Unit** — translates human intent (keystrokes, gestures, voice) into machine-readable binary.
2. **Memory Unit** — stores programs + data, hierarchically tiered from registers to cloud.
3. **Processing Unit (CPU)** — performs arithmetic, logic, and control operations.
4. **Output Unit** — translates binary results back into human-perceivable form (pixels, sound, haptics).

## 1.2 Devices — Input, Output, and Hybrid

### 1.2.1 Input Devices
Hardware through which *humans inject* signals into the machine.

| Category | Examples | HMI Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Textual** | Keyboard, keypad | Command-line interfaces |
| **Pointing** | Mouse, trackball, touchpad | GUI direct manipulation |
| **Touch/Surface** | Resistive/Capacitive touchscreen | Mobile & kiosk HMI |
| **Vision-based** | Webcam, IR/Depth camera, eye-tracker | Gaze-based, gesture-based HMI |
| **Audio** | Microphone, microphone array | Voice UIs (VUI), speech recognition |
| **Motion/Position** | Accelerometer, gyroscope, GPS, IMU | Wearable, AR/VR, automotive HMI |
| **Physiological** | EEG, ECG, GSR, fNIRS sensors | Brain–Computer Interfaces (BCI), affective computing |
| **Haptic** | Data glove, force feedback joystick | Immersive HMI |

### 1.2.2 Output Devices
Hardware that presents *machine results* to the human sensory system.

- **Visual:** Monitor (LCD, OLED, MicroLED), projector, head-mounted display (HMD), e-ink, Braille display.
- **Auditory:** Speaker, headphone, bone-conduction transducer, spatial audio array.
- **Haptic:** Vibration motor (LRA/ERM), ultrasonic mid-air haptics, exoskeleton, force-feedback arm.
- **Olfactory/Gustatory:** Experimental scent & taste synthesizers (emerging HMI research).

### 1.2.3 Hybrid I/O Devices
Devices that perform both input and output, e.g., **touchscreen**, **smartwatch face**, **VR controller with haptic triggers**, **AR glasses with see-through display + eye camera**.

> [!NOTE]
> **HMI Insight — The I/O Boundary is a Sensory Channel**
> In HMI, every device is mapped to a **human sensory modality** (vision, audition, touch, proprioception, olfaction). Nielsen's *visibility of system status* and Shneiderman's *direct manipulation* both fundamentally depend on the device layer.

## 1.3 Memory — The Computer's Workspace

**Memory** is the component that retains **data** and **instructions** in binary form. It is classified by *speed*, *volatility*, *capacity*, and *proximity to the CPU*.

### 1.3.1 Primary Memory (CPU-Adjacent, Volatile Unless Noted)
- **Registers** — tiny, fastest, inside the CPU (a few hundred bytes).
- **Cache Memory (L1, L2, L3)** — SRAM, latency in nanoseconds, **typical size: 32 KB – 64 MB**.
- **Main Memory (RAM)** — DRAM, latency in tens of nanoseconds, **typical size: 4 GB – 128 GB** on consumer devices.

### 1.3.2 Secondary Memory (Non-Volatile, Bulk)
- **HDD (Hard Disk Drive)** — magnetic platters, **typical capacity 500 GB – 20 TB**, access time ~5–10 ms.
- **SSD (Solid State Drive)** — NAND flash, **typical capacity 256 GB – 8 TB**, access time ~0.05–0.1 ms.
- **Optical** — CD, DVD, Blu-ray (archival).
- **USB / Flash drive** — portable NAND.

### 1.3.3 Tertiary & Network-Attached Storage
- **Magnetic tape** (enterprise backup), **Cloud storage** (S3, Drive, OneDrive), **NAS / SAN**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Volatility Quick Rule**
> *Powered → RAM holds bits. Power cut → RAM forgets; SSD/HDD/cloud remember.* This is the central trade-off in HMI responsiveness design — you cannot have a 0 ms "undo" history in volatile RAM alone.

## 1.4 Processing — The CPU & Beyond

**Processing** is the act of transforming input data into output information through **arithmetic, logic, and control** operations executed by silicon.

- **CPU (Central Processing Unit)** — general-purpose, sequential & limited-parallel, measured in **GHz (10⁹ cycles/sec)**.
- **GPU (Graphics Processing Unit)** — massively parallel (thousands of cores), optimized for matrix/matrix math.
- **TPU / NPU** — Tensor / Neural Processing Units for AI inference.
- **DSP (Digital Signal Processor)** — audio/video codecs, sensor fusion.
- **MCU / SoC** — microcontrollers in embedded HMI (smartwatch, IoT sensor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clock Speed Definition**
> **Clock speed (f)** = number of cycles the CPU completes per second, in **Hertz (Hz)**. Modern consumer CPUs run at **2.0 – 5.8 GHz**. Cycle time = **1 / f** (e.g., 1 / 3 GHz ≈ 0.333 ns).

## 1.5 Networks — The Connected Computer

A **computer network** is a set of interconnected computing devices that exchange data using shared **protocols** (TCP/IP, HTTP, BLE, Wi-Fi).

| Scale | Type | Typical Range | HMI Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Personal** | PAN (Personal Area Network) | < 10 m | Smartwatch ↔ phone (BLE) |
| **Local** | LAN (Local Area Network) | Building / campus | Office workstation, lab HMI rig |
| **Campus/City** | CAN / MAN | 1 – 50 km | University intranet |
| **Wide** | WAN (Inter-net) | Global | Cloud-based HMI services |
| **Body** | BAN (Body Area Network) | On-body | Wearable health monitors |
| **Things** | IoT / M2M | Variable | Smart home, industrial HMI |

## 1.6 Intuitive Overview — The "Computer = Human Body" Analogy

To a first-year HMI student, the four subsystems map cleanly to a human being:

- **Devices (I/O)** = **Senses & Limbs** (eyes, ears, hands, voice box) — the *boundary* with the world.
- **Memory** = **Brain's recall** — short-term (RAM) vs long-term (HDD/SSD); the more you rehearse, the more it moves from "short-term" to "long-term" (cache lines).
- **Processing** = **Brain's reasoning** — the "thinking" happens here.
- **Network** = **Language & Telephone** — how you talk to *other* bodies.

> [!TIP]
> **HMI Takeaway for Module 1**
> Every principle you learn later — *affordances, feedback, Fitts's Law, Hick's Law* — operates *on top of* this hardware stack. A touchscreen's *latency* is a memory + processing + network metric. A push notification's *snappiness* is a network + processing metric. Master the substrate first.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of a Modern Computer (HMI View)

We can model the computer as **six logical layers**, each of which an HMI designer can target:

1. **Physical Layer** — cables, radio waves, pin voltages, fiber.
2. **Hardware Layer** — transistors, gates, registers, ALU, buses.
3. **Firmware / Microcode Layer** — BIOS / UEFI, device drivers.
4. **Operating System Layer** — process scheduling, virtual memory, I/O multiplexing.
5. **Application / Middleware Layer** — HMI software (Qt, Android UI, Web front-end).
6. **User Interface Layer** — the actual pixels, sounds, haptics the human perceives.

> [!NOTE]
> HMI students will *primarily* design at layers 5–6, but cannot debug a "laggy" interface without understanding layers 1–4.

## 2.2 Memory Hierarchy — The Speed–Cost–Capacity Triangle

The single most important diagram in this topic. Memory is arranged in a **pyramid** because of three competing constraints:

- **Speed** ↑ as we go up the pyramid (closer to CPU).
- **Cost per byte** ↑ as we go up the pyramid.
- **Capacity** ↓ as we go up the pyramid.

The system exploits **locality of reference** (programs touch a small set of addresses repeatedly in a short time) to make this pyramid behave as if it were "the size of the bottom, the speed of the top."

**Locality of Reference** has two flavors:
- **Temporal Locality** — if address X is accessed now, it is likely to be accessed again soon (e.g., loop counter).
- **Spatial Locality** — if address X is accessed, X+1, X+2 … are likely to follow (e.g., scanning an array).

## 2.3 CPU Internals — ALU, CU, Registers, Buses

The CPU has three sub-blocks:

- **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** — performs +, −, ×, ÷, AND, OR, NOT, XOR, shifts, comparisons.
- **CU (Control Unit)** — decodes instructions, generates control signals, drives the bus.
- **Register File** — fastest storage; holds operands, instruction pointer, flags.

These talk to memory and devices via three bus families:
- **Data Bus** — carries the actual values.
- **Address Bus** — carries *where* to read/write.
- **Control Bus** — carries *read/write/IRQ/clock* signals.

A single **machine cycle** (fetch → decode → execute → store) takes *1 / f* seconds.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

The following table is the canonical formula set KTU examiners use for "calculate the …" sub-parts on this topic. Memorize the unit conversions first; the rest follow.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Storage size from bit-count | $N = 2^n$ bits, capacity = $N/8$ bytes | $n$ = number of address lines / bit-width | Bytes |
| 2 | Memory addressing range | $\text{Range} = 2^n$ distinct locations | $n$ = address bus width | Locations |
| 3 | CPU cycle time | $T_c = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | $f$ = clock frequency | Seconds |
| 4 | Instruction execution time | $T_{exec} = \text{CPI} \times T_c \times I$ | CPI = cycles per instruction, $I$ = # instructions | Seconds |
| 5 | CPU throughput | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^6}$ | $f$ in Hz | Millions of instructions / sec |
| 6 | Data transfer time | $t_{xfer} = \dfrac{\text{File size (bits)}}{\text{Bandwidth (bps)}}$ | Bandwidth in bits per second | Seconds |
| 7 | Bandwidth–Delay Product | $\text{BDP} = B \times \text{RTT}$ | $B$ = bandwidth (bps), RTT = round-trip time (s) | Bits "in flight" |
| 8 | File size conversion | $1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10} \text{ B}$, $1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20} \text{ B}$, $1 \text{ GB} = 2^{30} \text{ B}$ | Binary (IEC) convention | Bytes |
| 9 | Storage size decimal (SI) | $1 \text{ kB} = 10^3 \text{ B}$, $1 \text{ MB} = 10^6 \text{ B}$ | Marketing convention | Bytes |
| 10 | Amdahl's Law (parallel speedup) | $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ | $P$ = parallel fraction, $N$ = processors | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Amdahl's max speedup | $S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \dfrac{1}{1-P}$ | $P$ = parallel fraction | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Disk access time | $T_{disk} = T_{seek} + T_{rot} + T_{transfer}$ | Seek, rotational latency, transfer | Milliseconds |
| 13 | Average rotational latency | $T_{rot} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{60}{\text{RPM}}$ | RPM = revolutions per minute | Seconds |
| 14 | Cache hit rate | $H = \dfrac{\text{Cache hits}}{\text{Total accesses}}$ | $0 \le H \le 1$ | Ratio or % |
| 15 | Effective access time | $EAT = H \cdot T_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot (T_{cache} + T_{mem})$ | $T_{cache}$, $T_{mem}$ in ns | Seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall — Unit Conventions**
> KTU papers may use **KB = 1024 B** (binary) or **kB = 1000 B** (SI). Always read the question. A 1 TB drive marketed as $10^{12}$ bytes reads in Windows as $\approx 931$ GB ($2^{30}$). Show your conversion in the answer or risk a 1-mark deduction.

## 2.5 Network Stack in HMI Context

For HMI, the relevant network concepts are:

- **Latency (ms)** — time for one packet to travel A → B.
- **Jitter (ms)** — variation in latency; critical for real-time UIs.
- **Throughput (Mbps)** — sustained data rate.
- **Bandwidth (Mbps)** — theoretical maximum rate.
- **Packet loss (%)** — fraction of packets dropped.

**Application-layer relevance:**
- **Voice HMI** — needs < 150 ms one-way latency for natural conversation.
- **VR/AR HMI** — needs < 20 ms motion-to-photon latency to avoid simulator sickness.
- **Tactile Internet (5G/6G)** — target 1 ms round-trip for remote surgery HMI.

## 2.6 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering (HMI-specific utility)

- **Device Selection** — A gaze-tracker (1 kHz) vs. a 60 Hz mouse produces different Fitts's Law parameters; you must know the *device sampling rate* to design the right feedback loop.
- **Memory Budgeting** — Mobile HMI apps are constrained to < 200 MB RAM; knowing the memory hierarchy helps you write cache-friendly gesture pipelines.
- **Processing Cores** — Haptic rendering is parallelizable (Amdahl's Law limits); GPU offload vs. CPU is a design choice.
- **Network-Aware UI** — Loading-state design depends on bandwidth-delay product; a 10 Mbps link with 200 ms RTT has 2 Mbit "in flight" — this is why spinners exist.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Clock Frequency to Instruction Execution Time

A KTU classic. We derive the time a CPU takes to execute a given program.

**Given:** clock frequency $f$ (in Hz), number of instructions $I$, and average cycles per instruction $\text{CPI}$.

**Step 1.** Define cycle time $T_c$ as the time for one clock tick.

$$
T_c = \frac{1}{f}
$$

**Step 2.** For one instruction taking CPI cycles, the time is:

$$
T_{inst} = \text{CPI} \times T_c = \frac{\text{CPI}}{f}
$$

**Step 3.** For $I$ instructions (executed sequentially, no pipelining assumed):

$$
T_{total} = I \times T_{inst} = I \times \frac{\text{CPI}}{f}
$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $I$ in terms of $T_{total}$ (useful when given an execution deadline):

$$
I = \frac{T_{total} \times f}{\text{CPI}}
$$

**Step 5.** MIPS (Millions of Instructions Per Second) is a derived unit:

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU pattern):**
A processor runs at **2.5 GHz**, CPI = **2.4**, and executes $I = 10^9$ instructions.

$$
T_{total} = \frac{10^9 \times 2.4}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 0.96 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^9}{2.4 \times 10^6} \approx 1041.67 \text{ MIPS}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic row for Step 1:** "Cycle time is the reciprocal of frequency because one cycle occupies 1/f seconds on a periodic clock."
> **Logic row for Step 3:** "If instructions are sequential, total time scales linearly with instruction count — a first-order approximation we will refine with pipelining in higher semesters."

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Amdahl's Law for HMI Parallel Pipelines

HMI pipelines (e.g., rendering + gesture recognition + haptic synthesis) are increasingly parallel. Amdahl's Law bounds the speedup.

**Given:** fraction $P$ of the program that is parallelizable, run on $N$ processors; the remaining $(1-P)$ is strictly sequential.

**Step 1.** Normalize the original execution time to 1 (dimensionless "work unit"):

$$
T(1) = 1
$$

**Step 2.** The sequential part cannot be parallelized; it still takes time $(1-P)$ on any number of cores:

$$
T_{seq}(N) = (1-P)
$$

**Step 3.** The parallel part divides its work over $N$ processors:

$$
T_{par}(N) = \frac{P}{N}
$$

**Step 4.** Total time on $N$ processors:

$$
T(N) = (1-P) + \frac{P}{N}
$$

**Step 5.** Speedup $S(N)$ is the ratio of old time to new time:

$$
S(N) = \frac{T(1)}{T(N)} = \frac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}
$$

**Step 6.** Take the limit as $N \to \infty$:

$$
S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1-P}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (HMI flavor):**
A HMI render pipeline is **80%** parallel (ray tracing, particle systems) and **20%** sequential (UI event loop, synchronization). We throw **16 GPU cores** at it.

$$
S(16) = \frac{1}{(1-0.8) + \dfrac{0.8}{16}} = \frac{1}{0.2 + 0.05} = \frac{1}{0.25} = 4 \times
$$

$$
S_{\max} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5 \times
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**
> Even with infinite cores, we never exceed **5×** speedup because of the 20% sequential tax. This is the *Amdahl ceiling* — and it's why HMI engineers redesign the sequential portion (e.g., use lock-free queues, batched UI events) before adding cores.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effective Access Time with Cache

**Given:** cache access time $T_{cache}$ (ns), main-memory access time $T_{mem}$ (ns), hit ratio $H$.

**Step 1.** On a hit, total time = $T_{cache}$ (one access).
**Step 2.** On a miss, total time = $T_{cache}$ (cache lookup) + $T_{mem}$ (main-memory fetch).
**Step 3.** Weight by probability:

$$
EAT = H \cdot T_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot (T_{cache} + T_{mem})
$$

**Step 4.** Simplify:

$$
EAT = T_{cache} + (1-H) \cdot T_{mem}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
$T_{cache} = 2$ ns, $T_{mem} = 100$ ns, $H = 0.95$.

$$
EAT = 2 + (1 - 0.95) \times 100 = 2 + 5 = 7 \text{ ns}
$$

A 1% drop in hit rate (H = 0.94) → EAT = **8 ns** (≈ 14% slower). Cache sensitivity is *non-linear*.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Data Transfer Time for HMI File / Stream

**Given:** file size $S$ (in bytes), link bandwidth $B$ (in bits per second).

**Step 1.** Convert bytes to bits: $S_{bits} = S \times 8$.

**Step 2.** Time = work / rate:

$$
t_{xfer} = \frac{S_{bits}}{B} = \frac{8 S}{B}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:** Download a **250 MB** patch over a **100 Mbps** link.

$$
S_{bits} = 250 \times 10^6 \times 8 = 2 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
t_{xfer} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{100 \times 10^6} = 20 \text{ seconds}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Memory Technologies Compared

The single most-asked "compare & contrast" table for KTU Module 1.

| Property | Register | Cache (SRAM) | Main RAM (DRAM) | SSD (NAND Flash) | HDD (Magnetic) | Cloud / Tape |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Inside CPU | On-die / on-package | Motherboard DIMM slot | SATA / NVMe bus | SATA bus | Network / offline |
| **Volatility** | Volatile | Volatile | Volatile | Non-volatile | Non-volatile | Non-volatile |
| **Typical Size** | 64 B – 1 KB | 32 KB – 64 MB | 4 – 128 GB | 256 GB – 8 TB | 500 GB – 20 TB | Petabytes |
| **Access Time** | ~0.3 ns | 1 – 5 ns | 10 – 100 ns | 0.05 – 0.5 ms | 5 – 10 ms | 50 ms – seconds |
| **Cost / GB (approx.)** | $$$$$ | $$$$ | $$ | ¢¢ | ¢ | ¢ |
| **Best For** | ALU operands | Hot loops | Running programs | Apps, OS | Bulk, archival | Disaster recovery |
| **HMI Use** | Pointer math | Gesture state | Active scene | Logs, configs | Video assets | Long-term user data |

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Network Classes for HMI

| Class | Range | Typical Tech | HMI Application | Latency Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PAN** | < 10 m | Bluetooth, NFC | Watch, headset pairing | < 100 ms |
| **LAN** | ≤ 1 km | Ethernet, Wi-Fi 6 | Office HMI rigs, lab | < 10 ms |
| **MAN** | ≤ 50 km | Metro Ethernet, 5G | Campus-scale AR | < 20 ms |
| **WAN / Internet** | Global | Fiber, satellite, 5G | Cloud HMI services | 50 – 200 ms |
| **BAN** | On-body | BLE, conductive fabric | Wearable health | < 50 ms |
| **IoT / M2M** | Variable | LoRa, Zigbee, MQTT | Smart home | < 1 s OK |
| **Tactile Internet** | 5G/6G | URLLC | Remote surgery HMI | < 1 ms |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Computer Architecture (von Neumann Model)

```mermaid
    BUS <--> C1
    BUS <--> M1
    M1 <--> M2
    BUS --> A2
    C1 <--> NET
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

```mermaid
    IN[INPUT DEVICES<br/>Sense and Capture]:::in
    PRO[PROCESSING<br/>CPU GPU NPU]:::cpu
    MEM[MEMORY<br/>State Context History]:::mem
    OUT[OUTPUT DEVICES<br/>Display Speaker Haptic]:::out
    FEED[FEEDBACK LOOP<br/>Latency and Jitter Budget]:::fb

    PRO -.-> FEED
    OUT -.-> FEED
```
