> Where $C_{\text{external}}$ = load handled by artifacts, $C_{\text{offloaded}}$ = load delegated to tools/others.

### 2.1.6 Paradigm 6: Embodied Interaction Paradigm
- **Core Assumption:** Interaction is fundamentally **physical, social, and tangible**; meaning arises from *doing* in the world.
- **Focus:** Tangible interfaces, social computing, phenomenological experience, the *ontological* design of artifacts.
- **Time Frame:** 2000s onward.
- **Key Thinkers:** Paul Dourish (*Where the Action Is*, 2001), Hiroshi Ishii (Tangible Bits).
- **Core Principle:** Computation should engage the *physical world*, not just the screen.

## 2.2 Why These Paradigms Matter — Engineering Utility

| Paradigm | Real-World Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| Human Factors | Cockpit design, automotive dashboards, surgical instrument layout |
| Cognitive | Menu hierarchies, search engines, training software |
| Activity Theory | Enterprise software, CSCW (Computer-Supported Cooperative Work) tools |
| Situated Action | Mobile apps, field-worker tools, emergency response UIs |
| Distributed Cognition | Air traffic control, hospital information systems, smart rooms |
| Embodied Interaction | AR/VR, IoT, tangible computing, gestural interfaces |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Key Formulas Across Paradigms**

| Paradigm | Key Formula / Model | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Human Factors | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(\frac{D}{W}+1)$ (Fitts' Law) | $D$ = distance, $W$ = width | $T$ in seconds |
| Human Factors | $ID = \log_2(\frac{D}{W}+1)$ (Index of Difficulty) | $D, W$ | bits |
| Cognitive | $RT = t_p + t_c + t_m$ (MHP) | $t_p, t_c, t_m$ | milliseconds |
| Cognitive | $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ (Hick's Law information) | $p_i$ = prob. of choice $i$ | bits |
| Activity Theory | $A = S \rightarrow O$ mediated by tools/rules/community | Symbolic | — |
| Distributed Cognition | $C_{total} = C_{internal} + C_{external} + C_{represented} - C_{offloaded}$ | All load terms | abstract units |
| Embodied | No closed formula; principle of *tangible engagement* | Qualitative | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** In 14-mark KTU questions, **always draw the Activity Theory triangle** (Section 3) and **cite the paradigm by name** (e.g., "Following Suchman's Situated Action perspective...") to fetch full marks.

## 2.4 Paradigm-to-Method Mapping Table (Frequently Tested)

| Paradigm | Preferred Research Method | Preferred Design Method |
|---|---|---|
| Human Factors | Controlled experiment | Ergonomic standards (e.g., ANSI/HFES) |
| Cognitive | GOMS, KLM, protocol analysis | Information architecture |
| Activity Theory | Ethnography, activity checklist | Activity-centered design |
| Situated Action | Video ethnography, field studies | Contextual design, prototyping |
| Distributed Cognition | Cognitive systems analysis | Joint cognitive systems design |
| Embodied Interaction | Phenomenological interview | Sketching, low-fidelity physical prototypes |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1: Derivation of the Standard Fitts' Law Form

The **Index of Difficulty (ID)** is the information-theoretic difficulty of pointing at a target. We derive it from Shannon's channel capacity theorem.

**Step 1 — Define target width and amplitude.**
Let the target have width $W$ and be at distance $D$ from the pointer.

**Step 2 — Express the spatial tolerance.**
The user must land within $\pm W/2$ of the target centre, giving a tolerance region of size $W$.

**Step 3 — Use Shannon's information theorem.**
Movement time is proportional to the information content of the pointing task. For a one-dimensional movement with tolerance $W$ and amplitude $D$:

$$ID = \log_2\left(\frac{\text{Amplitude}}{\text{Tolerance}} + 1\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

> The "1" inside the logarithm avoids negative IDs for $W > D$.

**Step 4 — Relate movement time to ID linearly (Fitts, 1954).**
Empirically, $T$ grows linearly with $ID$:

$$T = a + b \cdot ID = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for throughput (a KTU favorite).**
Throughput $TP$ (in bits/second) is the slope's inverse:

$$TP = \frac{ID}{T - a} = \frac{\log_2\left(\frac{D}{W}+1\right)}{T - a}$$

Typical human throughput: $TP \approx 4$ to $10\,\text{bits/s}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:** A designer can predict the time a user will take to acquire a button by knowing its size and distance — fundamental to UI layout.

## 3.2 Derivative 2: Hick's Law Derivation (Cognitive Paradigm)

**Step 1 — Setup.** Suppose a user is presented with $n$ equally probable choices. The time to make a choice is proportional to the entropy $H$ of the choice set.

**Step 2 — Information content of one choice.**
For one of $n$ equiprobable choices:

$$p_i = \frac{1}{n} \quad \text{for all } i$$

**Step 3 — Compute Shannon entropy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i \\
  &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{n} \log_2 \left(\frac{1}{n}\right) \\
  &= -\log_2 \left(\frac{1}{n}\right) \\
  &= \log_2 n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Linear model for reaction time.**

$$RT = a + b \cdot H = a + b \cdot \log_2 n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design takeaway:** More menu items ⇒ exponentially longer decision time. This is why flat menus with $\leq 7 \pm 2$ items are preferred in cognitive paradigm UIs.

## 3.3 Derivative 3: Model Human Processor Cycle Times

**Step 1 — Decompose processing into three subsystems.** The cognitive paradigm models human cognition as three interacting subsystems:
- **Perceptual processor** (sensory input)
- **Cognitive processor** (decision/working memory)
- **Motor processor** (action output)

**Step 2 — Sum the cycle times for a simple reaction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT_{total} &= t_{\text{perceptual}} + t_{\text{cognitive}} + t_{\text{motor}} \\
           &= 100\,\text{ms} + 70\,\text{ms} + 70\,\text{ms} \\
           &= 240\,\text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to a multi-step task.**
For a task with $k$ cognitive steps:

$$RT_{k} = 100\,\text{ms} + 70k\,\text{ms} + 70\,\text{ms} = 170 + 70k \;\;\; (\text{ms})$$

> Used in **KLM (Keystroke-Level Model)** predictions for routine cognitive tasks.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Paradigm Cross-Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Comparison Table — Frequently Asked in KTU 14-Mark Questions**

| Comparison Axis | Human Factors | Cognitive | Activity Theory | Situated Action | Distributed Cognition | Embodied |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit of analysis** | Body | Mind | Activity | Action | Cognitive system | Bodily experience |
| **View of human** | Physical organism | Information processor | Social actor | Improvising agent | Node in network | Embodied, situated |
| **View of technology** | Tool for fit | External memory | Mediating artifact | Resource for action | Cognitive artifact | Tangible extension |
| **Methodology** | Lab experiment | Lab + think-aloud | Ethnography | Video study | System-level analysis | Phenomenology |
| **Criticism** | Ignores mind | Ignores context | Hard to operationalize | Hard to design from | Complex to model | Hard to evaluate |
| **Key idea in 1 line** | "Fit the body" | "Fit the mind" | "Fit the activity" | "Fit the situation" | "Fit the cognitive system" | "Fit the lived body" |

## 3.5 Worked Example — Fitts' Law in Menu Design

> **Problem:** A dropdown menu item is 24 pixels wide, and the cursor is 192 pixels away. Using $a = 50\,\text{ms}$ and $b = 150\,\text{ms/bit}$, predict movement time.

**Step 1 — Compute ID.**

$$ID = \log_2\left(\frac{192}{24} + 1\right) = \log_2(8 + 1) = \log_2 9$$

$$ID \approx 3.170 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Fitts' Law.**

$$T = 50 + 150 \times 3.170 = 50 + 475.5 = 525.5\,\text{ms}$$

> **Conclusion:** The user will take roughly **half a second** to acquire this menu item.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Evolution of HCI Paradigms (Timeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["1950s HUMAN FACTORS"] --> B["1970s COGNITIVE"]
    B --> C["1980s ACTIVITY THEORY"]
    C --> D["LATE 1980s SITUATED ACTION"]
    D --> E["1990s DISTRIBUTED COGNITION"]
    E --> F["2000s EMBODIED INTERACTION"]
    F --> G["TODAY SOCIO TECHNICAL ECOSYSTEMS"]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> This figure shows the **chronological shift of focus** — from body → mind → activity → situation → system → body-in-world. The trajectory is not strictly linear; modern systems integrate multiple paradigms.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Activity Theory Triangle (Engeström's Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph topRow["Activity System"]
        S["SUBJECT\nThe user or group"]
        O["OBJECT\nThe goal / outcome"]
        T["TOOLS\nMediating artifacts"]
        R["RULES\nNorms and policies"]
        C["COMMUNITY\nSocial context"]
        D["DIVISION OF LABOUR\nRoles and tasks"]
    end

    S <-->|mediated by| T
    S <-->|mediated by| C
    T <-->|mediated by| R
    C <-->|mediated by| D
    R <-->|mediated by| D
    S --> O

    style S fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style O fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style T fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style R fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style C fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style D fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the triangle:** The **Subject** (e.g., a nurse) uses **Tools** (e.g., a hospital app) to transform the **Object** (e.g., patient recovery) within a **Community** (e.g., hospital), governed by **Rules** (e.g., medical protocols) and a **Division of Labour** (e.g., doctor–nurse roles). This is the canonical diagram for the **Activity Theory paradigm**.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Block Architecture of the Cognitive Paradigm (Model Human Processor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["Sensory Input\nLight, sound, touch"] --> pp["PERCEPTUAL PROCESSOR\ncycle = 100 ms"]
    pp --> wm["WORKING MEMORY\ncapacity = 7 plus or minus 2"]
    wm --> cp["COGNITIVE PROCESSOR\ncycle = 70 ms"]
    cp --> ltm["LONG TERM MEMORY\nunlimited capacity"]
    ltm --> cp
    cp --> mp["MOTOR PROCESSOR\ncycle = 70 ms"]
    mp --> out["Motor Output\nClick, type, gesture"]

    style pp fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style wm fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style cp fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style ltm fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style mp fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> **Reading the architecture:** Information flows from perception → working memory → cognition → long-term memory (consulted as needed) → motor output. Each box has a *cycle time* used in MHP-based predictions.

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Embodied Interaction — Three Layers of Engagement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LAYER 1\nTangible Bits\nPhysical artifacts"]
    L2["LAYER 2\nSocial Computing\nPresence and awareness"]
    L3["LAYER 3\nPhenomenological Meaning\nLived experience"]
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 -.->|informs design of| L1

    style L1 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> The **embodied interaction paradigm** is layered: *physical artifacts* enable *social presence*, which generates *phenomenological meaning*. Each layer feeds back into the design of artifacts — a circular, iterative relationship.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "paradigm" in the context of HCI. List any four major HCI paradigms.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A paradigm in HCI is a **theoretical framework or set of fundamental assumptions** that shapes how human-machine interaction is understood, studied, and designed. It defines the *unit of analysis* (body, mind, activity, situation, system, or lived body) and the *preferred research and design methods*.
>
> **Four major paradigms (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Human Factors / Ergonomic Paradigm** — focuses on physical fit between user and machine.
> 2. **Cognitive Paradigm** — views the human as an information processor.
> 3. **Activity Theory Paradigm** — emphasizes tool-mediated, goal-directed activity in a social context.
> 4. **Embodied Interaction Paradigm** — emphasizes physical, social, and phenomenological engagement.

---

### Question 2: State Fitts' Law. What does it predict, and under which paradigm does it belong?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (1.5 marks):**
>
> $$T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$$
>
> where $T$ = movement time, $D$ = distance to target, $W$ = target width, and $a, b$ are empirically fitted constants.
>
> **What it predicts (1 mark):** The time required to rapidly move to a target area is a logarithmic function of the ratio of the distance to the target's size. **Larger or closer targets are acquired faster.**
>
> **Paradigm (0.5 marks):** Fitts' Law is a cornerstone of the **Human Factors / Ergonomic Paradigm** and is also widely used in the Cognitive Paradigm for motor-prediction tasks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Module 1 Internal Choice, typical structure]**

**(a)** Explain the **cognitive paradigm** of HCI in detail. Discuss the **Model Human Processor (MHP)** and the **GOMS** family of models. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is **Hick's Law**? Derive it for a choice task with $n$ equally probable options. State **two design implications** of Hick's Law for menu design. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to A(a) — The Cognitive Paradigm (7 marks)

**1. Definition and Core Idea (2 marks):**
The cognitive paradigm treats the human user as an **information-processing system** analogous to a computer. The mind receives input, processes it through memory and decision stages, and produces output actions. The goal of HCI design under this paradigm is to align the system's information flow with the human's cognitive architecture to maximize efficiency and minimize errors.

**2. The Model Human Processor (MHP) — Card, Moran & Newell (1983) (3 marks):**
MHP decomposes human cognition into **three interacting subsystems** with characteristic cycle times:

- **Perceptual Processor:** receives sensory input; cycle time $\approx 100\,\text{ms}$.
- **Cognitive Processor:** operates on working memory; cycle time $\approx 70\,\text{ms}$.
- **Motor Processor:** executes actions; cycle time $\approx 70\,\text{ms}$.

These subsystems communicate via **working memory** (limited to $7 \pm 2$ chunks) and **long-term memory** (effectively unlimited). For a simple perceptual-motor reaction:

$$RT = 100 + 70 + 70 = 240\,\text{ms}$$

MHP enables **quantitative prediction** of task times (e.g., via the Keystroke-Level Model).

**3. The GOMS Family (2 marks):**
GOMS stands for **Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection Rules**:
- **Goal** — what the user wants to achieve.
- **Operator** — basic perceptual, cognitive, or motor action.
- **Method** — a sequence of operators to achieve a sub-goal.
- **Selection Rule** — a heuristic for choosing between competing methods.

Variants include **CMN-GOMS**, **KLM (Keystroke-Level Model)**, **NGOMSL**, and **CPM-GOMS**, each trading off predictive accuracy against modeling effort.

---

#### Solution to A(b) — Hick's Law Derivation & Implications (7 marks)

**1. Statement of Hick's Law (1 mark):**
The time taken to make a choice from $n$ alternatives is a logarithmic function of the number of alternatives:

$$RT = a + b \cdot H = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$$

(The "+1" appears in the original formulation; many texts omit it for the equiprobable case $n$.)

**2. Derivation (4 marks):**
Let $p_i$ be the probability of choosing the $i$-th option. Shannon's information theory gives the entropy of the choice set:

$$H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

Assuming equiprobable choices, $p_i = 1/n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{n} \log_2 \left(\frac{1}{n}\right) \\
  &= -\log_2 \left(\frac{1}{n}\right) \\
  &= \log_2 n
\end{aligned}
$$

Empirically, $RT$ is observed to be **linear in $H$**:

$$RT = a + b \cdot H = a + b \cdot \log_2 n$$

**3. Two Design Implications (2 marks — 1 each):**

> **Implication 1:** Designers should **limit menu depth and breadth**; doubling the number of items adds $\log_2 2 = 1$ bit of decision time.
>
> **Implication 2:** **Probabilistic, frequency-based menus** (where commonly chosen items are highlighted or placed first) reduce $H$ and speed up decisions — even without reducing the absolute number of options.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Human Factors paradigm** and the **Activity Theory paradigm** with respect to: (i) unit of analysis, (ii) view of the user, (iii) preferred research method, and (iv) limitations. (7 marks)

**(b)** What is the **Situated Action paradigm**? Explain Lucy Suchman's critique of plan-based models. Discuss the implications for HCI design. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to B(a) — Comparison (7 marks)

| Axis | Human Factors Paradigm | Activity Theory Paradigm |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Unit of analysis** | The **physical body** of the user | The **activity system** (user + tools + community) |
| **(ii) View of the user** | A **physical organism** with anthropometric limits | A **social actor** whose actions are mediated by tools and shaped by community |
| **(iii) Preferred method** | Controlled **laboratory experiments** and ergonomic standards | **Ethnographic fieldwork**, activity checklists, and historical analysis |
| **(iv) Limitations** | Ignores cognition, context, and meaning; cannot handle non-physical tasks | Hard to operationalize; difficult to derive concrete design rules |

**Synthesis (1 mark):** The two paradigms sit at opposite ends of a spectrum — Human Factors zooms into the body, while Activity Theory zooms out to the entire socio-technical system. Modern HCI often **combines** them (e.g., ergonomically sound tools designed around activity-flow analysis).

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Situated Action Paradigm (7 marks)

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
The Situated Action paradigm, championed by **Lucy Suchman** in *Plans and Situated Actions* (1987), argues that **every human action is fundamentally shaped by its material and social circumstances**. Plans are not scripts that dictate behavior; they are *resources* that actors use opportunistically as situations unfold.

**2. Suchman's Critique of Plan-Based Models (3 marks):**
- Traditional AI and HCI models (e.g., classical GOMS) treat action as a **sequential execution of a pre-formed plan**.
- Suchman's ethnographic studies (notably of a photocopier interaction) showed that users **improvise, interpret, and adapt** continuously.
- Plans are inherently **underspecified** — they cannot anticipate every contingency.
- Therefore, the *situatedness* of action (its embodied and contextual character) must be central to interaction design, not an afterthought.

**3. Implications for HCI Design (2 marks):**
- **Design for interpretation, not just instruction** — interfaces should make the *consequences* of actions visible.
- **Make the situation legible** — provide feedback about context, history, and state.
- **Support improvisation** — allow users to recover from errors gracefully and pursue unexpected goals.
- These ideas directly influence **Contextual Design**, **Participatory Design**, and modern **conversational / agent-based UIs**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — How Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Vague definitions:** Writing "a paradigm is a way of thinking" — the examiner expects a mention of *theoretical framework*, *unit of analysis*, and *fundamental assumptions*. (–1 mark)
> 2. **Confusing paradigms with interaction styles:** Paradigms are *theoretical*; styles (WIMP, post-WIMP, etc.) are *design categories*. Don't mix them up. (–1 to 2 marks)
> 3. **Skipping the formula derivation:** In 14-mark questions asking "derive Hick's Law," simply writing the final equation fetches **at most 1 of 7 marks**. You MUST show the entropy computation step-by-step. (–4 to 6 marks)
> 4. **Forgetting to label diagrams:** Drawing the Activity Theory triangle without labeling Tools/Rules/Community/Division of Labour loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **No real-world example:** A question on "Embodied Interaction" without a single example (tangible bits, AR, gestural UIs) appears as a *bookish* answer — deduct up to 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist — Paradigms in HCI**

- ✅ A **paradigm** is a theoretical framework with its own *unit of analysis, view of the user, and preferred method*.
- ✅ The **six major paradigms** (in order of emergence):
   1. **Human Factors** → Body → Fitts' Law, ergonomics.
   2. **Cognitive** → Mind → MHP, GOMS, Hick's Law.
   3. **Activity Theory** → Activity → Engeström's triangle, mediation.
   4. **Situated Action** → Situation → Suchman, improvisation.
   5. **Distributed Cognition** → Cognitive System → Hutchins, artifacts as cognition.
   6. **Embodied Interaction** → Lived Body → Dourish, tangible/social.
- ✅ **Fitts' Law** is the formula of the Human Factors paradigm:
   $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$.
- ✅ **Hick's Law** is the formula of the Cognitive paradigm:
   $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2 n$.
- ✅ **MHP** cycle times: **100, 70, 70 ms** — sum = 240 ms.
- ✅ **Activity Theory triangle** has **6 elements** — Subject, Object, Tools, Rules, Community, Division of Labour.
- ✅ **Suchman's** key phrase: *"Plans are resources, not prescriptions."*
- ✅ **Embodied Interaction** = Tangible + Social + Phenomenological.
- ✅ Modern HCI **integrates** all paradigms rather than choosing one — design is **multi-paradigmatic**.
- ✅ For 14-mark KTU answers, always:
   • Define the paradigm clearly.
   • Draw a relevant diagram (Fitts' geometry, MHP, or Activity triangle).
   • Cite at least one key thinker by name.
   • Give one real-world application.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mistakes Performed While Designing a Computer System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT614 - INTRODUCTION TO HUMAN MACHINE INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mistakes Performed While Designing a Computer System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)**, *mistakes performed while designing a computer system* refer to the systematic, recurring errors in design philosophy, methodology, and execution that occur when engineers and developers build interactive systems without adequately considering human factors, cognitive limitations, and user requirements. These mistakes, extensively documented by HCI pioneers such as **Donald Norman** (*The Design of Everyday Things*), **Ben Shneiderman** (*Designing the User Interface*), and **Jakob Nielsen** (usability heuristics), represent violations of established usability principles and often lead to products that are functional but frustrating, error-prone, and ultimately rejected by end-users.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 OECPT614 syllabus specifically emphasizes that understanding design mistakes is a prerequisite for applying *user-centered design (UCD)* principles. These mistakes form the **negative knowledge foundation** — knowing what *not* to do is as critical as knowing best practices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine commissioning an architect to build a house. The architect is a brilliant structural engineer but has never actually lived in a house. He designs:
- Staircases with **uniform step heights**, but ignores that a 1-foot riser is dangerous,
- Light switches **placed 7 feet up** because "it looks symmetrical with the door frame,"
- A kitchen where the stove, sink, and refrigerator form a **triangle 30 feet apart**,
- Doors that open **inward** into narrow hallways.

The house stands. It is structurally sound. But the people living in it struggle daily, bump into things, and curse the architect. **This is precisely what happens in poorly designed computer systems** — the software "runs," but the user suffers.

## 1.3 Why These Mistakes Matter — The Cost of Bad Design

- **Economic Cost:** Industry studies cited in Nielsen's reports suggest that fixing a usability problem **after release** can cost **100x** more than fixing it during the design phase.
- **Safety Cost:** In medical devices, aviation, and nuclear control systems, design mistakes can lead to fatalities.
- **Adoption Cost:** Products with poor usability suffer market rejection regardless of feature richness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric to Remember:** The *5 E's of Usability* (Effective, Efficient, Engaging, Error-tolerant, Easy to learn) are the **positive benchmark** against which design *mistakes* are measured. A design that violates any of these is considered a failure.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Ten Cardinal Mistakes — Structured Logic Breakdown

Below is the **exhaustive taxonomy** of design mistakes as per the KTU-prescribed HMI literature. Each mistake is broken into its *root cause*, *manifestation*, and *correction principle*.

### Mistake 1: Designing for "Averageness" — Ignoring User Diversity
- **Root Cause:** Designers imagine a single "average user" who does not exist.
- **Manifestation:** Interfaces that suit neither novices nor experts; complex features that confuse beginners, while lacking shortcuts for power users.
- **Why it Happens:** Lack of *persona* development and *user segmentation*.
- **Correction:** Apply *user profiling* and design for *user classes* (novice, intermediate, expert).

### Mistake 2: Neglecting the User's Mental Model
- **Root Cause:** Designers expose the *system model* (implementation model) directly to the user.
- **Manifestation:** Technical jargon in the UI (e.g., "NullPointerException" instead of "Please enter a valid name").
- **Correction:** Build the UI around the *user's mental model*, not the *engineer's model*. Use **Norman’s Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation** framework.

### Mistake 3: Treating Users as Programmers
- **Root Cause:** Forcing users to think in procedural, command-driven logic.
- **Manifestation:** Systems that require memorizing commands instead of offering discoverable GUI options.
- **Correction:** Adopt **direct manipulation** principles (visibility of objects, incremental actions, reversibility).

### Mistake 4: No or Inadequate Feedback
- **Root Cause:** Designers assume users "know what happened."
- **Manifestation:** Buttons that appear "clicked" but provide no response; long operations without progress bars.
- **Correction:** Every user action must produce a **visible, immediate, and understandable** response within **0.1 to 1.0 seconds** for basic interactions.

### Mistake 5: Designing Without Iteration (The "Big Bang" Approach)
- **Root Cause:** Skipping the *prototype-test-refine* loop.
- **Manifestation:** A "perfect" system released to users that fails because real-world usage patterns were never observed.
- **Correction:** Follow the **user-centered design (UCD) cycle**: Specify context → Specify requirements → Produce design solutions → Evaluate.

### Mistake 6: Inconsistent Interface Design
- **Root Cause:** Different designers, no *design system* or *style guide*.
- **Manifestation:** "Save" represented by different icons, in different colors, at different locations across screens.
- **Correction:** Establish and enforce a **design language** with consistent terminology, layout, and iconography.

### Mistake 7: Treating Interface Design as Decoration
- **Root Cause:** Designers prioritize aesthetics over functionality.
- **Manifestation:** Beautiful animations that hide critical information; low-contrast text that is "artistic."
- **Correction:** Apply **Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics** (visibility of system status, match between system and real world, user control and freedom, consistency, error prevention, recognition rather than recall, flexibility and efficiency, aesthetic and minimalist design, help users recognize/recover from errors, help and documentation).

### Mistake 8: Confusing Novices and Experts with a Single Interface
- **Root Cause:** No *progressive disclosure* strategy.
- **Manifestation:** Beginners are overwhelmed by expert menus; experts are slowed down by hand-holding wizards.
- **Correction:** Implement *adaptive* or *adaptable* interfaces that reveal complexity as proficiency grows.

### Mistake 9: Unrecoverable Actions — No Undo
- **Root Cause:** Lack of **error tolerance** in design.
- **Manifestation:** "Are you sure?" dialogs that are too generic, or worse, no confirmation at all before destructive actions.
- **Correction:** Provide a **multi-level Undo** (minimum 5-10 steps) and confirm destructive actions with **specific** messages (e.g., "Delete 'Project_Report.pdf' from Trash?").

### Mistake 10: Ignoring the Physical and Social Environment
- **Root Cause:** Lab testing under ideal conditions.
- **Manifestation:** Mobile apps that fail in bright sunlight; medical interfaces that ignore gloved hands; noisy factory environments.
- **Correction:** Conduct **contextual inquiry** and **field studies** in the actual deployment environment.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Map (Cheat Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is exam-critical. Memorize the mapping between mistake, principle violated, and corrective framework.**

| **S.No** | **Design Mistake** | **Principle Violated** | **Corrective Framework** | **Related KTU CO** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Designing for "averageness" | User diversity, user profiling | **Persona development** | CO2 |
| 2 | Ignoring mental model | User's conceptual model | **Norman's gulfs** | CO2 |
| 3 | Treating users as programmers | Direct manipulation | **Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules** | CO3 |
| 4 | No feedback | Visibility of system status | **Nielsen Heuristic #1** | CO3 |
| 5 | No iteration | User-centered design | **UCD Cycle (ISO 9241-210)** | CO2 |
| 6 | Inconsistency | Consistency & standards | **Style guides, design systems** | CO3 |
| 7 | Decoration over function | Aesthetic & minimalist design | **Nielsen Heuristic #8** | CO4 |
| 8 | Single interface for all | Progressive disclosure | **Adaptive/adaptable UI** | CO3 |
| 9 | No undo | Error tolerance & recovery | **Nielsen Heuristics #3, #9** | CO4 |
| 10 | Ignoring context | Contextual design | **Contextual inquiry (Beyer & Holtzblatt)** | CO2 |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding these mistakes is not academic — it directly applies to:
- **Medical Device Design** (e.g., infusion pumps, surgical robots)
- **Avionics Cockpit Design** (FAA/Human Factors standards)
- **Automotive HMI** (touchscreen dashboards, ADAS interfaces)
- **Industrial Control Systems** (SCADA interfaces for power plants)
- **Consumer Software** (mobile apps, web platforms)

In all these domains, design mistakes have caused **documented accidents, fatalities, and financial losses**, making this knowledge essential for any engineer whose work touches an interface.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Model Frameworks

## 3.1 Norman's Gulf Framework — A Step-by-Step Analysis

The most common *root cause* of design mistakes can be formalized using **Donald Norman's Two Gulfs** model. Below is the analytical derivation of how a design mistake manifests as a *gulf*.

### Step 1: Define the User's Intention
The user forms a **goal** in their mind, e.g., "I want to save my document."

$$G_{user} = \text{Goal: Persist current work to durable storage}$$

### Step 2: Define the Gulf of Execution
This is the gap between what the user **wants to do** and what the system **allows them to do**.

$$G_{exec} = \text{Distance}(\text{User Intention}, \text{System Affordances})$$

- If the "Save" button is hidden, labeled as "Commit," or requires a keyboard shortcut unknown to the user → **$G_{exec}$ is large** → **Mistake occurs**.

### Step 3: Define the Gulf of Evaluation
This is the gap between the **system's state** and the user's **perception/understanding** of that state.

$$G_{eval} = \text{Distance}(\text{Actual System State}, \text{User's Perceived System State})$$

- If the file *was* saved but the user sees no "Saved" indicator → **$G_{eval}$ is large** → **User assumes failure and repeats the action**, potentially causing errors.

### Step 4: Combine into the Total Design Friction

The *total design mistake magnitude* can be expressed as:

$$\text{Friction}_{total} = \alpha \cdot G_{exec} + \beta \cdot G_{eval} + \gamma \cdot C_{env}$$

Where:
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are **weighting coefficients** based on context (e.g., $\gamma$ is high in safety-critical systems).
- $C_{env}$ represents **environmental constraints** (lighting, noise, urgency, gloves, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **A "good" design minimizes** $\text{Friction}_{total}$ **. A "bad" design — the kind that manifests the 10 mistakes above — allows one or more terms to grow unbounded.**

## 3.2 The UCD Cycle — Iterative Correction

The **User-Centered Design (UCD) cycle** prescribed by **ISO 9241-210** is the formal countermeasure to "Mistake 5" (no iteration). It is a four-phase loop:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Phase 1:} & \quad \text{Specify the context of use} \\
\text{Phase 2:} & \quad \text{Specify user & organizational requirements} \\
\text{Phase 3:} & \quad \text{Produce design solutions} \\
\text{Phase 4:} & \quad \text{Evaluate designs against requirements}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each iteration **reduces the residual error** of the previous iteration:

$$E_{n+1} = E_{n} \cdot (1 - \eta), \quad \text{where } 0 < \eta < 1$$

Here, $\eta$ is the **learning rate** per iteration. Empirically, $\eta \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ for well-conducted usability tests.

## 3.3 The Shneiderman 8 Golden Rules — Direct Counter-Mapping

Each of Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules directly counters one or more of the 10 mistakes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rule 1: Strive for consistency} &\longrightarrow \text{counters Mistake 6} \\
\text{Rule 2: Enable frequent users to use shortcuts} &\longrightarrow \text{counters Mistake 8} \\
\text{Rule 3: Offer informative feedback} &\longrightarrow \text{counters Mistake 4} \\
\text{Rule 4: Design dialogs to yield closure} &\longrightarrow \text{reduces user anxiety} \\
\text{Rule 5: Offer simple error handling} &\longrightarrow \text{counters Mistake 9} \\
\text{Rule 6: Permit easy reversal of actions} &\longrightarrow \text{counters Mistake 9} \\
\text{Rule 7: Support internal locus of control} &\longrightarrow \text{counters Mistake 3} \\
\text{Rule 8: Reduce short-term memory load} &\longrightarrow \text{counters Mistake 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "How would you correct Mistake X?", the answer is **never** "I would fix it." The answer **must cite** the specific rule, heuristic, or framework being applied, as shown above.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — The Cascading Effect of Design Mistakes

The diagram below illustrates how a **single root-cause mistake** (e.g., ignoring the mental model) **cascades** into multiple user-facing failures, eventually leading to system rejection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root Mistake: Ignoring User Mental Model] --> B[Interface Uses Jargon]
    A --> C[Workflow Mismatches User Expectation]
    A --> D[No Recognition over Recall]
    B --> E[User Confusion]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Increased Error Rate]
    E --> G[Slower Task Completion]
    E --> H[User Frustration]
    F --> I[Task Failure]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Loss of Trust]
    I --> J
    J --> K[System Rejection or Abandonment]
    K --> L[Commercial or Safety Failure]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Norman's Two Gulfs Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userA[User Mind] -->|Intent forms| gulf1[Gulf of Execution]
    gulf1 -->|Bridges via design| systemA[System Interface]
    systemA -->|State changes| gulf2[Gulf of Evaluation]
    gulf2 -->|Bridges via feedback| userB[User Perception]
    userB -->|Loops back| userA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A *good* design makes both gulfs **narrow arrows** (small distance). A *bad* design — manifesting the 10 mistakes — makes these arrows **thick, broken, or invisible**, indicating that the user cannot execute intentions or evaluate results.

## 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — The UCD Iteration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p1[Phase 1: Context of Use] --> p2[Phase 2: Specify Requirements]
    p2 --> p3[Phase 3: Produce Design Solutions]
    p3 --> p4[Phase 4: Evaluate]
    p4 -->|Issues Found| p1
    p4 -->|All Requirements Met| done[Validated Design]
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Mermaid Matrix — Mistake-to-Framework Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Mistake 1: User Averageness] --> F1[Persona Method]
    M2[Mistake 2: Mental Model] --> F2[Norman Gulfs]
    M3[Mistake 3: User as Programmer] --> F3[Direct Manipulation]
    M4[Mistake 4: No Feedback] --> F4[Nielsen H1]
    M5[Mistake 5: No Iteration] --> F5[UCD Cycle]
    M6[Mistake 6: Inconsistency] --> F6[Design System]
    M7[Mistake 7: Decoration] --> F7[Nielsen H8]
    M8[Mistake 8: Single Interface] --> F8[Progressive Disclosure]
    M9[Mistake 9: No Undo] --> F9[Nielsen H3 H9]
    M10[Mistake 10: No Context] --> F10[Contextual Inquiry]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> List any **five common mistakes** made by designers when developing a computer system. Briefly explain why each is harmful.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Designing for an "average user"** — ignores the wide diversity of users (novices vs. experts, young vs. old, abled vs. differently-abled). Harmful because the system suits no one well.
2. **Ignoring the user's mental model** — exposing the system's internal logic instead of the user's conceptual understanding. Harmful because it forces users to "think like a computer."
3. **Providing no or inadequate feedback** — users cannot tell if their action succeeded. Harmful because it creates anxiety and leads to repeated or erroneous actions.
4. **Inconsistent interface design** — same function accessed differently across screens. Harmful because it increases learning time and memory load.
5. **No undo/recovery mechanism** — destructive actions are irreversible. Harmful because small user errors become catastrophic.

*(Award 0.5 mark per correctly identified mistake with harm. 0.5 mark for coherent structure. Total 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> What is the *Gulf of Execution* and the *Gulf of Evaluation* as defined by Donald Norman? How do poor designs widen these gulfs?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Gulf of Execution** is the gap between the **user's intention** (what they want to do) and the **system's affordances** (what the system allows them to do). It is widened when controls are hidden, labels are jargon-heavy, or the workflow is non-intuitive.
- **Gulf of Evaluation** is the gap between the **system's actual state** and the **user's perception** of that state. It is widened when feedback is missing, delayed, or ambiguous.
- Poor designs widen both gulfs simultaneously, causing the user to be unable to *do* what they intend and unable to *understand* what the system has done.

*(Award 1 mark per correct definition, 1 mark for the explanation of how designs widen them. Total 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B1 — Option A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain in detail **Donald Norman's Seven Principles for Transforming Difficult Tasks into Simple Ones**, mapping each to a specific design mistake it counters.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The seven principles, drawn from *The Design of Everyday Things* (Norman, 2013), are:

1. **Use both knowledge in the world and knowledge in the head.** Designers should make objects, actions, and options visible rather than relying on memory. → *Counters Mistake 4 (No feedback) and Mistake 2 (Ignoring mental model).*
2. **Simplify the structure of tasks.** Reduce the number of steps, choices, and information units. → *Counters Mistake 7 (Decoration over function) and Mistake 8 (Single interface).*
3. **Make things visible: bridge the gulfs of execution and evaluation.** Provide clear signifiers and continuous feedback. → *Counters Mistake 4 (No feedback).*
4. **Get the mappings right.** The relationship between controls and their effects should be natural and culturally consistent (e.g., volume slider up = louder). → *Counters Mistake 6 (Inconsistency).*
5. **Exploit the power of constraints, both natural and artificial.** Guide the user by limiting possible actions. → *Counters Mistake 9 (No undo) by preventing the error in the first place.*
6. **Design for error.** Assume users will make mistakes; make the system forgiving. → *Counters Mistake 9 (Unrecoverable actions).*
7. **When all else fails, standardize.** Adopt industry-wide conventions. → *Counters Mistake 6 (Inconsistency).*

*(Award 1 mark per principle with its mapping. Total 7 marks. Examiner will look for **specific** mapping to mistakes, not generic praise of "good design.")*

#### (b) [7 Marks] Critically evaluate how **iterative User-Centered Design (UCD)** addresses the "Big Bang" design mistake. Use the **ISO 9241-210** four-phase cycle in your answer.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

The "Big Bang" mistake (Mistake 5) occurs when a system is designed in a single, monolithic effort without consulting users or testing prototypes. The consequences are well-documented: late-stage rework, budget overruns, and user rejection.

**ISO 9241-210 UCD Cycle** explicitly counters this through four mandatory phases:
- **Phase 1 – Context of Use:** Field studies identify *who* will use the system, *where*, and *for what tasks*. This addresses the "averageness" mistake (Mistake 1).
- **Phase 2 – Requirements:** User needs are translated into measurable usability goals. This addresses the "no iteration" mistake directly.
- **Phase 3 – Design Solutions:** Multiple low-fidelity prototypes (sketches, wireframes) are created, not a single "perfect" design.
- **Phase 4 – Evaluation:** Real users perform real tasks; metrics like *task completion rate*, *time-on-task*, and *error rate* are measured. Findings feed back to Phase 1, creating a **closed feedback loop**.

**Critical Evaluation:**
- *Strengths:* The cycle is **empirically validated** and is the de-facto standard in safety-critical industries (medical, aviation).
- *Weaknesses:* It can be **time-consuming** and requires organizational buy-in. In startups, the cost of iteration is sometimes misjudged as wasteful.
- *Conclusion:* Despite its cost, the cost of *not* iterating is statistically higher — research by **Nielsen (1993)** and **Standish Group CHAOS reports** consistently show that iterative UCD reduces post-release defects by **60–80%**.

*(Award 2 marks for UCD phase explanation, 2 marks for mapping to the "Big Bang" mistake, 2 marks for critical evaluation with reference, 1 mark for structured conclusion. Total 7 marks.)*

---

### **Question B1 — Option B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Discuss **Jakob Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics**. For each, identify the specific design mistake from the standard KTU taxonomy that it counters.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

| **#** | **Heuristic** | **Mistake Countered** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Visibility of system status | Mistake 4: No feedback |
| 2 | Match between system and real world | Mistake 2: Ignoring mental model |
| 3 | User control and freedom | Mistake 9: No undo |
| 4 | Consistency and standards | Mistake 6: Inconsistency |
| 5 | Error prevention | Mistake 9 (proactive side) |
| 6 | Recognition rather than recall | Mistake 3: User as programmer |
| 7 | Flexibility and efficiency of use | Mistake 8: Single interface for all |
| 8 | Aesthetic and minimalist design | Mistake 7: Decoration over function |
| 9 | Help users recognize, diagnose, recover from errors | Mistake 9: Unrecoverable actions |
| 10 | Help and documentation | Mistake 5: No iteration / no user support |

*(Award 0.5 mark per correct pairing, 2 marks for overall coherence and naming the framework. Total 7 marks.)*

#### (b) [7 Marks] With a suitable **real-world case study** (e.g., a hospital software, ATM, or mobile app), explain how three specific design mistakes led to user failure, and how each was corrected using a known HCI framework.

**Model Answer (7 Marks):**

**Case Study: A Hospital Electronic Health Record (EHR) System**

- **Mistake Identified 1: No feedback (Mistake 4).**
  - *Symptom:* Nurses administering medication received no confirmation that the system had logged the dose. They would re-enter, leading to **double dosing** — a documented cause of patient harm.
  - *Correction:* Applied **Nielsen Heuristic #1 (Visibility of System Status)**. A clear, color-coded "Logged at 14:32" indicator with a confirmation sound was added. Audible feedback reduced double-dosing errors by **~70%** (per published case studies).

- **Mistake Identified 2: Inconsistent interface (Mistake 6).**
  - *Symptom:* The "Save Patient Note" button was a green disk in the consultation screen but a blue square in the discharge screen. Senior clinicians mistook it for "Print" and printed blank pages.
  - *Correction:* Adopted a **hospital-wide design system** (analogous to Material Design). All primary actions standardized to a **single color, shape, and label**. Inconsistency eliminated.

- **Mistake Identified 3: Ignoring the physical/social context (Mistake 10).**
  - *Symptom:* The original system required fine touch on capacitive screens, but nurses wear **latex gloves** — touch did not register. They removed gloves hundreds of times a day, wasting time and breaking hygiene protocols.
  - *Correction:* Conducted **contextual inquiry** (Beyer & Holtzblatt). System was migrated to a **glove-compatible resistive touch screen** with larger hit targets (minimum 9 mm × 9 mm per **Fitt's Law**).

*(Award 2 marks per mistake–correction pair, 1 mark for choosing a realistic case study. Total 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT write generic answers** like "Designers should make the system user-friendly." This will fetch **0 marks**. KTU examiners specifically look for the *named framework* (Norman's gulfs, Nielsen heuristics, Shneiderman's rules, UCD cycle, ISO 9241-210).
> 2. **Do NOT confuse "mistakes" with "usability problems."** A usability problem is a *symptom*; a design mistake is the *root cause* that produces it. Examiners want the **cause**, not the symptom.
> 3. **In Part B questions, missing sub-parts (a) and (b)** loses you 7 marks each. Always attempt both.
> 4. **Avoid hand-waving phrases** like "improve the UI" or "enhance UX." Use precise terms: *signifiers, affordances, feedback, visibility, mapping, constraints, error tolerance*.
> 5. **In 14-mark questions, diagrams fetch 1–2 extra marks.** Even a hand-drawn Mermaid-style flowchart on the answer sheet impresses the examiner.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 1 hour before the exam.**

- ✅ The **10 mistakes** form the **negative knowledge base** of HCI design. Memorize all 10 with their root causes.
- ✅ The **counter-frameworks** (Nielsen, Norman, Shneiderman, UCD/ISO 9241-210) must be paired with their corresponding mistakes.
- ✅ **Norman's Two Gulfs** are the *primary diagnostic tool* for any design failure — Gulf of Execution (user-to-system) and Gulf of Evaluation (system-to-user).
- ✅ **Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules** are the *prescriptive* (positive) counterpart to the 10 mistakes. Know them by number and name.
- ✅ **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** are the most frequently cited in KTU exams. Memorize all 10.
- ✅ **UCD is iterative** — it is **never** a linear, one-shot process. ISO 9241-210 specifies **four phases**, and the loop must close.
- ✅ The cost of fixing a usability problem grows **exponentially** with the development stage — **100x more expensive post-release** than at design stage.
- ✅ **The 5 E's of Usability** — Effective, Efficient, Engaging, Error-tolerant, Easy to learn — are the positive yardstick.
- ✅ Always cite the **named framework, year, and author** (e.g., "Nielsen's 1993 Heuristics," "Norman's 2013 Principles") in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Real-world examples (hospital EHR, ATM, mobile apps, avionics) carry **bonus marks** in KTU valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Interaction with Computers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT614 - INTRODUCTION TO HUMAN MACHINE INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Human Interaction with Computers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Human-Computer Interaction

**Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** is a multidisciplinary field of study that focuses on the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use, and the study of the major phenomena surrounding them. It is concerned with the theoretical, empirical, and methodological aspects of designing technology that supports and enhances human activities, productivity, and experience.

According to the **Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) Special Interest Group on Computer-Human Interaction (SIGCHI)**, HCI is the discipline concerned with the **design, implementation, and evaluation of interactive computing-based artifacts** intended to support human activities across diverse socio-cultural contexts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - OECPT614):** Human Interaction with Computers is the *foundation stone* of this course. Every later module (design process, prototyping, evaluation, emerging paradigms) builds on the terminology and frameworks introduced here. A clear understanding of HCI's multidisciplinary nature, its goals, and its core interaction models is *non-negotiable* for scoring in later modules.

The **three core constituents** of the HCI triad are:

1. **The User (H)** — A human operator with cognitive, physical, perceptual, and emotional capabilities and limitations.
2. **The Computer / System (C)** — A computing device with hardware, software, network, and storage capabilities.
3. **The Interaction (I)** — The bidirectional communication channel that mediates between H and C.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: HCI as a Skilled Hotel Concierge**
>
> Imagine a foreign tourist (the **User**) standing in the lobby of a luxury hotel. The hotel concierge (the **Interface**) speaks the tourist's language, understands the tourist's needs, and translates them into actions that the hotel's vast machinery (the **Computer System**) can execute — booking a cab, reserving a table, finding a translator.
>
> If the concierge speaks clearly, anticipates needs, and resolves complaints quickly, the tourist's experience is *excellent*. If the concierge is slow, speaks jargon, or misunderstands, even the *best* hotel machinery will fail to deliver satisfaction.
>
> **The concierge is the Interface. The tourist is the User. The hotel machinery is the Computer.** HCI is the *art and science of designing that concierge*.

## 1.3 Why HCI Matters — The Three Pillars

| Pillar | Description | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Usability** | How effectively, efficiently, and satisfactorily a user can achieve a goal in a particular environment. | Reduces training cost, support calls, and abandonment. |
| **User Experience (UX)** | The totality of perceptions, emotions, and responses arising from interaction with a product. | Drives brand loyalty, NPS score, and product-market fit. |
| **Accessibility** | Designing for users with the widest range of abilities (WCAG 2.1/2.2 compliance). | Legal compliance, social inclusion, market reach. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Expectation:** A textbook answer must explicitly *define HCI as multidisciplinary*, *list at least 4 contributing disciplines*, and *state the two-fold goal*: (a) producing usable, safe, functional systems; and (b) influencing future design based on user studies. Skipping the multidisciplinary angle is a guaranteed **2-mark penalty** in KTU ESE answers.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in HCI (Familiarization Layer)

Although HCI is heavily qualitative, a few quantitative anchors appear repeatedly in KTU and GATE-style questions:

- **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** — % of users completing a task successfully.
- **Time on Task (ToT)** — Seconds taken by a user to complete a given task.
- **Error Rate** — Average number of errors per task or per session.
- **System Usability Scale (SUS)** — A 10-item Likert scale questionnaire yielding a usability score out of **100**.
- **Learnability (L)** — Time required to reach a target efficiency level.

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics are *introduced* in Module 2 but *applied* later in the evaluation module. KTU often asks 3-mark questions on SUS (Brooke, 1996) and the definition of usability (ISO 9241-11).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Multidisciplinary Nature of HCI

HCI draws intellectual capital from at least **eight** parent disciplines. Each contributes a unique lens:

1. **Computer Science** — Software engineering, programming languages, graphics, AI, databases.
2. **Cognitive Psychology** — Mental models, attention, memory, perception, learning theory.
3. **Design / Industrial Design** — Graphic design, typography, aesthetics, form factors.
4. **Ergonomics / Human Factors** — Physical fit between user and system (posture, reach, vision, hearing).
5. **Linguistics** — Natural language interfaces, formal syntax of commands.
6. **Sociology** — Group dynamics, organizational adoption, computer-supported cooperative work (CSCW).
7. **Anthropology / Ethnography** — Field study of users in their natural settings.
8. **Artificial Intelligence** — Intelligent agents, adaptive interfaces, recommendation systems.

## 2.2 Goals of HCI (The "Why")

The **two overarching goals** as per Dix, Finlay, Abowd & Beale (the canonical HCI text) are:

- **Goal 1 — Usability Goal:** To *produce* usable, safe, and functional interactive systems.
- **Goal 2 — Scientific Goal:** To *develop* or refine theories, models, and frameworks of interaction that can be applied in evaluation, prediction, and design of future systems.

Secondary goals (often tested in KTU ESE):
- **Effectiveness** (can the user achieve the goal?)
- **Efficiency** (with what resource consumption?)
- **Safety** (no catastrophic error paths)
- **Utility** (does the system provide the right functionality?)
- **Learnability, Memorability** (cognitive ergonomic goals)

## 2.3 The Framework of Interaction (Dix et al.)

The canonical HCI framework can be summarized as:

$$
\text{User} \xleftrightarrow{\text{Input} \mid \text{Output}} \text{Interface} \xleftrightarrow{\text{Input} \mid \text{Output}} \text{Computer}
$$

A more formal layered model used in KTU answers is:

$$
\text{User} \rightarrow \text{Task} \rightarrow \text{Interface} \rightarrow \text{System} \rightarrow \text{User (feedback loop)}
$$

This loop is **closed**: every action produces feedback that the user *perceives*, *interprets*, and *evaluates*.

## 2.4 Historical Evolution of HCI

| Era | Computing Paradigm | HCI Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **1950s–60s** | Mainframe batch processing | Punched cards, command-line interfaces. |
| **1970s** | Time-sharing, teletype | TTY, line editors, early study of human factors. |
| **1980s** | Personal Computer (Mac, IBM PC) | **GUI revolution** (Xerox PARC → Apple Macintosh). Direct manipulation. |
| **1990s** | World Wide Web | Browser-based interaction, hyperlink navigation, accessibility (W3C/WCAG). |
| **2000s** | Mobile / Ubiquitous | Touch interfaces, responsive design, multi-touch. |
| **2010s** | Cloud, IoT, Voice | Conversational agents (Siri, Alexa), gesture, AR/VR. |
| **2020s+** | Generative AI, Spatial Computing | LLM-mediated interaction, prompt engineering, neural interfaces, ambient computing. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Standard Landmark:** The 1983 publication of *"The Psychology of Human-Computer Interaction"* by **Stuart K. Card, Thomas P. Moran, and Allen Newell** is widely credited with formally founding HCI as a scientific discipline. Their **GOMS model** (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules) and **Model Human Processor** are still examined.

## 2.5 Norman's Seven Stages of Action (Conceptual Engine)

Donald Norman's seminal 1986 model decomposes the action cycle into **seven stages** grouped around a **gulf of execution** and a **gulf of evaluation**:

- **Gulf of Execution:** Distance between the user's *intention* and the *physical actions* the system affords.
- **Gulf of Evaluation:** Distance between the *system state* and the user's *perception/understanding* of that state.

| Side of the Loop | Stage | Question it Answers |
|---|---|---|
| **Execution** (User → System) | 1. Forming the goal | *What do I want to achieve?* |
| | 2. Forming the intention | *What specific state must I produce?* |
| | 3. Specifying an action | *What concrete action achieves it?* |
| | 4. Executing the action | *I press the button / type the command.* |
| **Evaluation** (System → User) | 5. Perceiving the system state | *What did the system actually do?* |
| | 6. Interpreting the state | *What does that mean in my world?* |
| | 7. Evaluating the outcome | *Did my goal get achieved?* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU **2-mark / 3-mark classic question:** *"Differentiate between Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation with an example."* Always answer by *first defining the gulf*, *then* giving a *daily-life example* (e.g., flipping a light switch in a dark room = failure of the gulf of evaluation because you cannot perceive the state).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Quantitative HCI Models

Although HCI is predominantly qualitative, the following predictive models are **high-yield** for KTU 2024:

| Model | Equation | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fitts' Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | $T$ = movement time, $D$ = distance to target, $W$ = target width, $a,b$ = empirical constants. | $T$ in **seconds**; $D,W$ in **pixels** or **mm**. | Predicts pointing time (mouse to button, finger to icon). |
| **Hick-Hyman Law** | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)$ | $n$ = number of equally probable choices, $a,b$ = constants. | $T$ in **seconds**. | Predicts decision time for menu selection. |
| **Steering Law** (Accot & Zhai) | $T = a + b \cdot \dfrac{D}{W}$ | $D$ = tunnel length, $W$ = tunnel width. | $T$ in **seconds**; $D,W$ in **pixels** or **mm**. | Predicts time to traverse a constrained path (scrollbars, sliders). |
| **Throughput (Fitts' ID)** | $ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Index of Difficulty. | **bits**. | Quantifies task difficulty independent of device. |
| **SUS Score** | $\text{SUS} = 2.5 \times \sum (x_i - 1)$ for odd items; $2.5 \times (5 - x_i)$ for even. | $x_i \in \lbrace 1,2,3,4,5 \rbrace$ Likert values. | **0–100** scale. | Standard usability questionnaire. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula Sheet Discipline:** When transcribing Fitts' Law in KTU exams, *always* state the variables *and* the unit. Examiners deduct 0.5 mark for ambiguous variables like $D$ or $W$ when units are not declared.

## 2.7 Principles of Good Interaction (Heuristics Preview)

While detailed heuristics are taught in Module 3, the **universal principles** a student must internalize in Module 2 are:

- **Visibility of system status** — Always keep the user informed.
- **Match between system and the real world** — Speak the user's language, not jargon.
- **User control and freedom** — Provide undo, redo, exit.
- **Consistency and standards** — Same word/icon means the same thing.
- **Error prevention** — Better than good error messages.
- **Recognition rather than recall** — Minimize memory load.
- **Flexibility and efficiency of use** — Accelerators for experts.
- **Aesthetic and minimalist design** — No irrelevant information.
- **Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors.**
- **Help and documentation.**

> [!NOTE]
> These are Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994). They are *the* most cited reference in KTU ESE for "principles of good interaction design." The first one — *Visibility* — is often the *only* one a student remembers; examiners *love* to ask for the full list and penalize partial recall.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | HCI Application | Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Avionics (Cockpit Design)** | Glass cockpits, head-up displays. | Reduced pilot error by 30–50 % (FAA reports). |
| **Medical Devices** | Touchscreens in ICUs, infusion pumps. | Reduced medication errors (Joint Commission Sentinel). |
| **Automotive** | Infotainment, voice control, driver monitoring. | Driver distraction metrics standardized by NHTSA. |
| **Assistive Tech** | Screen readers, eye-tracking, switch input. | Empowering users with disabilities. |
| **Industrial Control** | SCADA / DCS control rooms. | Chernobyl (1986) and Three Mile Island (1979) were *human-factors failures*. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, examiner-ready derivations and logical breakdowns of the foundational models introduced in Section 2.

## 3.1 Derivation of Norman's Seven Stages of Action

The 7-stage model is a *decomposition*, not a calculus-based derivation, but it is derived from a simple **information-theoretic action loop**.

**Step 1 — Define the action cycle as a closed feedback system.**
A user has a *Goal (G)*. The system has a *State (S)*. The user takes action to transform G into S.

$$
G \xrightarrow{\text{Execution}} S \xrightarrow{\text{Evaluation}} \text{(Compared to G)}
$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the Execution path into four cognitive-motor steps.**

$$
G \rightarrow \text{Intention (I)} \rightarrow \text{Action Sequence (A)} \rightarrow \text{Execution (E)}
$$

These correspond to Norman's stages 1, 2, 3, 4:
1. **Forming the goal** — Convert the high-level desire into a concrete goal $G$.
2. **Forming the intention** — Decide the target state $S_{target}$.
3. **Specifying an action** — Choose *which* actuator (mouse, keyboard, voice) to use and *how*.
4. **Executing the action** — The motor activity is performed.

**Step 3 — Decompose the Evaluation path into three perceptual-cognitive steps.**

$$
\text{System State (S)} \rightarrow \text{Perception (P)} \rightarrow \text{Interpretation (I')} \rightarrow \text{Evaluation (E')}
$$

These correspond to Norman's stages 5, 6, 7:
5. **Perceiving the system state** — Sensory registration.
6. **Interpreting the state** — Cognitive decoding (what does the new state mean?).
7. **Evaluating the outcome** — Comparing the perceived state to the original goal.

**Step 4 — Identify the two gulfs.**

$$
\text{Gulf of Execution} = \text{dist}(I, A)
$$

$$
\text{Gulf of Evaluation} = \text{dist}(S, E')
$$

Where $\text{dist}(\cdot)$ is the *cognitive distance* the user must bridge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** A well-designed interface *minimizes* both gulfs. The execution gulf is closed by *clear affordances*; the evaluation gulf is closed by *immediate, perceivable feedback*.

## 3.2 Fitts' Law — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation

Fitts' Law is the **most quoted quantitative law in HCI**. It is derived from **Shannon's information theorem** applied to a target-acquisition motor task.

**Step 1 — Define the task.** A user must move a pointer from a starting position to a target of width $W$ located at a distance $D$ away.

**Step 2 — Quantify task difficulty using Shannon's information index.**

Shannon's theorem states that the **information transmitted** through a channel is:

$$
H = \log_2 \!\left( \frac{\text{No. of distinguishable symbols}}{\text{Uncertainty per symbol}} \right)
$$

For pointing, the "signal" is the target among the spatial possibilities. The number of distinguishable target positions along the distance $D$ is roughly $D/W$. Therefore:

$$
ID = \log_2 \!\left( \frac{D}{W} + 1 \right)
$$

The "+1" prevents $\log(0)$ when $D \ll W$.

> The $ID$ is called the **Index of Difficulty** and is measured in **bits**. A smaller target or larger distance increases $ID$.

**Step 3 — Form the linear human-operator model.** Fitts hypothesized that human movement time $T$ is a linear function of the information content of the task:

$$
T = a + b \cdot ID
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $ID$ to obtain Fitts' Law.**

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2 \!\left( \frac{D}{W} + 1 \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Define the Index of Performance (Throughput).**

$$
IP = \frac{ID}{MT} \quad \text{(bits / second)}
$$

A high $IP$ means an efficient interface; typical values are 4–10 bits/s for mouse, 1–2 bits/s for a finger on a small touch screen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Fitts' Law predicts that **larger targets** and **shorter distances** reduce pointing time. This is why KTU exam answers should *always* recommend "Place primary buttons in the Fitts' Law path of the user's gaze/mouse and make them at least 44×44 pixels (WCAG 2.5.5)."

## 3.3 Hick-Hyman Law — Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Decision task definition.** A user must select **one** action out of $n$ equally probable choices.

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula for the entropy of a uniform distribution.**

$$
H = \log_2(n)
$$

**Step 3 — Linear operator model.** Reaction time $T$ scales linearly with $H$:

$$
T = a + b \cdot H
$$

**Step 4 — Empirical correction.** Real experiments show that adding a "null" choice (not choosing) shifts the curve. The corrected form is:

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Consequence:** A flat menu of 64 items is *slower* than a hierarchical menu of two levels of 8 items each. KTU ESE answers must use this law to *justify* hierarchical navigation design.

## 3.4 The Steering Law — Derivation

Accot and Zhai (1997) extended Fitts' Law to **path traversal** through a tunnel of varying width.

**Step 1 — Define the path as a continuous sequence of subtasks.** Each infinitesimal step $ds$ presents a new target of width $W(s)$ to be acquired while moving along direction $s$.

**Step 2 — Integrate the local Fitts' difficulty along the path length $D$.**

$$
T = \int_{0}^{D} \frac{1}{v(s)} \, ds
$$

**Step 3 — Assume constant width $W$ and steady-state velocity proportional to $W$.** Substituting yields the **Steering Law**:

$$
T = a + b \cdot \frac{D}{W}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the contrast with Fitts' Law:** Steering has a *linear* cost in $D/W$ rather than *logarithmic*. This is because *steering* requires continuous sub-movements, not a single ballistic motion.

## 3.5 User-Centered Design (UCD) Cycle — Stepwise Logic

**Step 1 — Specify the context of use.** Identify users, tasks, and environments (organizational, technical, physical).

**Step 2 — Specify requirements.** Translate user needs into functional, data, environmental, and usability requirements.

**Step 3 — Produce design solutions.** Sketch, prototype, refine iteratively.

**Step 4 — Evaluate designs against requirements.** Empirical testing with users → iterate.

The cycle is a **closed loop** with four ordered phases:

$$
\text{Specify Context} \rightarrow \text{Specify Requirements} \rightarrow \text{Design} \rightarrow \text{Evaluate} \rightarrow \text{(loop)}
$$

ISO 9241-210 (Human-Centred Design) formalizes this as the canonical engineering process.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Model

| Engineering Problem | Applicable Model | Key Output |
|---|---|---|
| "Where to place the *Save* button?" | Fitts' Law | Optimal button size, distance. |
| "How many items in a menu?" | Hick-Hyman Law | Maximum items per menu level. |
| "How fast will a user scroll a track?" | Steering Law | Time to traverse a slider. |
| "Why did the user fail to detect a system error?" | Norman's 7 Stages | Identifies the failed stage (usually 5 or 6). |
| "How usable is my prototype?" | SUS + Task Completion | Quantified usability score. |
| "What is the cognitive load of a screen?" | GOMS / KLM (Module 3) | Predicted task time. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Heuristic:** Whenever a question mentions *predicting time* or *optimizing buttons / menus*, the model expected is *Fitts' Law* or *Hick-Hyman Law*. Whenever the question mentions *understanding user error*, the expected framework is *Norman's 7 Stages* or the *Gulfs model*.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write**
>
> 1. **Do not confuse HCI with UI/UX design.** UI is the artifact; UX is the experience; HCI is the *discipline* that studies both. Examiners will deduct a full 2 marks if a 14-mark answer reduces HCI to "designing screens."
> 2. **For Norman's Stages, list all 7 — not just the first 3.** Partial answers (e.g., stopping at "execution") lose 4 of 7 marks.
> 3. **For Fitts' Law, *declare* the unit of $D$ and $W$.** Writing the equation without units is a 0.5-mark penalty in technical questions.
> 4. **Disciplines question:** The minimum acceptable list is **4 disciplines** with **one-line contribution** of each. Writing "psychology and computers" alone will fetch only 2 of 7 marks.
> 5. **Avoid colloquial language.** KTU board examiners expect formal terms — "affordance," "feedback," "visibility," "mapping," "gulf" — to be used correctly.
> 6. **Diagrams must be neat and labeled.** A diagram without arrows or labels is treated as decorative, not as content.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define Human-Computer Interaction. List any four disciplines that contribute to HCI.**

**Model Answer:**

Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is a multidisciplinary field that studies the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use, focusing on the phenomena surrounding them.

**Four contributing disciplines:**

1. **Computer Science** — Provides software engineering, programming languages, and computer graphics to build the system.
2. **Cognitive Psychology** — Provides theories of perception, memory, attention, and mental models of the user.
3. **Ergonomics / Human Factors** — Provides principles of the physical fit between user and system (posture, reach, vision).
4. **Design** — Provides principles of visual aesthetics, typography, and form for usable interfaces.

*(Any other valid disciplines such as Linguistics, Sociology, Anthropology, AI are also acceptable.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Gulf of Execution | Gulf of Evaluation |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | The gap between the user's *intention* and the *physical actions* the system affords. | The gap between the *system state* and the user's *perception and understanding* of that state. |
| **Closed by** | Good affordances, clear mapping, predictability. | Immediate, perceivable feedback. |
| **Question it answers** | *"How do I tell the system to do it?"* | *"How do I know what the system just did?"* |
| **Example** | A user wants to print a document but cannot locate the print icon. | A user clicks "Save" but the screen does not confirm whether the file was saved, leaving the user in doubt. |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. The student answers **one** of the two internal choices (OR). Sub-parts typically include a 4–5 line theoretical question and a 5–7 line applied/derivation question.

### Question 1 — Choice A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) Explain the multidisciplinary nature of Human-Computer Interaction. Discuss the role of cognitive psychology and ergonomics in HCI design. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Human-Computer Interaction is *inherently* multidisciplinary because the central problem — making a computing system usable by humans — requires expertise from fields that study the human, the machine, and the artifact between them.

**Why multidisciplinary?** A usable system must satisfy *technical* correctness (Computer Science), *cognitive* alignment with the user's mental model (Psychology), *physical* comfort (Ergonomics), *aesthetic* appeal (Design), and *social/organizational* adoption (Sociology). No single discipline possesses all these competencies.

**Role of Cognitive Psychology:**
- Provides the **Model Human Processor** (Card, Moran & Newell, 1983) which decomposes human cognition into *perceptual, cognitive,* and *motor* processors with associated cycle times.
- Supplies theories of **attention** (limited capacity — informs the "minimalist design" heuristic).
- Supplies theories of **memory** (short-term 7±2 items — informs chunking, recognition over recall).
- Supplies the concept of **mental models** — designers must align the system's image with the user's mental model.

**Role of Ergonomics / Human Factors:**
- Defines **anthropometric** limits (Fitts' Law input — target sizes 44×44 px, minimum touch target 9 mm).
- Addresses **postural** and **repetitive-strain** issues (keyboard height, screen distance 50–70 cm).
- Addresses **environmental** factors (lighting, noise, temperature).
- Empirically tested in safety-critical domains such as aviation, nuclear control rooms, and medical devices.

**Concluding line:** HCI's strength lies in this synthesis — without cognitive psychology, designs ignore the mind; without ergonomics, they ignore the body.

**(b) Describe Norman's Seven Stages of Action with a neat diagram. Explain how Gulfs of Execution and Evaluation arise. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Seven Stages of Action (Norman, 1986) decompose the *closed loop* of human-computer interaction into seven cognitive-motor stages grouped around two gulfs.

| # | Stage | Layer | Gulf |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forming the goal | Cognition | — |
| 2 | Forming the intention | Cognition | **Gulf of Execution** |
| 3 | Specifying an action | Cognition | **Gulf of Execution** |
| 4 | Executing the action | Motor | — |
| 5 | Perceiving the system state | Perception | — |
| 6 | Interpreting the state | Cognition | **Gulf of Evaluation** |
| 7 | Evaluating the outcome | Cognition | — |

**Stages 1–4 (Execution side):** The user forms a *goal* (e.g., "send an email"), translates it into an *intention* ("click Compose"), *specifies* the action sequence ("move mouse to the top-left, click"), and *executes* it.

**Stages 5–7 (Evaluation side):** The user *perceives* the system's response (a new email window opens), *interprets* it ("an empty compose form has appeared"), and *evaluates* whether the original goal has been achieved.

**Gulfs:**
- The **Gulf of Execution** is the cognitive distance between stages 2 and 4 — the difficulty of *translating intent into action*. Designers close it with **clear affordances** (e.g., a printer-shaped "Print" button).
- The **Gulf of Evaluation** is the distance between stages 5 and 7 — the difficulty of *interpreting* what the system did. Designers close it with **immediate, perceivable feedback** (e.g., a "Message sent" toast notification).

```
[Goal] → [Intention] → [Action Spec] → [Execute]
                ↘ GULF OF EXECUTION ↗
                                        
[Evaluate] ← [Interpret] ← [Perceive] ← [System]
       ↖ GULF OF EVALUATION ↙
```

---

### Question 1 — Choice B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) Discuss the framework of HCI with a neat diagram. Describe the role of feedback in closing the interaction loop. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The HCI framework views interaction as a *closed loop* between the user, the interface, and the computer. The canonical model:

$$
\text{User} \xrightarrow{\text{Input}} \text{Interface} \xrightarrow{\text{Command}} \text{Computer}
$$

$$
\text{Computer} \xrightarrow{\text{Response}} \text{Interface} \xrightarrow{\text{Output}} \text{User}
$$

The two halves are:
- **Input path** (User → System): Affordances, input devices, command languages.
- **Output path** (System → User): Displays, sounds, haptic feedback, error messages.

**Role of Feedback:** Feedback is the system's response that informs the user about the *result* of their action. It is the *only* mechanism by which the system informs the user that something has happened. Without feedback, the user has no way of knowing whether the action was successful, failed, or is in progress.

**Types of feedback:**
- **Visual** — Color change, animation, icon update.
- **Auditory** — Click sound, beep, voice confirmation.
- **Haptic** — Vibration on touchscreens, key-click force.
- **Linguistic** — Text messages, alerts.

**Design principle:** Feedback must be **immediate** (< 100 ms for a click response, < 1 s for a command response), **informative** (what happened), and **perceivable** (the user must notice it). Visibility of system status is *the* most important Nielsen heuristic for exactly this reason.

**(b) State and explain Fitts' Law. A user moves a cursor over a screen where the target is 200 pixels away and 50 pixels wide. Using Fitts' Law with empirical constants $a = 50$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit, calculate the predicted movement time and the Index of Performance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State Fitts' Law.**

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left( \frac{D}{W} + 1 \right)
$$

Where $T$ = movement time, $D$ = distance, $W$ = width, $a$ and $b$ are empirical constants.

**Step 2 — Identify the given values.**

$$
D = 200 \text{ px}, \quad W = 50 \text{ px}, \quad a = 50 \text{ ms}, \quad b = 150 \text{ ms/bit}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Index of Difficulty (ID).**

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left( \frac{200}{50} + 1 \right) = \log_2\!\left( 4 + 1 \right) = \log_2(5)
$$

Using $\log_2(5) \approx 2.3219$:

$$
ID \approx 2.32 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the movement time $T$.**

$$
T = 50 + 150 \cdot 2.3219
$$

$$
T = 50 + 348.29
$$

$$
