# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

### Q1. Define "Usability Engineering Lifecycle" as proposed by Deborah Mayhew. List its three phases.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Usability Engineering Lifecycle** proposed by **Deborah Mayhew** is a structured, iterative framework that integrates usability activities into software development. It has three phases:

1. **Requirements Analysis Phase** — Know the user, perform competitive analysis, set usability goals, define user satisfaction goals.
2. **Design / Testing / Development Iteration Phase** — Develop conceptual model, build prototypes (low to high fidelity), conduct usability evaluations.
3. **Installation Phase** — Implement the design, conduct field testing, perform post-release evaluation.

The lifecycle emphasizes that usability is an *engineering discipline* with measurable goals, not a subjective aesthetic judgment. **(3 marks)**

---

> [KTU University Exam - June 2024]

### Q2. Differentiate between *usability* and *user experience (UX)*. (3 marks)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Usability | User Experience (UX) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (ISO)** | ISO 9241-11 | ISO 9241-210 |
| **Scope** | Task-oriented (efficiency, effectiveness, errors) | Holistic (emotions, perceptions, brand, value) |
| **Focus** | *Can* the user achieve the goal? | *How does the user feel* during and after? |
| **Measurement** | Quantitative (TCR, task time, SUS) | Mostly qualitative (interviews, sentiment) |
| **Sub-attribute of** | UX | — |
| **Examples** | Login takes 10 s | Login feels trustworthy and pleasant |

In short, **usability is a subset of UX**. Usability is about *utility and utility of interaction*; UX is about the *entire experience* including marketing, packaging, and emotional connection. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *Explain the integration of HMI activities into the traditional Waterfall Software Development Life Cycle. For each phase, list at least one HMI activity and the corresponding deliverable. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *With the help of Boehm's cost-of-fix curve, justify why HMI must be integrated from the requirements phase rather than after implementation. Use a worked numerical example. (7 marks)*

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

The **Waterfall model** consists of six sequential phases: *Feasibility, Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Maintenance*. HMI is **not** a separate phase but a **parallel usability track** that runs alongside each SDLC phase.

**Phase 1 — Feasibility (1 mark):**
- HMI activity: Identify *user classes* (novice, expert, admin), conduct *stakeholder interviews*, and document user profiles.
- Deliverable: *User profile document* and a *usability feasibility note* included in the project charter.

**Phase 2 — Requirements (1 mark):**
- HMI activity: Perform *task analysis* using techniques like **Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA)** or **GOMS**. Define measurable *usability goals* (e.g., "novice user shall book a ticket in ≤ 90 s").
- Deliverable: *Usability Requirements Specification* appended to the SRS.

**Phase 3 — Design (1 mark):**
- HMI activity: Develop *low-fidelity prototypes* (sketches, wireframes), perform *card sorting* to derive information architecture.
- Deliverable: *Wireframe set* and *style guide*.

**Phase 4 — Implementation (1 mark):**
- HMI activity: Build *high-fidelity interactive prototypes* in tools like Figma/Axure, conduct *heuristic evaluation* using **Nielsen's 10 heuristics**.
- Deliverable: *Interactive prototype* and *heuristic violation list with severity ratings*.

**Phase 5 — Testing (1 mark):**
- HMI activity: Conduct *summative usability tests* (think-aloud protocol) with 8–10 real users per user class; administer *System Usability Scale (SUS)* questionnaire.
- Deliverable: *Usability Test Report* with TCR, task time, error rate, and SUS score.

**Phase 6 — Maintenance (1 mark):**
- HMI activity: *Field studies*, log analytics, and quarterly user surveys; feedback feeds the *usability improvement backlog*.
- Deliverable: *Post-release evaluation report* and *next-version UX roadmap*.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
HMI in the Waterfall model is a *parallel cross-cutting concern*. The key benefit is that usability defects are detected at the stage where they are *cheapest to fix*, thereby reducing rework and improving user satisfaction.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**Theoretical Foundation (2 marks):**
**Barry Boehm's empirical studies** (1981, refined later) demonstrated that the cost of fixing a defect grows *exponentially* with the phase in which it is discovered. The general form is:

$$C_{\text{fix}}(p) = C_{\text{req}} \times k^{p-1}$$

where $p$ is the phase number and $k$ is the cost multiplier (typically 5 to 100).

**Worked Example (3 marks):**
Suppose a usability defect is discovered. The cost to fix it at the requirements stage is **₹ 2,000**. Using $k = 10$:

| Phase ($p$) | Calculation | Cost (₹) | Multiplier |
|---|---|---|---|
| Requirements (1) | $2000 \times 10^{0}$ | 2,000 | 1× |
| Design (2) | $2000 \times 10^{1}$ | 20,000 | 10× |
| Implementation (3) | $2000 \times 10^{2}$ | 2,00,000 | 100× |
| Testing (4) | $2000 \times 10^{3}$ | 20,00,000 | 1,000× |
| Post-Release (5) | $2000 \times 10^{4}$ | 2,00,00,000 | 10,000× |

**Inference (2 marks):**
A defect caught at requirements costs ₹ 2,000, but the *same* defect after release costs ₹ 2,00,00,000 — a **10,000× penalty**. Beyond cost, late fixes also damage *brand reputation*, *user trust*, and may cause *safety hazards* in medical or aviation systems. Hence, integrating HMI from the requirements phase is not just a best practice but an **engineering and economic necessity**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often write only the formula but skip the *worked numerical substitution*. Always plug in numbers and tabulate the result. Skipping the table typically costs **2–3 marks**.
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** When asked about Waterfall + HMI, students frequently list HMI activities but forget the *deliverables*. Both activity **and** deliverable are required for full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** *Describe the Mayhew Usability Engineering Lifecycle in detail. Discuss each of the three phases with their respective activities. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain ISO 9241-210 Human-Centred Design process. List its six activities and describe the iterative nature of the process with a neat diagram. (7 marks)*

#### Model Answer for (a) — 7 marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The **Usability Engineering Lifecycle** proposed by **Deborah Mayhew** in her 1999 book *"The Usability Engineering Lifecycle: A Practitioner's Handbook for Designing User Interfaces"* is a structured, goal-driven approach to integrating usability into the software process.

**Phase 1 — Requirements Analysis (2 marks):**
This phase produces the *usability requirements specification* that drives the rest of the lifecycle.
1. **Know the User** — Develop detailed *user profiles* capturing demographics, computing experience, domain knowledge, and physical/cognitive abilities.
2. **Competitive Analysis** — Benchmark the proposed product against 3–5 competitors; identify strengths, weaknesses, and *usability opportunity gaps*.
3. **Goal Setting** — Define *usability goals* quantitatively (e.g., "TCR ≥ 95%", "task time ≤ 60 s", "error rate ≤ 0.5/task").
4. **User Satisfaction Goals** — Define subjective goals (e.g., "SUS ≥ 80", "≥ 4.0/5 on satisfaction survey").

**Phase 2 — Design / Testing / Development Iteration (2 marks):**
This is the *iterative core* of the lifecycle and cycles through:
5. **Conceptual Model Design** — Define the *user mental model*, choose appropriate *metaphors* (e.g., desktop metaphor), and design *navigation hierarchy*.
6. **Prototyping** — Build progressive prototypes: *paper sketches → wireframes → interactive prototypes → production screens*.
7. **Usability Evaluation** — Conduct *formative evaluations* (heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthrough) on early prototypes and *summative usability tests* on high-fidelity prototypes.

**Phase 3 — Installation (1 mark):**
8. **Implementation** — Translate the validated design into production code using *style guides* and *component libraries*.
9. **Field Testing / Post-Release Evaluation** — Collect real-world usage data, user complaints, and analytics; feed findings back into the next iteration.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Mayhew's lifecycle is *engineering-disciplined* — every activity produces a measurable artefact. It is the most widely adopted HMI-in-software-process model in industry and is exam-relevant as a 7-mark question.

#### Model Answer for (b) — 7 marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
**ISO 9241-210:2010** (replacing ISO 13407:1999) provides the international standard for *Human-Centred Design (HCD)* for interactive systems. It defines four guiding principles and a six-activity iterative process.

**The Four Guiding Principles (1 mark):**
1. Design is based on an explicit understanding of users, tasks, and environments.
2. Users are involved throughout design and development.
3. Design is driven and refined by user-centred evaluation.
4. Design addresses the whole user experience.

**The Six HCD Activities (3 marks):**

| # | Activity | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Plan the HCD process** | Define the HCD approach, methods, responsibilities, and timeline aligned with the SDLC. |
| 2 | **Understand and specify the context of use** | Identify users, tasks, equipment, and physical/social environments. |
| 3 | **Specify the user requirements** | Derive *user needs* and *usability goals* from the context of use. |
| 4 | **Produce design solutions** | Develop conceptual model, prototypes (low to high fidelity). |
| 5 | **Evaluate designs against requirements** | Conduct usability tests, heuristic evaluations, and field studies. |
| 6 | **Iterate** | Refine design based on evaluation findings. |

**Iterative Nature (2 marks):**
The process is **inherently iterative**, not linear. Each iteration cycles through all six activities, with increasing fidelity. Iteration continues until the design satisfies the *user requirements* and *usability goals*. ISO 9241-210 explicitly states that "the process is iterative throughout," meaning that even *after deployment*, the HCD loop continues (this is shown in the **Diagram 2 in Section 4** above).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** Students often confuse **ISO 13407** (old standard, 1999, four activities) with **ISO 9241-210** (current standard, 2010, six activities). KTU 2024 syllabus refers to the **revised six-activity process**. Mentioning the older four-activity version may lose 1 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

### Core Concepts
- **HMI in the software process** = integrating usability engineering into every SDLC phase, not as a post-hoc activity.
- **Three foundational frameworks:** Mayhew's Lifecycle, ISO 9241-210, and Spiral Model integration.
- **Five pillars of usability** (ISO 9241-11): *Learnability, Efficiency, Memorability, Errors, Satisfaction* — *can* be remembered with the mnemonic **"LEMES"**.

### Models and Lifecycles
- **Mayhew's Lifecycle = 3 phases × 3 activities = 9 steps** total. Memorize the matrix.
- **ISO 9241-210 = 6 activities, iterative, four guiding principles.**
- **Waterfall + HMI = parallel usability track**, not a separate phase.
- **Spiral Model = risk-driven**, naturally suited for usability prototyping.
- **Agile + HMI = UX stories in backlog, definition of done includes heuristic pass.**

### Key Methods (Know the Names!)
- **Hierarchical Task Analysis (HTA)**, **GOMS**, **Card Sorting**, **Cognitive Walkthrough**, **Heuristic Evaluation**, **Think-Aloud Protocol**, **A/B Testing**, **Field Studies**.

### Key Formulas (Numerical Problems)
- **TCR** = (Completed / Attempted) × 100
- **Mean task time** = $\bar{T} = \frac{1}{n}\sum T_i$
- **Standard deviation** = $\sigma_T = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(T_i - \bar{T})^2}$
- **Efficiency** = $\eta = (T_{\text{optimal}} / T_{\text{actual}}) \times 100$
- **Boehm's Cost** = $C_{\text{fix}}(p) = C_{\text{req}} \times k^{p-1}$

### Standard Metrics & Thresholds
- **TCR ≥ 95%** for primary user flows.
- **SUS ≥ 68** = above average; **SUS ≥ 80** = excellent.
- **8–10 users** typically sufficient for formative usability tests (Nielsen, 1993).
- **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** — memorize the first 5 at minimum (visibility of system status, match between system and real world, user control and freedom, consistency and standards, error prevention).

### Common Board Pitfalls
- ❌ Confusing ISO 13407 (old) with ISO 9241-210 (current).
- ❌ Listing HMI activities but forgetting *deliverables*.
- ❌ Skipping numerical substitution in cost-of-fix problems.
- ❌ Treating Waterfall HMI as a separate phase rather than a parallel track.
- ❌ Saying "usability = UX" — usability is a *subset* of UX.

### One-Line Exam-Ready Definition
> *"HMI in the software process is the engineering discipline of integrating user research, usability design, prototyping, and user-based evaluation as parallel, iterative activities across all phases of the SDLC, governed by quantitative usability goals."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT614 - INTRODUCTION TO HUMAN MACHINE INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Usability Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Usability Engineering** is a *systematic, disciplined, and goal-directed approach* to designing and developing interactive computer systems that are **usable**, **efficient**, **effective**, and **satisfying** for end-users. It is a sub-discipline of Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) that applies empirical methods, measurable criteria, and iterative design techniques throughout the entire software development lifecycle.

According to **ISO 9241-11 (2018)**, usability is defined as:

> *"The extent to which a system, product, or service can be used by specified users to achieve specified goals with **effectiveness**, **efficiency**, and **satisfaction** in a specified context of use."*

According to **Jakob Nielsen (1993)**, usability is a **quality attribute** that evaluates how easy user interfaces are to use. Nielsen's framework, widely adopted in KTU pedagogy, identifies **five attributes of usability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Usability Engineering bridges *design intuition* and *empirical measurement*. It is the **engineering counterpart** of usability, replacing guesswork with **metrics, heuristics, and user testing**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are designing a **door handle** for a public building.

- A *non-usable* door has a flat plate — users push, pull, and struggle (Norman, 1988 — the famous "Norman Door").
- A *usable* door has a **push plate** on one side and a **pull handle** on the other — its design *communicates* the action.
- A **usability-engineered** door would have been: **prototyped, tested with real users, observed for errors, measured on time-to-exit, refined, and re-tested**.

Usability Engineering treats the **user interface** with the same scientific rigor an engineer applies to a **bridge**: measurements, tolerances, stress tests, and iterative refinement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** A usability engineer is to a UI designer what a *test pilot* is to an *aircraft designer*. Both are essential, neither is sufficient alone, and both rely on **empirical observation** rather than assumption.

---

## 1.3 The Five Usability Attributes (Nielsen, 1993)

| # | Attribute | Definition | Example Metric |
|---|-----------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Learnability** | How easy is it for users to accomplish basic tasks the first time they encounter the design? | Time to complete task on first attempt |
| 2 | **Efficiency** | Once users have learned the design, how quickly can they perform tasks? | Time per task after learning |
| 3 | **Memorability** | When users return to the design after a period of not using it, how easily can they re-establish proficiency? | Time to re-acquire skill after 30 days |
| 4 | **Errors** | How many errors do users make, how severe are these errors, and how easily can they recover? | Error count, error severity, recovery time |
| 5 | **Satisfaction** | How pleasant is the design to use? | Subjective ratings on Likert scales (1–7) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** All five attributes must be **measured quantitatively**. Usability Engineering is *evidence-based*, not opinion-based.

---

## 1.4 Goals of Usability Engineering

1. **Improve user productivity** — reduce time-to-task completion.
2. **Reduce training and support costs** — intuitive interfaces require less documentation.
3. **Increase user satisfaction** — leading to higher adoption rates.
4. **Reduce errors and rework** — preventing costly mistakes in safety-critical systems.
5. **Lower total cost of ownership (TCO)** — fewer help-desk tickets, less redesign.
6. **Achieve competitive advantage** — usable products dominate markets (e.g., iPhone's pinch-to-zoom).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Usability Engineering Lifecycle (Mayhew, 1999)

Deborah Mayhew's **Usability Engineering Lifecycle** is the canonical KTU-referenced framework. It comprises **three iterative phases**:

### Phase 1: Requirements Analysis
- **Task analysis** (GOMS, KLM, hierarchical task analysis)
- **User profiling** (skill level, frequency of use, domain expertise)
- **Usability goals specification** (target metrics for each of Nielsen's 5 attributes)
- **Platform constraints identification** (hardware, environment, accessibility)

### Phase 2: Design/Testing/Development
- **Style guide creation** (visual consistency rules)
- **Prototype construction** (low-fidelity → high-fidelity)
- **Iterative evaluation cycles** (heuristic evaluation → user testing → redesign)
- **Formative evaluation** (diagnostic, conducted *during* design)

### Phase 3: Installation
- **Summative evaluation** (validation against the originally specified usability goals)
- **Field deployment** with **field studies** / **contextual inquiry**
- **Long-term monitoring** of user satisfaction and error rates

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three phases?** The lifecycle ensures that usability is *designed in* (not bolted on), *measured early and often*, and *validated against original goals* — preventing the "we'll add usability later" anti-pattern.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes the **core quantitative metrics** used in Usability Engineering. These formulas are directly testable in KTU exams.

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Interpretation |
|--------|---------|------|----------------|
| **Task Completion Rate (TCR)** | $TCR = \dfrac{\text{Number of tasks completed successfully}}{\text{Total number of attempts}} \times 100\%$ | **Percentage (%)** | Higher is better; $< 70\%$ = critical usability failure |
| **Time on Task (TOT)** | $TOT = T_{\text{end}} - T_{\text{start}}$ | **Seconds / Minutes** | Lower is better (efficiency) |
| **Errors per Task (EPT)** | $EPT = \dfrac{\text{Number of user errors}}{\text{Number of tasks attempted}}$ | **Dimensionless ratio** | Lower is better |
| **Error Recovery Rate (ERR)** | $ERR = \dfrac{\text{Errors recovered without assistance}}{\text{Total errors}} \times 100\%$ | **Percentage (%)** | Higher is better |
| **System Usability Scale (SUS)** | $SUS = \sum_{i=1}^{10} \text{(score}_i \text{)} \times 2.5$ | **Score 0–100** | $\geq 68$ = acceptable, $\geq 80$ = excellent (Bangor et al., 2009) |
| **Learning Curve Slope** | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{\Delta \text{Time}}{\Delta \text{Trial}}$ | **Time per trial** | Negative slope = learning; steep negative = rapid learnability |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \% \text{Promoters} - \% \text{Detractors}$ | **Score $-100$ to $+100$** | Higher is better (satisfaction proxy) |
| **Cost-Benefit Ratio (CBR)** | $CBR = \dfrac{\text{Usability Engineering Cost}}{\text{Reduction in Support Cost}}$ | **Ratio (unitless)** | $CBR < 1$ = economically justified |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SUS Interpretation (Bangor, Kortum & Miller, 2009):**
> - **0 – 25**: Awful (unacceptable)
> - **26 – 51**: Poor
> - **52 – 68**: OK
> - **69 – 80**: Good
> - **81 – 100**: Excellent

---

## 2.3 Usability Heuristics — Nielsen's 10 Heuristics (1994)

These are **evaluation criteria** used in *heuristic evaluation* (expert-based, discount usability method).

| # | Heuristic | Plain Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Visibility of system status** | The system should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback within reasonable time. |
| 2 | **Match between system and the real world** | Speak the users' language, follow real-world conventions. |
| 3 | **User control and freedom** | Support "undo" and "redo" — users often choose system functions by mistake. |
| 4 | **Consistency and standards** | Follow platform conventions; same words/situations should mean the same thing. |
| 5 | **Error prevention** | Even better than good error messages is a careful design that prevents problems from occurring in the first place. |
| 6 | **Recognition rather than recall** | Minimize the user's memory load; make objects, actions, and options visible. |
| 7 | **Flexibility and efficiency of use** | Accelerators (unseen by the novice) may often speed up the interaction for the expert user. |
| 8 | **Aesthetic and minimalist design** | Dialogues should not contain information that is irrelevant or rarely needed. |
| 9 | **Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors** | Error messages should be in plain language, precisely indicate the problem, and constructively suggest a solution. |
| 10 | **Help and documentation** | Provide easily searchable, task-focused help. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Do **NOT** list only 3–4 heuristics and call it complete. KTU valuation requires **all 10** to be present for full marks (typically 7–10 marks question).

---

## 2.4 Usability Evaluation Methods — Classification

```
Usability Evaluation
├── Empirical (User-based)
│   ├── User Testing (Hallway / Field / Remote)
│   ├── Think-Aloud Protocol
│   ├── Surveys & Questionnaires (SUS, QUIS)
│   └── Interviews
├── Analytical (Expert-based)
│   ├── Heuristic Evaluation (Nielsen)
│   ├── Cognitive Walkthrough (Wharton)
│   ├── Pluralistic Walkthrough
│   ├── Feature Inspection
│   └── Consistency Inspection
└── Model-based
    ├── GOMS (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection)
    ├── KLM (Keystroke-Level Model)
    └── ACT-R (Adaptive Control of Thought–Rational)
```

---

## 2.5 The "Discount Usability" Concept (Nielsen, 1993)

Nielsen advocated for **lean, fast, iterative methods** that fit into real engineering budgets. These are called *discount* methods because they trade some rigor for **speed and low cost**:

1. **Heuristic evaluation** — 3–5 experts; ~$0; 2 hours.
2. **User testing** — 5 users; ~$0–500; 1 day.
3. **Simplified thinking aloud** — combined with user testing.
4. **Formal usability inspection** — paper prototype + expert walkthrough.
5. **Questionnaires** — large-N statistical power at low cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "5 users"?** Nielsen's empirical research showed that **5 users** find ~85% of usability problems. Testing 15 users raises the rate to ~95%, but at 3× the cost. The diminishing returns justify the small sample.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Usability Engineering

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Aviation** | Cockpit displays — error prevention is life-critical |
| **Medical devices** | Infusion pumps, ventilators — memorability under stress |
| **E-commerce** | Checkout flow optimization — TCR directly affects revenue |
| **Banking ATMs** | Universal accessibility — learnability for first-time users |
| **Mobile apps** | Touch targets, gesture design — efficiency on small screens |
| **Enterprise software** | SAP/Oracle UIs — efficiency for power users |
| **Automotive** | Infotainment systems — minimising driver distraction (ISO 26262) |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Computing the System Usability Scale (SUS)

The **SUS** is a 10-item Likert-scale questionnaire (1 = Strongly Disagree, 5 = Strongly Agree).

**Step 1: Odd-numbered items (1, 3, 5, 7, 9)** — subtract 1 from the user response.

$$\text{Odd Item Contribution} = \sum_{i \in \{1,3,5,7,9\}} (\text{User Score}_i - 1)$$

**Step 2: Even-numbered items (2, 4, 6, 8, 10)** — subtract the user response from 5.

$$\text{Even Item Contribution} = \sum_{i \in \{2,4,6,8,10\}} (5 - \text{User Score}_i)$$

**Step 3: Sum both contributions, then multiply by 2.5.**

$$SUS = \left( \text{Odd Item Contribution} + \text{Even Item Contribution} \right) \times 2.5$$

---

### Illustrative Numerical Evaluation

Suppose 5 users respond to a SUS questionnaire for a new banking app. Their scores are:

| User | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Q5 | Q6 | Q7 | Q8 | Q9 | Q10 |
|------|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|-----|
| U1   | 4  | 2  | 5  | 1  | 4  | 2  | 4  | 2  | 5  | 1   |
| U2   | 3  | 2  | 4  | 2  | 5  | 2  | 3  | 3  | 4  | 2   |
| U3   | 5  | 1  | 5  | 1  | 5  | 1  | 5  | 1  | 5  | 1   |
| U4   | 4  | 3  | 4  | 2  | 3  | 3  | 4  | 2  | 4  | 2   |
| U5   | 3  | 2  | 4  | 3  | 4  | 2  | 3  | 3  | 4  | 3   |

**Step 4: Compute odd-item contributions for User 1:**

$$C_{odd} = (4-1) + (5-1) + (4-1) + (4-1) + (5-1) = 3+4+3+3+4 = 17$$

**Step 5: Compute even-item contributions for User 1:**

$$C_{even} = (5-2) + (5-1) + (5-2) + (5-2) + (5-1) = 3+4+3+3+4 = 17$$

**Step 6: Compute User 1's SUS score:**

$$SUS_{U1} = (17 + 17) \times 2.5 = 34 \times 2.5 = 85$$

**Step 7: Repeat for all 5 users and average.**

| User | Odd Sum | Even Sum | SUS |
|------|---------|----------|-----|
| U1   | 17      | 17       | **85** |
| U2   | 14      | 14       | **70** |
| U3   | 20      | 20       | **100**|
| U4   | 14      | 15       | **72.5**|
| U5   | 12      | 14       | **65** |

**Step 8: Compute group mean SUS:**

$$\overline{SUS} = \frac{85 + 70 + 100 + 72.5 + 65}{5} = \frac{392.5}{5} = 78.5$$

**Interpretation:** A mean SUS of **78.5** places the banking app in the **"Good"** band (69–80), but below the 80-point "Excellent" threshold. Recommended action: conduct targeted heuristic evaluation focusing on Nielsen's heuristics 3 (User Control) and 8 (Aesthetic Design).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Keystroke-Level Model (KLM) Prediction

**KLM (Card, Moran & Newell, 1983)** is a *predictive* usability model. It decomposes a task into primitive operators, each with an empirically measured execution time.

### KLM Operator Times (Standard Set)

| Operator | Symbol | Time (seconds) | Description |
|----------|--------|----------------|-------------|
| Keystroke | **K** | 0.28 (expert) / 0.20 (fast) / 0.12 (fastest) | Press a single key |
| Pointing (mouse) | **P** | 1.10 | Move mouse to a target |
| Homing (hand movement) | **H** | 0.40 | Move hand between keyboard and mouse |
| Drawing (straight line) | **D** | variable (e.g., 0.9 for 1 cm) | Mouse drag for straight segment |
| Mental act | **M** | 1.35 | Cognitive processing |
| System response | **R** | variable | Wait for system feedback |
| Button press | **B** | 0.10 | Press/release a mouse button |

### Task: "Save the current document as 'Report.docx' using Ctrl+S, type the filename, click Save."

**Step 1: Decompose the task into operators:**

```
Task: Save Document as 'Report.docx'
 1. Press Ctrl key     → K
 2. Press S key        → K
 3. Release keys       → (B + B)
 4. System opens dialog → R (assume 1.5s)
 5. User reads dialog  → M (1.35s)
 6. Click filename box → P (1.10s) + B (0.10s)
 7. Type 'Report'      → K × 6
 8. Click Save button  → P (1.10s) + B (0.10s)
```

**Step 2: Apply the KLM equation (sum of all operators):**

$$T_{\text{task}} = \sum K + \sum P + \sum H + \sum M + \sum R + \sum B + \sum D$$

**Step 3: Plug in numerical values (using expert times):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{task}} &= (2 \times 0.28) + (2 \times 0.10) + 1.5 + 1.35 + (1.10 + 0.10) + (6 \times 0.20) + (1.10 + 0.10) \\
&= 0.56 + 0.20 + 1.5 + 1.35 + 1.20 + 1.20 + 1.20 \\
&= 7.21 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret the prediction.**

A KLM-predicted time of **7.21 seconds** serves as a **baseline**. Actual user times (measured empirically) can then be compared:
- If actual < 7.21 s → system is faster than predicted (good).
- If actual > 7.21 s → the interface introduces **cognitive overload** (each extra *M* = +1.35 s) or **inefficient targeting** (each extra *P* = +1.10 s).

> [!NOTE]
> **KLM is deterministic**, not statistical. It predicts the time for an *expert user with no errors* following an *optimal method*. Use it for benchmarking, not for novice behavior.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Task Completion Rate (TCR) Analysis

**Scenario:** 40 users attempt a "Reset Password" workflow in a web application. 28 succeed, 10 fail, 2 partially complete.

**Step 1: Classify outcomes.**

- **Successes** (S) = 28
- **Failures** (F) = 10
- **Partial** (P) = 2 → Treat as failures for binary TCR.

**Step 2: Apply the formula.**

$$TCR = \frac{\text{Successes}}{\text{Total Attempts}} \times 100\%$$

$$TCR = \frac{28}{40} \times 100\% = 70\%$$

**Step 3: Interpret against the KTU benchmark scale.**

| TCR Range | Verdict | Action |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| $\geq 90\%$ | Excellent | Document and maintain |
| 70–89% | Acceptable | Minor refinements |
| 50–69% | Poor | Mandatory redesign |
| $< 50\%$ | Critical | Architecture-level overhaul |

**Result:** 70% is the **lower bound of acceptable** — the workflow requires iterative refinement.

**Step 4: Cross-tabulate with errors.**

Suppose further that the 10 failures generated 14 distinct error events. Compute:

$$EPT = \frac{14}{40} = 0.35 \text{ errors/task}$$

This is **moderate**. A target of $< 0.1$ errors/task would be required for a safety-critical system.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing an Evaluation Method

| Method | Cost | Time | Users Required | Type of Data | When to Use |
|--------|------|------|----------------|--------------|-------------|
| Heuristic Evaluation | Very Low | 2–4 hours | 0 (experts) | Qualitative (list of problems) | Early design, paper prototypes |
| Cognitive Walkthrough | Low | 1–2 hours | 0 (experts) | Qualitative (step-by-step) | First-time-use scenarios |
| User Testing (Hallway) | Medium | 1 day | 5+ (any) | Quantitative + Qualitative | Mid-fidelity prototype |
| Remote Unmoderated Test | Low | Hours | 100+ | Quantitative (large N) | Final design validation |
| Survey (SUS/QUIS) | Very Low | Days | 30+ | Quantitative (Likert) | Pre/post comparison |
| Field Study | High | Weeks | 5–20 | Qualitative (rich context) | Post-deployment |
| KLM/GOMS | Low | Hours | 0 (analyst) | Quantitative (predicted time) | Estimating expert performance |
| Eye-Tracking | High | Days | 10–30 | Quantitative (fixation data) | Visual attention studies |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define Usability Engineering. List any three of Nielsen's five usability attributes with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Usability Engineering is a systematic and iterative approach to designing interactive systems that focuses on making products **learnable, efficient, memorable, error-tolerant, and satisfying** for end-users, by applying measurable usability criteria and empirical evaluation throughout the development lifecycle.

**Three of Nielsen's five attributes (2 marks = ½ + ½ + ½ + ½):**

1. **Learnability:** How easily a user can accomplish basic tasks on first encountering the system.
2. **Efficiency:** The speed at which experienced users can complete tasks once the system is learned.
3. **Memorability:** The ease with which users can re-establish proficiency after a period of non-use.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Errors, Satisfaction.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**What is Heuristic Evaluation? Mention any two of Nielsen's heuristics used in this method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Heuristic Evaluation is a *discount usability engineering method* (Nielsen, 1990) in which a small set of evaluators (typically **3–5 experts**) examine a user interface and judge its compliance with a set of recognised **usability principles called heuristics**. The method is cheap, fast, requires no users, and is most useful in the early stages of design.

**Two heuristics (½ + ½):**

1. **Visibility of system status:** The system should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback.
2. **Match between system and real world:** The system should speak the users' language, with words, phrases and concepts familiar to the user.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Error prevention, Recognition rather than recall, Help and documentation, etc.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Rule (ESE Module 2 — 14 Marks):** Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 📌 QUESTION A — OPTION 1 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the **Usability Engineering Lifecycle** proposed by Deborah Mayhew. Describe all three phases in detail with appropriate diagrams. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A team conducted a usability test on a new mobile application. The SUS scores from **10 users** are:

**78, 62, 85, 71, 68, 90, 55, 74, 81, 66**

Compute the **mean SUS score** and interpret the result according to Bangor's adjective rating scale. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(a) (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** The **Usability Engineering Lifecycle** (Mayhew, 1999) is a structured methodology that integrates usability activities into the conventional software development process. It consists of **three iterative phases** with feedback loops between them.

**Phase 1: Requirements Analysis (2 marks):**

This phase establishes the foundation of usability work. It involves:

- **Task Analysis:** Decomposing user goals into sub-tasks (using HTA, GOMS, or KLM).
- **User Profiling:** Identifying user characteristics — skill level (novice/intermediate/expert), frequency of use (first-time/occasional/frequent), and domain expertise.
- **Usability Goal Specification:** Defining *measurable* targets for each of Nielsen's 5 attributes. Example: *"90% of first-time users shall complete the checkout flow in under 3 minutes with no more than 2 errors."*
- **Platform Constraints:** Identifying hardware, environmental, and accessibility constraints (e.g., screen size, latency, ADA compliance).

**Phase 2: Design / Testing / Development (2 marks):**

This is the *iterative core* of the lifecycle.

- **Style Guide Creation:** Establishing visual and interaction consistency rules.
- **Prototype Construction:** Building low-fidelity (paper) → mid-fidelity (wireframe) → high-fidelity (functional) prototypes.
- **Formative Evaluation:** Conducting heuristic evaluation, cognitive walkthroughs, and small-N user tests (typically 5 users per iteration).
- **Iterative Redesign:** Each evaluation cycle produces findings that are prioritised and addressed in the next prototype.

**Phase 3: Installation (1 mark):**

- **Summative Evaluation:** Validating the final product against the originally specified usability goals.
- **Field Deployment & Field Studies:** Observing real users in the actual context of use (contextual inquiry).
- **Long-term Monitoring:** Tracking satisfaction, error rates, and adoption over time.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Mayhew's lifecycle ensures usability is treated as a **first-class design constraint**, not a post-hoc polish, and that measurable goals drive every iteration.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(b) (7 marks)

**Given data (N = 10):**
78, 62, 85, 71, 68, 90, 55, 74, 81, 66

**Step 1: Compute the sum of all SUS scores (2 marks):**

$$\sum x_i = 78 + 62 + 85 + 71 + 68 + 90 + 55 + 74 + 81 + 66$$

**Step-by-step accumulation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= 78 \\
S_2 &= 78 + 62 = 140 \\
S_3 &= 140 + 85 = 225 \\
S_4 &= 225 + 71 = 296 \\
S_5 &= 296 + 68 = 364 \\
S_6 &= 364 + 90 = 454 \\
S_7 &= 454 + 55 = 509 \\
S_8 &= 509 + 74 = 583 \\
S_9 &= 583 + 81 = 664 \\
S_{10} &= 664 + 66 = 730
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the arithmetic mean (2 marks):**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x_i}{N} = \frac{730}{10} = 73.0$$

**Step 3: Interpret using Bangor's Adjective Rating Scale (2 marks):**

| SUS Range | Adjective Rating |
|-----------|------------------|
| $> 80$   | Excellent        |
| $68$ to $80$ | Good         |
| $51$ to $67$ | OK           |
| $26$ to $50$ | Poor         |
| $\leq 25$  | Awful           |

Since $\bar{x} = 73.0$, the mobile application falls in the **"GOOD"** band (between 68 and 80).

**Step 4: Optional — Compute standard deviation for completeness (1 mark):**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{N}}$$

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ |
|-------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 78    | 5               | 25                  |
| 62    | −11             | 121                 |
| 85    | 12              | 144                 |
| 71    | −2              | 4                   |
| 68    | −5              | 25                  |
| 90    | 17              | 289                 |
| 55    | −18             | 324                 |
| 74    | 1               | 1                   |
| 81    | 8               | 64                  |
| 66    | −7              | 49                  |

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 25 + 121 + 144 + 4 + 25 + 289 + 324 + 1 + 64 + 49 = 1046$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1046}{10}} = \sqrt{104.6} \approx 10.23$$

**Final Verdict (1 mark):** The mean SUS of **73.0** with a standard deviation of **±10.23** indicates the application is **Good** but exhibits significant variability — suggesting **inconsistent user experience** across the user base. Recommended action: perform a **heuristic evaluation** to identify which user groups (e.g., novices) are dragging the average down, and target those flow paths for redesign.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B — OPTION 2 (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q. (a)** Explain **Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics** in detail. Why is heuristic evaluation considered a "discount usability" method? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An e-commerce website's "Add to Cart" workflow was tested with **50 users**. **35 users** completed the task successfully, **10 users** failed, and **5 users** partially completed. The errors logged were **22 in total**, of which **18 were recovered without assistance**. Compute:
**(i)** Task Completion Rate **(ii)** Errors per Task **(iii)** Error Recovery Rate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(a) (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Jakob Nielsen's **10 usability heuristics** (1994) are a set of broad, experience-based principles that form the most widely used framework for **heuristic evaluation** — a discount usability method in which 3–5 expert evaluators inspect an interface for compliance with these rules.

**All 10 Heuristics (4 marks = ½ mark each for correctly stated name + ½ mark for explanation; allocate 4 marks across 10 heuristics; key ones below):**

1. **Visibility of system status:** The system should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback within reasonable time. *(Example: progress bar during file upload.)*

2. **Match between system and the real world:** The system should speak the users' language, with words, phrases, and concepts familiar to the user, rather than system-oriented terms. Follow real-world conventions. *(Example: a trash-can icon for "delete".)*

3. **User control and freedom:** Users often choose system functions by mistake and need a clearly marked "emergency exit" to leave the unwanted state — support **undo** and **redo**. *(Example: Ctrl+Z in word processors.)*

4. **Consistency and standards:** Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing. Follow platform conventions. *(Example: same icon for "save" across all screens.)*

5. **Error prevention:** Even better than good error messages is a careful design that **prevents problems from occurring in the first place**. *(Example: disabling the "Submit" button until all required fields are filled.)*

6. **Recognition rather than recall:** Minimize the user's memory load by making objects, actions, and options **visible**. The user should not have to remember information from one part of the dialogue to another. *(Example: showing recently visited pages in a menu.)*

7. **Flexibility and efficiency of use:** Accelerators — often unseen by novices — can speed up the interaction for expert users. Allow users to tailor frequent actions. *(Example: keyboard shortcuts for power users.)*

8. **Aesthetic and minimalist design:** Dialogues should not contain information that is irrelevant or rarely needed. Every extra unit of information in a dialogue competes with the relevant units and diminishes their relative visibility. *(Example: clean checkout forms without distracting ads.)*

9. **Help users recognize, diagnose, and recover from errors:** Error messages should be in **plain language** (no error codes), precisely indicate the problem, and **constructively suggest a solution**. *(Example: "Incorrect password. Please try again or click 'Forgot password' to reset.")*
   
   *(Note: the prompt says "recognize" — a known typo in the original Nielsen 1994 paper.)*

10. **Help and documentation:** Even though it is better if the system can be used without documentation, help and documentation may be necessary. They should be **easy to search**, focused on the user's task, list concrete steps, and not be too large. *(Example: searchable in-app help.)*

**Why heuristic evaluation is a "discount usability" method (2 marks):**

Nielsen coined the term **"discount usability"** to describe methods that are **fast, cheap, and easy to learn** — trading some empirical rigor for **practicality within real engineering budgets**.

- **Cost:** No user recruitment; only 3–5 expert evaluators needed.
- **Time:** A single evaluator can complete a heuristic review in **1–2 hours**.
- **Expertise required:** No specialised lab equipment or trained moderators.
- **Iterative use:** Can be applied at *any* stage of design, including paper prototypes.

The trade-off is that heuristic evaluation catches **common, known design problems** but may miss **domain-specific or task-specific issues** that only real users would surface. Hence it is often used in **combination** with empirical user testing in a layered, iterative approach.

---

#### MODEL ANSWER — Q(b) (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- Total users = **50**
- Successes (S) = **35**
- Failures (F) = **10**
- Partial (P) = **5** (counted as failures for binary TCR)
- Total errors = **22**
- Recovered without assistance = **18**

---

**(i) Task Completion Rate (TCR) (2 marks):**

Treating partial completions as failures:

$$TCR = \frac{\text{Successful completions}}{\text{Total attempts}} \times 100\%$$

$$TCR = \frac{35}{50} \times 100\% = 70\%$$

**Interpretation (½ mark):** 70% is the **lower boundary of acceptable usability**. The workflow needs refinement.

---

**(ii) Errors per Task (EPT) (2 marks):**

$$EPT = \frac{\text{Total errors observed}}{\text{Total tasks attempted}}$$

$$EPT = \frac{22}{50} = 0.44 \text{ errors per task}$$

**Interpretation (½ mark):** 0.44 errors/task is **moderate-to-high**; an acceptable target is typically **< 0.1 errors/task** for routine workflows.

---

**(iii) Error Recovery Rate (ERR) (2 marks):**

$$ERR = \frac{\text{Errors recovered without external help}}{\text{Total errors}} \times 100\%$$

$$ERR = \frac{18}{22} \times 100\% = 81.82\%$$

**Interpretation (½ mark):** 81.82% is **good recovery** — the system supports users in self-correcting, indicating reasonable error-handling design (e.g., undo, clear error messages).

---

**Summary Table (½ mark):**

| Metric | Value | Verdict |
|--------|-------|---------|
| TCR | 70% | Acceptable (lower bound) |
| EPT | 0.44 | Moderate — needs improvement |
| ERR | 81.82% | Good |

**Conclusion (½ mark):** The "Add to Cart" workflow has **good error recovery** but **insufficient task completion**. Recommended action: conduct a cognitive walkthrough to identify the step at which most users fail (e.g., a hidden coupon-code field causing abandonment).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 2):**
> 1. **Confusing "Discount Usability" with "Low Quality":** It does NOT mean inferior — it means *fast, cheap, and good-enough*. Students lose 2 marks for implying discount = bad.
> 2. **Listing only 5 of Nielsen's 10 heuristics:** KTU requires **all 10** to be present for full marks on that 7-mark sub-part.
> 3. **Forgetting to treat partial completions:** In TCR calculations, partial completions are conventionally **counted as failures**. Omitting this nuance loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up SUS interpretation bands:** Students frequently quote "50 = average". The correct value per Bangor et al. is **68 = acceptable** and **80 = excellent**.
> 5. **Confusing KLM with GOMS:** KLM is a *predictive time model*; GOMS is a *cognitive task-decomposition theory*. They are related but **not identical**.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Usability Engineering**

- ✅ **Usability Engineering** = systematic, measurement-driven approach to making UIs effective, efficient, and satisfying.
- ✅ **ISO 9241-11** defines usability through three pillars: **Effectiveness, Efficiency, Satisfaction**.
- ✅ **Nielsen's 5 Attributes:** **LEMS** — **L**earnability, **E**fficiency, **M**emorability, **E**rrors, **S**atisfaction.
- ✅ **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics:** memorise all 10 — KTU exam almost always tests 7–8 of them.
- ✅ **Discount Usability Methods:** heuristic evaluation, hallway testing, simplified thinking-aloud, paper prototypes.
- ✅ **5 users ≈ 85% of problems** (Nielsen's empirical finding) — diminishing returns after 15 users.
- ✅ **Mayhew's Usability Engineering Lifecycle:** 3 phases — Requirements → Design/Test/Dev → Installation. Each phase has feedback loops.
- ✅ **Formative** = during design (diagnostic); **Summative** = after design (validation against goals).
- ✅ **SUS ≥ 68 = acceptable**, **≥ 80 = excellent** (Bangor's adjective rating scale).
- ✅ **TCR ≥ 70% = acceptable**, **< 50% = critical failure**.
- ✅ **KLM** uses operators K, P, H, D, M, R, B with standard times (e.g., K = 0.28s, P = 1.10s, M = 1.35s for experts).
- ✅ **Heuristic Evaluation** is expert-based, no users needed; **User Testing** is empirical, requires real users.
- ✅ **Norman Doors** (1988) = the canonical example of a usability failure.
- ✅ **Nielsen (1993) and Mayhew (1999)** are the two KTU-prescribed authors; expect direct reference to their frameworks.
- ✅ **Three evaluation paradigms:** **Empirical (user-based), Analytical (expert-based), Model-based (GOMS/KLM).**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prototyping in Practice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT614 - INTRODUCTION TO HUMAN MACHINE INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Prototyping in Practice

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prototyping in HMI** is the iterative process of building early, incomplete, and exploratory representations of an interactive system to **test, communicate, refine, and validate** design ideas with users and stakeholders before committing to full-scale development.

In the KTU 2024 HMI syllabus context, prototyping is positioned as a **core User-Centered Design (UCD) activity** that bridges the gap between abstract conceptual design (sketches, personas, scenarios) and concrete implementation (coded interfaces). A prototype is a *throwaway* or *evolutionary* artifact that embodies a design hypothesis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Definition to Memorize**
> *"A prototype is a concrete, usable, though incomplete, representation of a design idea. It supports communication, feedback, and learning through doing."*

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Bold Constants & Standard Metrics)

- **Fidelity** — The degree to which a prototype resembles the final product in look, feel, and behavior. Measured on a scale: **Low → Mid → High**.
- **Throwaway Prototype** — Built to answer specific questions, then discarded. *(Synonym: Rapid Prototype)*
- **Evolutionary Prototype** — Continually refined and grown into the final system.
- **Horizontal Prototype** — Provides a wide surface but limited depth (many screens, shallow interactions).
- **Vertical Prototype** — Provides deep functionality in a narrow slice of the system (one feature, fully working).
- **Wizard of Oz (WOz)** — A hidden human operator simulates the system's intelligence to test user reactions.
- **Five-Second Test** — A usability metric exposing a design for 5 seconds to measure first-impression comprehension.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Architectural Blueprint Model

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: Building a House**
> Before laying a single brick, an architect builds a **miniature scale model** of the house. The client walks through the model, says *"I want the kitchen window larger,"* or *"Move the staircase here."* The architect iterates on the model — never touching the real house.
> That miniature model = a **Prototype**.
> The real house = the **Final HMI Product**.

Similarly, in HMI:
- The **client** = the **user** (who provides feedback)
- The **architect** = the **interaction designer**
- The **miniature model** = a **UI prototype** (testable but not final)
- **Moving the staircase** = **iterative redesign** based on usability data

### 1.4 Why Prototyping Matters in HMI

- **Risk Reduction:** Catching design flaws early is **10×–100× cheaper** than fixing them post-deployment.
- **Communication:** A working prototype conveys intent better than a 50-page specification document.
- **User Involvement:** Enables *participatory design* — users can react to something tangible.
- **Learning:** Designers discover edge cases, mental model mismatches, and interaction gaps.
- **Negotiation:** Stakeholders (management, marketing) align on scope when they *see* the product.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Theoretical Foundation: Prototyping in the Design Lifecycle

Prototyping sits inside the **Iterative Design Cycle** (sometimes called the **Star Lifecycle** by Hix & Hartson). The cycle has four phases that repeat:

1. **Design** — Hypothesize a solution (sketch, storyboard, scenario).
2. **Prototype** — Build a tangible, testable artifact embodying the hypothesis.
3. **Evaluate** — Users interact with the prototype; designers observe, measure, gather feedback.
4. **Reflect & Refine** — Analyze feedback, identify problems, return to Step 1 with new design.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Concept**: The iterative nature of prototyping aligns with **Norman’s Theory of Action** — the user forms an **Execution** intent, the prototype's interface supports it, and feedback confirms or violates the **Evaluation** stage. A prototype is essentially a *hypothesis-testing machine* for the Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation.

### 2.2 Classification of Prototypes (The Why & The How)

Prototypes are classified along **three orthogonal dimensions**:

#### A) By **Fidelity** (Resolution / Realism)

| Dimension | Low-Fidelity | Mid-Fidelity | High-Fidelity |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|----------------|
| **Look & Feel** | Sketches, paper, sticky notes | Wireframes in software, grayscale mockups | Pixel-perfect, branded, interactive |
| **Interactivity** | None / static | Click-through, no real logic | Fully functional, database-connected |
| **Cost** | Negligible | Low | High |
| **Speed to Build** | Minutes–Hours | Hours–Days | Days–Weeks |
| **Best For** | Early brainstorming, concept validation | Layout testing, IA validation | Final pre-launch usability tests, demos |
| **Risk of Bias** | Low (users forgive rough) | Medium | High (users treat as final) |

#### B) By **Purpose / Lifecycle Role**

| Prototype Type | Purpose | KTU Example Context |
|----------------|---------|---------------------|
| **Throwaway / Rapid** | Test a specific question, then discard | Paper mockup to test button labels |
| **Evolutionary** | Grows into the final system | HTML/CSS prototype that becomes production |
| **Incremental** | Different modules prototyped separately | Login module + Dashboard module built independently, then merged |
| **Extreme** | Drives the entire system architecture | Used in XP/Agile, one per user story |

#### C) By **Scope**

| Scope Type | What is Covered | Use Case |
|------------|----------------|----------|
| **Horizontal** | Wide breadth, shallow depth | Show all screens of an app with click-through |
| **Vertical** | Narrow breadth, deep depth | Fully working checkout flow in an e-commerce site |

### 2.3 Common Prototyping Techniques (Why + How)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield List — Memorize All Five**

1. **Paper Prototyping** (Low-Fi, Throwaway)
   - *Why:* Fastest, cheapest, forces discussion over decoration.
   - *How:* Hand-draw screens on paper, "click" by swapping sheets. A human "computer" simulates the system's response.

2. **Wireframing** (Low/Mid-Fi)
   - *Why:* Establishes layout, information architecture, hierarchy.
   - *How:* Tools like Balsamiq, Figma, Moqups produce grayscale structural mockups.

3. **Storyboarding** (Conceptual, Low-Fi)
   - *Why:* Captures user journey and emotional context, not just screens.
   - *How:* Sequence of panels (like a comic) showing a user performing a task over time.

4. **Wizard of Oz Prototyping** (Mid-Fi, Behavioral)
   - *Why:* Test intelligent/speech interfaces without building real AI.
   - *How:* Hidden operator responds to user input as if the system were intelligent.

5. **Interactive / Click-Through Prototyping** (Mid-to-High Fi)
   - *Why:* Test navigation, transitions, micro-interactions.
   - *How:* Tools like Figma, InVision, Axure, Adobe XD link screens with hotspots and animations.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Prototyping

- **Mobile UI Design:** Figma prototypes reduce app store rejection rates by surfacing usability flaws pre-submission.
- **Automotive HMI:** Steering-wheel control prototypes tested in driving simulators before production.
- **Medical Device Interfaces:** FDA increasingly requires **Human Factors Validation** with high-fidelity prototypes.
- **Industrial Control Panels:** SCADA system prototypes tested with plant operators to prevent operator-induced errors.
- **Voice Assistants (Alexa, Google Home):** Wizard of Oz prototypes are still the industry standard for testing new intents.

### 2.5 KTU Cheat Sheet — The "Decide-Your-Prototype" Quick Reference

| **If your design question is…** | **Use this prototype** |
|---------------------------------|------------------------|
| "Is this concept even worth pursuing?" | Low-fi paper sketch |
| "Where should this button go?" | Wireframe + 5-second test |
| "Does the navigation flow make sense?" | Mid-fi click-through (Figma) |
| "Will users enjoy using this in context?" | High-fi interactive + field test |
| "Can users understand our AI's behavior?" | Wizard of Oz |
| "Will this scale to the real product?" | Vertical evolutionary prototype |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Prototyping Process — Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation

Below is the full analytical derivation of the prototyping process, from hypothesis to refined design. Each step is justified by HMI theory.

---

**Step 1 — Identify the Design Question (Hypothesis Formation)**

Every prototype exists to test exactly **one** falsifiable claim. Vague questions yield useless prototypes.

$$
H_0: \text{Users will complete checkout in under 60 seconds with this layout.}
$$

$$
H_1: \text{Users will require more than 60 seconds, or fail.}
$$

> **Logic:** The null hypothesis (H₀) is the designer's claim. The prototype is an experiment to refute or support it.

---

**Step 2 — Choose the Appropriate Fidelity Level**

Use the **Fidelity Selection Rule**:

$$
F = \begin{cases} \text{Low} & \text{if } S \in [0, 0.33) \\ \text{Mid} & \text{if } S \in [0.33, 0.66) \\ \text{High} & \text{if } S \in [0.66, 1.0] \end{cases}
$$

where $S$ is the **Stage Score**, computed as:

$$
S = 0.4 \cdot \text{Maturity} + 0.3 \cdot \text{QuestionPrecision} + 0.3 \cdot \text{StakeholderRisk}
$$

**Line-by-line explanation of S:**

- **Maturity** (0 to 1): How mature is the concept? 0 = brand-new idea, 1 = well-defined spec.
- **QuestionPrecision** (0 to 1): How specific is the test question? 0 = vague, 1 = measurable.
- **StakeholderRisk** (0 to 1): How high is the cost of being wrong? 0 = internal feature, 1 = public launch.

> **Logic:** If the concept is new, the question is vague, and the risk is low → go Low-Fi (cheaper, faster). If risk is high, the question is measurable, and maturity is high → go High-Fi.

---

**Step 3 — Build the Prototype**

Apply the **Prototype Construction Principle**: *Build only what you need to test.*

$$
\text{BuildScope} = \text{HypothesisScope} \cup \text{NecessaryContext}
$$

> **Logic:** Add just enough surrounding context that the user understands the task, but no more. Extra polish biases feedback toward aesthetic opinions rather than functional ones.

---

**Step 4 — Conduct the Evaluation**

Run the prototype through at least **3 evaluation methods** (triangulation):

$$
\text{Confidence} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - c_i)
$$

where $c_i$ is the confidence gained from each evaluation method (e.g., heuristic evaluation $c_1 = 0.3$, user testing $c_2 = 0.6$, A/B test $c_3 = 0.5$).

**Numerical Example:**

$$
\text{Confidence} = 1 - (1 - 0.3)(1 - 0.6)(1 - 0.5) = 1 - (0.7)(0.4)(0.5) = 1 - 0.14 = 0.86
$$

> **Logic:** Each independent evaluation method increases overall confidence multiplicatively. Three moderate methods (0.3, 0.6, 0.5) yield 86% confidence — better than relying on a single 0.7 method.

---

**Step 5 — Analyze, Reflect, and Iterate**

Compute the **Issue Density** to decide whether to iterate:

$$
\text{IssueDensity} = \frac{\text{Number of Usability Issues Found}}{\text{Number of Users Tested}}
$$

**Decision rule (Nielsen's classic heuristic):**

$$
\text{NextAction} = \begin{cases} \text{Continue Testing} & \text{if } \text{IssueDensity} > 0.5 \\ \text{Iterate Design} & \text{if } 0.1 < \text{IssueDensity} \leq 0.5 \\ \text{Move to Development} & \text{if } \text{IssueDensity} \leq 0.1 \end{cases}
$$

> **Logic:** Nielsen's research showed that ~5 users reveal ~85% of usability issues. If issues keep appearing past 5 users with high density, the design is fundamentally flawed — iterate. If density drops, the design is maturing.

---

**Step 6 — Refine or Discard (Throwaway vs Evolutionary Decision)**

Apply the **Survival Criterion**:

$$
\text{Survival} = \begin{cases} \text{Evolutionary} & \text{if } \text{CodeReuseRatio} > 0.6 \text{ and } \text{ArchitectureStable} = \text{True} \\ \text{Throwaway} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> **Logic:** If more than 60% of the prototype's structure can carry forward and the architecture is stable, evolve it. Otherwise, throw it away and start fresh on the next iteration — keeping a flawed prototype alive leads to "prototype debt."

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Prototype Selection Decision Table

| **Design Context** | **Recommended Fidelity** | **Recommended Technique** | **Evaluation Method** | **Expected Iterations** |
|--------------------|--------------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Brand-new mobile app concept | Low → Mid | Paper → Figma wireframe | Hallway testing | 3–5 |
| Dashboard for power users | Mid → High | Interactive Figma | Task-based usability test | 2–4 |
| Voice interface for elderly users | Mid (WOz) | Wizard of Oz | Think-aloud protocol | 4–6 |
| Medical infusion pump UI | High | Functional HTML/CSS prototype | Summative HF validation | 5–8 |
| E-commerce checkout flow | Mid → High | Figma click-through + live A/B | Conversion funnel analytics | 2–3 |

> **Logic of the Matrix:** The further right and down you go, the higher the user-safety and revenue stakes, demanding higher fidelity and more rigorous evaluation.

### 3.3 The "Throwaway vs Evolutionary" Trade-Off Derivation

Two opposing forces govern whether to keep or discard a prototype:

$$
\text{KeepValue} = \alpha \cdot \text{EffortInvested} + \beta \cdot \text{ArchitectureFit}
$$

$$
\text{DiscardValue} = \gamma \cdot \text{TechnicalDebt} + \delta \cdot \text{ConceptualContradictions}
$$

where typically $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 0.6$, $\gamma = 0.5$, $\delta = 0.5$.

**Decision rule:**

$$
\text{Action} = \begin{cases} \text{Evolve the Prototype} & \text{if } \text{KeepValue} > \text{DiscardValue} \\ \text{Discard and Rebuild} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> **Logic:** A prototype that has high invested effort AND a clean architecture is worth evolving. If it accumulated technical debt (quick hacks) or has logical contradictions with the new design direction, discard it. The cost of carrying bad architecture forward exceeds the cost of rebuilding.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Prototyping Lifecycle (Iterative Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Design Question and Hypothesis"] --> B["Choose Fidelity Level Low Mid High"]
    B --> C["Build Prototype Paper Wireframe Interactive WOz"]
    C --> D["Conduct Evaluation with Users"]
    D --> E["Measure Issue Density and Confidence"]
    E --> F{"Issue Density greater than 0 point 5"}
    F -->|Yes| G["Iterate Design Return to Step 1"]
    G --> A
    F -->|No| H{"Confidence greater than 0 point 8"}
    H -->|No| D
    H -->|Yes| I{"Survival Criterion Met"}
    I -->|Yes| J["Evolve to Final Product"]
    I -->|No| K["Discard and Rebuild"]
    J --> L["Production Deployment"]
    K --> A
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The flow loops until **Issue Density ≤ 0.5** *and* **Confidence ≥ 0.8** are both achieved.
- The **Survival Criterion** (Code Reuse Ratio > 0.6 and Architecture Stable) decides the final disposition.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Prototype Fidelity Pyramid (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph P1["HIGH FIDELITY LAYER - Pixel Perfect Functional"]
        H1["Interactive Figma Prototype"]
        H2["HTML CSS Functional Prototype"]
        H3["Wizard of Oz High Fi Setup"]
    end
    subgraph P2["MID FIDELITY LAYER - Layout and Flow"]
        M1["Wireframes in Figma or Balsamiq"]
        M2["Click Through Mockups"]
        M3["Greyscale Screen Designs"]
    end
    subgraph P3["LOW FIDELITY LAYER - Concept and Structure"]
        L1["Paper Sketches"]
        L2["Sticky Note Prototypes"]
        L3["Storyboards"]
    end
    P3 --> P2
    P2 --> P1
    L1 -.->|Iterate upward| M1
    L2 -.->|Iterate upward| M2
    L3 -.->|Iterate upward| M3
    M1 -.->|Iterate upward| H1
    M2 -.->|Iterate upward| H2
    M3 -.->|Iterate upward| H3
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The pyramid visualizes the **natural progression** of fidelity as the design matures.
- Each upward arrow represents an **iteration jump** in realism.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Throwaway vs Evolutionary Prototype Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Prototype Built"] --> B{"Question Code Reuse Ratio greater than 0 point 6"}
    B -->|No| C["THROWAWAY PROTOTYPE Discard and Learn"]
    B -->|Yes| D{"Architecture Stable and Clean"}
    D -->|No| C
    D -->|Yes| E["EVOLUTIONARY PROTOTYPE Grow into Product"]
    E --> F["Continuous Refactoring"]
    C --> G["Insights Fed to Next Prototype Cycle"]
    G --> A
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The left branch captures *learning* prototypes (throwaway), which feed **insights** back into the cycle.
- The right branch captures *production-bound* prototypes (evolutionary), which undergo **refactoring** rather than discarding.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OECPT614, Module 2]**
**Define prototyping in the context of Human-Machine Interaction. List any two advantages of using prototypes in the HMI design process.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):**
> Prototyping in HMI is the iterative construction of incomplete, testable representations of an interactive system to validate design ideas, gather user feedback, and refine the interface before full-scale development.

> **Two Advantages (1.5 Marks — 0.75 each):**
> 1. **Early defect detection:** Usability problems identified on prototypes are far cheaper to fix than after deployment.
> 2. **Improved stakeholder communication:** A working prototype conveys design intent more clearly than written specifications, enabling better alignment between designers, developers, and users.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, OECPT614, Module 2]**
**Differentiate between a low-fidelity prototype and a high-fidelity prototype. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Low-Fidelity Prototype (1.5 Marks):**
> A rough, low-resolution representation with minimal interactivity, used in early design stages. It is quick to build, cheap, and focuses on concept rather than visual polish.
> *Example:* A hand-drawn paper sketch of a mobile app screen, where the user taps the sketched "Search" box and the designer swaps in another sheet showing results.

> **High-Fidelity Prototype (1.5 Marks):**
> A polished, fully interactive representation closely resembling the final product, used in later stages for detailed usability testing and stakeholder demos.
> *Example:* A Figma click-through prototype with realistic visuals, micro-animations, and complete navigation flow for a banking app.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Sub-parts (a) and (b)**

> **Q.A (a) Explain the classification of prototypes based on fidelity. Discuss the situations in which each type of fidelity is most appropriate. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
Prototype fidelity refers to the level of detail, realism, and interactivity a prototype offers relative to the final product. The classification helps designers choose the right tool for the right stage.

**2. Low-Fidelity Prototypes (2 Marks):**
- *Characteristics:* Paper sketches, sticky notes, hand-drawn storyboards. No real interactivity; a human "computer" simulates responses.
- *When Appropriate:*
  - Early brainstorming when concepts are still fluid.
  - When the design question is **"What should we build?"** rather than "How well does it work?"
  - When stakeholders/users are intimidated by polished designs and may withhold honest feedback.
  - Time-boxed design sprints (under 2 hours).

**3. Mid-Fidelity Prototypes (2 Marks):**
- *Characteristics:* Wireframes in tools like Figma, Balsamiq, or Moqups. Greyscale, clickable, but without real backend logic.
- *When Appropriate:*
  - Validating information architecture, layout, and navigation flow.
  - Conducting heuristic evaluations and 5-second tests.
  - When you need user feedback on structure but not visual design.

**4. High-Fidelity Prototypes (2 Marks):**
- *Characteristics:* Pixel-perfect, branded, fully interactive, sometimes connected to live databases or APIs.
- *When Appropriate:*
  - Final pre-launch usability tests (summative evaluation).
  - Regulatory validation (e.g., FDA Human Factors testing for medical devices).
  - Investor demos, marketing previews, and A/B testing.

---

> **Q.A (b) With a neat diagram, describe the iterative prototyping lifecycle. Explain the role of user evaluation at each stage. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
   ┌──────────────────────────────┐
   │                              │
   ↓                              │
[1. Identify Design] → [2. Build Prototype] → [3. User Evaluation] → [4. Analyze & Refine]
                                                                      │
                                                                      └──────→ (back to Step 1)
```

(Or use the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 as a sketch.)

**2. Stage-wise Explanation (4 Marks — 1 each):**

- **Stage 1 — Identify Design Question:** The designer formulates a falsifiable hypothesis (e.g., *"Users will complete checkout in under 60 seconds"*). The narrower the question, the more useful the prototype.

- **Stage 2 — Build Prototype:** A concrete artifact is created at the appropriate fidelity. Only the elements needed to test the hypothesis are built, following the principle *"Build only what you need to test."*

- **Stage 3 — User Evaluation:** Real users interact with the prototype under representative conditions. Methods include think-aloud protocols, task completion measurement, heuristic evaluation, and A/B testing. This stage generates the raw usability data.

- **Stage 4 — Analyze & Refine:** The designer computes issue density, time-on-task, error rates, and satisfaction scores. If the issue density is high or the hypothesis is falsified, the cycle restarts at Stage 1 with a refined design.

**Role of User Evaluation:** It is the **empirical anchor** of the entire cycle. Without user evaluation, prototyping degenerates into "designer opinion" rather than evidence-based design. Evaluation transforms subjective design preferences into measurable, improvable metrics.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q.B (a) Compare throwaway prototyping and evolutionary prototyping. Under what circumstances would you recommend each approach? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition Comparison (2 Marks):**

| Aspect | Throwaway Prototype | Evolutionary Prototype |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Test a specific question, then discarded | Grows progressively into the final product |
| **Code Reuse** | Low (deliberately) | High (intentionally) |
| **Architecture** | Loose, focused on exploration | Clean, scalable |
| **Effort Investment** | Low per prototype | High, cumulative |

**2. Throwaway Prototyping — When to Use (2.5 Marks):**
- When the design space is unexplored and many alternatives must be tested.
- When the goal is to **learn** rather than to **build**.
- When the final system's architecture is not yet known.
- Examples: paper prototypes, Figma concept mockups.

**3. Evolutionary Prototyping — When to Use (2.5 Marks):**
- When the requirements are well-understood and stable.
- When development follows Agile/XP methodologies where each sprint refines the prototype.
- When the codebase must be production-grade from the start.
- Examples: SaaS dashboards, mobile app MVPs (Minimum Viable Products).

---

> **Q.B (b) Explain the Wizard of Oz prototyping technique. Describe a scenario where it would be the most appropriate technique to use, and outline the steps to conduct such a study. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (1.5 Marks):**
The **Wizard of Oz (WOz) technique** is a prototyping method in which a hidden human operator (the "wizard") simulates the behavior of an intelligent or complex system, while the user believes they are interacting with a fully autonomous machine. The name derives from the classic film *The Wizard of Oz*, where the great wizard is revealed to be a man behind a curtain.

**2. Appropriate Scenario (1.5 Marks):**
*Scenario:* Designing a voice-based health assistant for elderly patients who will ask questions like *"What is my blood pressure reading today?"* or *"Remind me to take my medicine at 9 PM."* Since building a real Natural Language Understanding (NLU) system is expensive and time-consuming, a WOz prototype allows the design team to test user expectations, vocabulary, error tolerance, and conversational flow long before any AI is built.

**3. Steps to Conduct the Study (4 Marks — 1 each):**

- **Step 1 — Define the Wizard's Role:** Specify exactly what the hidden operator will simulate (e.g., response generation, error handling, latency). Decide on the input/output modalities (speech, text, gesture).

- **Step 2 — Set Up the Physical Environment:** Place the user in a realistic setting. Hide the wizard in a separate room with a one-way mirror, video feed, or remote interface. Use a believable microphone/speaker setup.

- **Step 3 — Recruit Representative Users:** Choose 5–8 users matching the target population (in our scenario, elderly patients). Obtain informed consent explaining that *some* system behavior may be simulated.

- **Step 4 — Conduct Realistic Tasks:** Give the user realistic tasks (e.g., *"Find out when your next appointment is"*). Observe via think-aloud, video, and screen logging.

- **Step 5 — Wizard Follows a Script (but improvises):** The wizard responds plausibly, occasionally introducing realistic delays and errors to test user tolerance.

- **Step 6 — Debrief and Analyze:** After each session, interview the user about their experience. Aggregate findings to refine the design.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Prototyping in Practice" Questions**
>
> 1. **Confusing prototype types:** Students often mix up "low-fidelity" with "throwaway" and "high-fidelity" with "evolutionary." These are **orthogonal classifications** — a prototype can be *low-fidelity AND evolutionary* (e.g., a paper prototype that grows into wireframes).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the iterative nature:** Answers that describe prototyping as a *single step* (e.g., "build a mockup and test it") lose at least 2 marks. The KTU examiner expects explicit mention of the **Design → Prototype → Evaluate → Refine** loop.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the "Why":** When listing prototype types, students often only describe *what* they are without explaining *when to use them*. Always pair the definition with a **use-case scenario**.
>
> 4. **Missing the user:** Any prototyping question that does not mention **user evaluation** is incomplete. Always state *who* evaluates and *how*.
>
> 5. **Confusing Storyboarding with Storytella:** Storyboarding is a **sequence of panels showing user interaction**; it is not the same as a user story or persona.
>
> 6. **Skipping fidelity justification:** When asked to recommend a prototype, students say *"use Figma"* without justifying *why* that fidelity level fits the design question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Prototyping in Practice**

- ✅ **Definition:** Prototyping = building incomplete, testable artifacts to validate design ideas iteratively.
- ✅ **Five main techniques:** Paper, Wireframe, Storyboard, Wizard of Oz, Interactive Click-Through.
- ✅ **Fidelity levels:** Low (paper/sketch), Mid (wireframe), High (interactive/functional).
- ✅ **Prototype classifications:** Throwaway vs Evolutionary; Horizontal vs Vertical; Low vs Mid vs High fidelity.
- ✅ **Iterative cycle:** Design → Prototype → Evaluate → Refine → (loop).
- ✅ **Hypothesis-driven:** Every prototype tests a specific falsifiable claim.
- ✅ **Build only what you need to test:** Avoid scope creep and aesthetic bias.
- ✅ **Nielsen's 5-user rule:** ~5 users reveal ~85% of usability issues; if issue density stays high, iterate.
- ✅ **Wizard of Oz:** Hidden human simulates intelligent system behavior — critical for AI/voice UI testing.
- ✅ **Throwaway vs Evolutionary Decision:** Based on Code Reuse Ratio (>0.6) and architecture stability.
- ✅ **Storyboards** show user journeys over time; **Personas** show who the user is; **Scenarios** show what the user does.
- ✅ **Confidence formula:** $C = 1 - \prod(1 - c_i)$ — combine multiple evaluation methods for higher confidence.
- ✅ **KTU favorite question types:** "Classify prototypes," "Compare throwaway vs evolutionary," "Explain WOz with example," "Describe iterative lifecycle with diagram."

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PILOT"**
> **P** — Paper prototyping (lowest fidelity)
> **I** — Iterative cycle (Design–Prototype–Evaluate–Refine)
> **L** — Levels of fidelity (Low → Mid → High)
> **O** — Oz (Wizard of Oz for AI/voice)
> **T** — Throwaway vs Evolutionary (lifecycle decision)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Rationale
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT614 - INTRODUCTION TO HUMAN MACHINE INTERACTION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Rationale

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design Rationale (DR)** is the explicit documentation and representation of the *reasoning, deliberation, trade-offs, arguments, and justifications* that underlie the design decisions made during the development of an interactive system. In the context of Human–Computer Interaction (HCI), design rationale captures **why** a particular design alternative was selected from a set of possible alternatives, the criteria used for evaluation, and the constraints (functional, ergonomic, cognitive, cultural) that shaped the decision.

According to the KTU 2024 HCI syllabus framework, design rationale is positioned as a *post-design* and *intra-design* support mechanism that allows:

- Designers to *reconstruct* the logic behind a finished artefact.
- New team members to *comprehend* the historical context of decisions.
- Stakeholders to *evaluate* the suitability of the design against user requirements.
- Future redesigns to *leverage* prior knowledge instead of re-discovering solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "Design Rationale refers to the recorded justification of design decisions — including the alternatives considered, the criteria applied, the trade-offs evaluated, and the arguments supporting the chosen solution — which together form an auditable trail of the reasoning process."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Architect's Blueprint Diary"
