# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define HMI tools. List the four main categories of tools used in Human–Machine Interaction.**

**Model Answer:**

HMI tools are software applications or analytical methods that assist designers in specifying, designing, implementing, and evaluating user interfaces.

The four main categories are:
1. **Specification Tools** — capture user requirements (e.g., use cases, personas).
2. **Design/Implementation Tools** — build the interface (e.g., Figma, Java Swing).
3. **Evaluation/Usability Tools** — test usability (e.g., heuristic evaluation, GOMS).
4. **Communication Tools** — coordinate design teams (e.g., Git, Confluence).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the GOMS model? Expand the acronym and explain each component in one line.**

**Model Answer:**

GOMS is a predictive cognitive modeling tool used in HMI to estimate user performance on routine tasks.

- **G — Goals:** The user’s desired end-state (e.g., save a file).
- **O — Operators:** Elementary actions (keystrokes, mouse clicks, mental acts).
- **M — Methods:** A sequence of operators that achieve a goal.
- **S — Selection Rules:** Decision logic for choosing between multiple methods.

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the four major categories of HMI tools with suitable examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the GOMS model in detail. With a suitable example, show how the Keystroke-Level Model (KLM) is used to predict the execution time of a task. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):** HMI tools are software/methodological aids that support the design, implementation, and evaluation of user interfaces. They are grouped into four families.

**Category 1 — Specification Tools (1.5 marks):**
These capture *what* the system should do *before* coding begins.
- Examples: Use-case diagrams, user stories, personas, task analysis worksheets.

**Category 2 — Design / Implementation Tools (1.5 marks):**
These help *build* the interface.
- Examples: Figma (screen design), Java Swing (programming), Balsamiq (rapid prototyping), HTML5 + CSS (web).

**Category 3 — Evaluation / Usability Tools (2 marks):**
These *measure* the interface against usability criteria.
- Examples: Heuristic evaluation (Nielsen), Cognitive walkthrough, GOMS/KLM, Think-aloud, SUS questionnaire.

**Category 4 — Communication / Project Tools (1 mark):**
These coordinate team efforts and track design rationale.
- Examples: Git (version control), Slack, Confluence, Microsoft Teams.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Heuristic Evaluation** (qualitative inspection) with **SUS** (quantitative survey). Heuristic evaluation does *not* require end users; SUS does. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this mix-up.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**GOMS Explanation (3 marks):**
GOMS, proposed by **Card, Moran & Newell (1983)**, is a structured cognitive modeling technique that breaks tasks into Goals, Operators, Methods, and Selection rules. It predicts the *time* and *learning behavior* of expert users without requiring live testing.

**KLM (Keystroke-Level Model) (2 marks):**
A simplified GOMS variant. Execution time is the sum of:

$$
T_{execute} = T_{K} + T_{P} + T_{H} + T_{M} + T_{R}
$$

where each $T$ corresponds to a fixed operator time.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**
*Task: Type "Hi" in a textbox and press Enter.*

- Click textbox: $T_{P} = 1.10$ s
- Mental prep: $T_{M} = 1.35$ s
- Type "H": $T_{K} = 0.28$ s
- Type "i": $T_{K} = 0.28$ s
- Press Enter: $T_{K} = 0.28$ s

$$
T_{execute} = 1.10 + 1.35 + 0.28 + 0.28 + 0.28 = 3.29 \text{ seconds}
$$

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Differentiate between **Heuristic Evaluation** and **Cognitive Walkthrough** as HMI evaluation tools. Mention the advantages and limitations of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **System Usability Scale (SUS)** tool. How is the SUS score calculated? Why is it considered an industry-standard usability tool? **(7 marks)**

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Heuristic Evaluation:** A usability inspection method where 3–5 experts evaluate the interface against Nielsen’s 10 usability heuristics.
- **Cognitive Walkthrough:** An inspection method where evaluators simulate a user’s cognitive process step-by-step to accomplish a task, focusing on *learning* by exploration.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Heuristic Evaluation | Cognitive Walkthrough |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | General usability principles | Task-completion by novice users |
| **Heuristics Used** | Nielsen’s 10 | Four key questions per step |
| **Time Required** | 1–2 hours per evaluator | 2–3 hours per task |
| **Best For** | Identifying broad UI flaws | Identifying learnability issues |
| **Output** | Severity-rated violations | Step-by-step success/failure notes |

**Advantages & Limitations (2 marks):**
- *Heuristic:* Fast, cheap, broad coverage. Limitation: evaluator bias.
- *Cognitive Walkthrough:* Excellent for learnability. Limitation: tedious for complex flows.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner’s Pitfall Alert:** A common student error is treating **Heuristic Evaluation** as a *user test*. It is an **expert-based inspection method** — no end users are required. Confusing this costs **2–3 marks**.

---

#### 🔹 Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition of SUS (2 marks):**
The **System Usability Scale (SUS)** is a 10-item questionnaire developed by **John Brooke (1986)**. Each item is rated on a 5-point Likert scale (1 = Strongly Disagree, 5 = Strongly Agree). It provides a quick, reliable measure of *perceived* usability.

**Score Calculation (3 marks):**
For odd-numbered items (1, 3, 5, 7, 9), subtract 1 from the score.
For even-numbered items (2, 4, 6, 8, 10), subtract the score from 5.
Then sum all transformed values and multiply by **2.5**.

$$
SUS_{score} = \left(\sum_{i \text{ odd}} (S_i - 1) + \sum_{i \text{ even}} (5 - S_i)\right) \times 2.5
$$

**Result Range:** 0 to 100.
- **> 80.3** → Excellent
- **68 – 80.2** → Good
- **51 – 67** → OK
- **< 51** → Poor

**Why Industry Standard (2 marks):**
- Validated across **thousands of studies**.
- Quick to administer (under 5 minutes).
- Free to use, language-independent.
- Comparable across products, teams, and industries.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Tools in HMI**

- ✅ HMI Tools = **Specification + Design + Evaluation + Communication**.
- ✅ GOMS stands for **Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules**.
- ✅ KLM is a *simplified* GOMS variant; formula: $T_{execute} = T_{K} + T_{P} + T_{H} + T_{M} + T_{R}$.
- ✅ Heuristic Evaluation = **Expert inspection**, no users required.
- ✅ Cognitive Walkthrough = **Simulated user walkthrough** for learnability.
- ✅ SUS = 10-item questionnaire, score is **0–100**, multiplied by **2.5**.
- ✅ Fitts’s Law predicts **movement time** to a target: $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(\tfrac{D}{W} + 1)$.
- ✅ Nielsen proposed **10 usability heuristics** — remember the keywords: *visibility, match, control, consistency, error prevention, recognition, flexibility, minimalist, recovery, help*.
- ✅ Always remember: SUS requires **real users**; Heuristic Evaluation does **not**.
- ✅ Tools are **software/methods**, not hardware — avoid the mix-up in definitions.
